actions.thought: Pocket 5s UTG in a 4-handed game with deep stacks (100BB). This is a strong hand that plays well multi-way for set value but also wants initiative. Open-raising is standard to build the pot and narrow the field. 3x raise to 300 is a good size given the pot of 150.

With J9o on the button, I have a marginally playable hand in position. Elon's 3x raise from UTG is standard and doesn't suggest extreme tightness. The pot odds are favorable (calling 300 to win 450+), and crucially, my button position gives me a significant informational and betting advantage post-flop. I can control the pot with position, see how the SB and BB respond, and make better decisions with more information. A raise would be overplaying J9o and inviting a 3-bet while out of position. A call allows me to leverage positional equity while keeping the pot manageable.

7♥4♠ is a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand. Elon opened from early and Claude flat-called, so this will be a multiway pot and I’ll be out of position postflop. Calling 250 to see a flop OOP is a marginal play with poor equity and bad implied odds, and a squeeze bluff here with such raggy cards is not justified without specific reads. With ~100bb effective and 149 hands left, preserving my stack and waiting for a better spot is the disciplined +EV play.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ten-Three offsuit. Elon raised to 3BB from the UTG position, and Claude called from the Button. While the pot odds are tempting (200 to call into a pot of 750), T3o is an extremely weak hand that performs poorly out of position, especially against two opponents. Given it is the first hand of the session and I have no reads on the players, I will choose the disciplined path and fold this garbage hand to preserve my stack for more favorable spots.

Flopped top set with pocket 5s on Jc9s5d board. Extremely strong hand in 4-handed deepstack play. Claude's button call to UTG raise includes pairs (including J9), overcards, suited connectors, and draws. Board has straight draw potential (T8) and spade flush draw. Betting for thin value, pot control, protection against draws, and to charge floats. 2/3 pot bet of 500 builds the pot without overcommitting OOP while denying equity to draws.

I flopped top two pair (JJ99) on a relatively dry 5d-Jc-9s board. This is a strong holding with limited draws available to Elon's likely range (AA, KK, QQ, AK, AQ, or continuation bets). Elon's 500 flop bet could be either value or a c-bet, and I need to extract value while defining his hand. Simply calling allows him to take free turns with potential overcards or draws. A raise to 1200 (2.4x his bet) is appropriately aggressive for my hand strength—it's not overplaying J9 but rather properly valuing top two pair on a relatively safe board. This action builds the pot while I have the best hand in many scenarios, charges any marginal hands, and puts pressure on Elon to make a decision with more information. Folding is clearly wrong, and calling is passive when I have both hand strength and position to leverage.

Flopped top set of 5s on Jc9s5d board, an extremely strong hand heads-up OOP in a deepstack spot. Board has spade flush draw and straight draws (T8, 87), so protection is key. Claude's button call pre-flop to UTG raise and flop raise on this board polarizes his range to strong value (JJ, J9s, 99, maybe 99 set) or semi-bluffs/draws (spade draws, T8s). Raising extracts value from worse hands like Jx, two-pair, weaker made hands, charges draws heavily, builds the pot with a monster, and denies equity to floats. Calling risks giving free cards to draws or allowing bluffs to realize equity cheaply; folding is never an option. Raising to 3500 (about 3x his 1200 bet size, pot-committing-ish without shoving deep stacks) balances value, protection, and fold equity against his bluffs/draws. Post-flop SPR remains manageable.

I have top two pair (JJ99) on a relatively dry 5d-Jc-9s board. Elon's aggressive flop re-raise from 1200 to 3500 likely indicates either a premium overpair (AA, KK, QQ) or a strong second-best hand (AK, AQ, A9, AJ). 

Pot odds analysis: I'm calling 2300 to contest a pot of 7750 after my call, requiring approximately 23% equity to break even. With top two pair, I have substantial equity (~55-60%) across Elon's likely UTG opening range, making this mathematically profitable.

Why call rather than raise to 5800 (minimum raise)? 
- My two pair, while strong, is vulnerable to overcards and premium pairs
- Raising commits too much of my stack (leaving only 2700) relative to the information I have
- The turn card will be crucial—it either gives me safety/improvement or warns me against a premium pair
- By calling, I keep the pot manageable while maintaining flexibility for the turn decision
- Elon's remaining stack (6200) suggests he'll likely continue, allowing me to extract additional value when the turn is safe

This call balances hand strength with positional advantage while controlling my exposure to premium holdings.

Top set of 5s remains an extremely strong hand on this turn card (Ac), completing a possible club flush draw (Jc-Ac) but unlikely to have disproportionately strengthened villain's calling range vs. my large flop 3-bet (which was ~3x his raise into a modest pot, pot-committing range). His button call pre and flop raise polarize to strong value (JJ set, J9 two-pair, 99 set, AJ, strong Jx) or stubborn draws/semi-bluffs (spade FD that missed, T8 straight draw now with A-top possible). Many of these are still behind (weaker sets/trips/two-pair), and Ac is often a brick or improver for medium strength. With SPR ~1.25 (pot 7750, stacks 6200), OOP first to act, shoving all-in maximizes value from worse made hands that call flop action (Jx, two-pair, weaker sets if any), protects vs. remaining draws, builds massive pot with monster, and applies fold equity to missed draws/A-high that might check-fold. Checking risks free card to draws, check-raise unnecessary with low SPR (he'd shove anyway), or check-call/jam facing bet similarly low EV. All-in is optimal balance of value, protection, and denial in deep-but-low-SPR spot.

I need to make a critical all-in decision on the turn with J9o that flopped top two pair (JJ99).

Current situation:
- Board: 5d-Jc-9s-Ac (turn)
- My hand: Top two pair (JJ99)
- Elon's stack: 0 (all-in)
- My stack: 6200 (exactly what I need to call)
- Pot: 13,950
- To call: 6200

The Ace on the turn is a scary card that could have improved Elon's range (AA, AK, AQ are all plausible). However, my equity calculation suggests calling is mathematically sound:

Equity calculation vs likely UTG range:
- vs AA (20%): ~4% equity
- vs KK (15%): ~32% equity  
- vs QQ (15%): ~32% equity
- vs AK (25%): ~46% equity
- vs AQ (20%): ~52% equity
- vs Other (5%): ~60% equity

Weighted equity: ~35%

Pot odds required: 6200/(13950+6200) ≈ 31%

Since my estimated equity (35%) exceeds the breakeven point (31%), calling is mathematically profitable. While the Ace is a dangerous card and Elon's aggressive line is concerning, my top two pair has sufficient equity to warrant the all-in call. The pot odds justify the risk, and folding would surrender a hand with meaningful showdown value.

Button with A♥8♣ — a standard open-raise spot. Pot is 150, to call 100. I want to seize the initiative, apply pressure to the blinds, and isolate heads-up where this Ace-rag plays well in position. Raise to 300 (≈3x BB) gives good fold equity while keeping the pot manageable with a marginal offsuit Ace. If I face a small 3-bet I will often call and play postflop in position; against a large 3-bet or shove I will usually fold. If called by one or both blinds I will C-bet favorable flops and re-evaluate on the turn.

I am holding 7-2 offsuit, which is widely considered the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Minni has raised from the button to 300. Although I am in the small blind and have already contributed 50 chips, calling another 250 with such a poor hand out of position is a losing play in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

78o is a speculative offsuit connector with good playability potential for straights, sets, and two-pair hands. Facing a button min-raise after SB fold, villain's range is wide (suited connectors, broadway, Ax, etc.). Pot odds are favorable: calling 200 into 450 gives ~2.25:1 (31% equity needed), and 78o has sufficient equity and implied odds deepstacked (my 200bb effective vs villain's 96bb). Defending wide OOP vs button is standard. Folding is too tight; 3-betting risks OOP with marginal hand. Plan to call and play postflop cautiously, looking to float or realize equity.

78o completely misses the Ts4cJh rainbow flop: no pair, no straight or flush draws (gutshot or backdoor at best). OOP vs BTN min-raiser with wide range, but my hand has zero showdown value and poor playability. Pot 650, effective stacks deep (mine 198bb, villain 96bb). Standard check to pot control and realize fold equity if villain c-bets (likely). Betting OOP as bluff is low EV with this holding; better to check-fold to a bet or check behind if checked to. No reason to bloat pot with air.

I opened preflop and got a single caller; on T♠4♣J♥ I hold A♥8♣ — Ace-high with a single backdoor club and a useful blocker to many Ax combos. Elon checked, so in-position a modest continuation bet is the best way to seize the initiative, deny free cards to pure draws, and fold out many unimproved hands. The flop is relatively dry and my Ace reduces some of opponent’s strongest Ace-x holdings, so I want a compact sizing that preserves stack versus a raise but still has fold equity. Betting 250 into the 650 pot (~38%) pressures Jx/Tx and small pairs while keeping pot control. Plan: if he folds we take it down; if he calls we re-evaluate on the turn — bet for value if an Ace or other improving card appears, proceed cautiously on blanks, and fold to a sizable check‑raise since Ace‑high will rarely be good.

78o completely whiffs the Ts4cJh flop: no pair, no straight draw (not even gutshot), no flush draw beyond weak backdoors, zero showdown value. OOP in BB after wide preflop defend vs BTN open. Villain's min-c-bet (250 into 900, ~28% pot) into checked pot reps a wide polarized range (value like Jx/Tx/sets, bluffs like air/ weak draws). Pot odds ~3.6:1 (call 250 to win 1150 post-call, needing ~18% equity), but 78o realizes <20% vs typical BTN c-bet range (blocks little, poor playability). Calling bloats pot OOP with reverse implied odds; floating risks getting check-raised or facing turn barrels. Check-fold exploits position and preserves stack for better spots in deepstack game. No value bet/raise possible.

I am holding Ace-Ten suited (Ah Th) on the Button, which is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. I want to open with a standard raise to take control of the hand and put pressure on the blinds. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard opening size.

AQo is a premium hand in 3-handed play facing a button open-raise, which is likely wide. Deep stacks (198BB effective). 3-betting from SB isolates the button and applies pressure OOP. Sizing to 3x the open (900 total) is standard and balanced. Calling is playable but 3-betting builds pot with equity advantage and fold equity.

I'm in the big blind with A♦8♥ facing a button open to 300 and a small-blind 3-bet to 900. A8o is a defendable hand here: it's an Ace with decent showdown value and the pot odds to call are reasonable (calling 800 into a 1300 pot). 4-betting to 1500 is unnecessary and risky with a marginal ace that will often be dominated by the 3-bettor's value range, and it would bloat the pot out of position or invite action from the button. I'll call to see a flop; plan is to continue with top-pair or strong draws, exercise pot control and fold to heavy aggression when I miss, and re-evaluate if the button also calls or if a 4-bet comes in.

I have Ace-Ten of hearts on the button. I originally opened for 300, Elon 3-bet to 900 from the small blind, and Minni cold-called from the big blind. I am getting excellent pot odds (600 to call into a pot of 2100) and I will have the advantage of being in position for the rest of the hand. 4-betting is an option, but calling allows me to play a strong suited-ace multi-way in position, which is highly profitable. I will call to see a flop.

Multiway 3-bet pot (two opponents) out of position on a flop of 8s9c4h, which heavily favors their Button open-call and BB defense calling ranges (suited connectors, small pairs, broadway suited). AQo misses entirely: Ace-high with no pair and only a weak backdoor spade draw. Showdown value is poor multiway. Betting pure air OOP multiway is highly -EV due to reverse implied odds and difficult post-flop decisions. Checking allows pot control, free equity realization if checked through, and options to fold to bets or selectively check-raise bluff if dynamics warrant. Preserves stack for better spots in deep 198BB+ game.

I have Ad8h and flopped a single pair (an 8) on 8s-9c-4h. Elon was the preflop 3-bettor, so his range is weighted toward strong hands that can easily dominate or outdraw me; Flash called the 3-bet and has a wider, often speculative calling range. This is a relatively dry board but I'm multiway and my pair is vulnerable to 9x, sets, and big overcards that may continue. I want pot control and to avoid getting check-raised or bloating the pot out of position with only a medium-strength holding. I'll check — plan is to call a small c-bet (~≤1/3 pot) to realize equity and fold to heavy action / sizable check-raises, and re-evaluate on the turn if checked through.

I am on the button with Ace-Ten of hearts. The board is 8s-9c-4h, which doesn't directly improve my hand, though I have two overcards and some backdoor potential. Elon, the pre-flop 3-bettor, has checked, and Minni has checked as well. In a three-way 3-bet pot, this board texture is quite dynamic and likely connects with the calling ranges of the blinds (pairs like 77-TT, or connectors). By checking back, I realize my equity for free and can potentially see a turn card that improves my hand or allows for a better bluffing opportunity if the action remains weak. There is no need to bloat the pot with Ace-high at this stage.

Multiway 3-bet pot OOP on turn with AQo (AsQc), complete air: no pair, no draw, poor showdown value vs. opponents' ranges (Flash's BTN open-call wide including suited connectors/pairs drawing to straights/flushes; Minni's BB defense similar). Board 8s9c4hKh coordinates well for their ranges (possible straights like T7/QJT, flush draws, pairs). Kh changes little for me but adds heart flush draw danger. Continued checking provides pot control, free card realization (unlikely but possible check-through), and easy fold to bets without committing further. Bluffing OOP multiway into two checked-through opponents is -EV: low fold equity, vulnerable to calls/raises from draws/medium strength. Preserve stack for superior spots in deep-stack 3-handed play.

Check. I have A♦8♥ — top pair with the best kicker on the flop/turn, but the King on the turn makes my hand vulnerable to Kx and I can still be behind sets (99/44) or higher pairs. Both opponents have acted passively (checked every street), and this is a multiway spot out of position, so pot control and card removal are priorities. There is no bet to call and the pot is only 2,700, so I prefer to see the river cheaply to realize equity. Plan: call a small-to-medium bet (~≤1/2 pot) to see the river, but fold to large aggression (>~2/3 pot or shove) since my hand has limited protection and I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position. If checked through, I’ll re-evaluate the river for thin value (A/8 or blanks) or to fold to heavy action.

I have the Nut Flush Draw (Ah Th) on a board of 8s-9c-4h-Kh. Elon, the pre-flop 3-bettor, has checked twice, and Minni has also checked twice. While betting could potentially take the pot down now, checking back is a solid play in a multi-way 3-bet pot. It allows me to realize my equity for free and guarantees I see the river card. If I hit a heart on the river, I will have the nuts and can value bet effectively. If I miss, I can still decide whether to bluff or check down depending on the final board and my opponents' actions.

River Js completes additional straight draws (e.g., T7 for 7-8-9-T-J, Q-10 for 8-9-T-J-Q possibilities in opponents' ranges) and adds a third spade for spade flush draws, further capping my perceived range as pre-flop 3-bettor but improving villains' calling ranges (BTN open-call and BB defense include suited connectors, broadway, pairs that connect). AQo remains pure ace-high with no pair/draw, mediocre showdown value multiway on this coordinated board after double check-through (suggests capped ranges but villains likely hold 8x/9x/Jx/Kx/pairs/sd value that beats A-high). OOP first to act in 2700 pot vs. two deep opponents (95BB+ eff.), bluffing has low fold equity (need both to fold better hands/draws post-checks) and exposes to calls/raises. Value betting impossible. Checking maximizes EV: pot control, induces bluffs/checks-behind from worse (unlikely but possible Kx/medium pairs wary of my range), free realization of thin A-high equity, easy fold to any bet. Preserves deep stack for +EV spots in 3-handed play.

I have Ad8h — top pair (eights) with an ace kicker, but the river added two overcards (K and J) that beat me and there are plausible hands (Kx, Jx, 99, 44, sets) that are ahead. This was a passive, three-way hand postflop, so a small river bet is unlikely to extract reliable value and a bet risks folding out the few worse hands while getting called by better. Pot control and showdown value are correct here: check and go to showdown. If someone bets a small size I’ll consider a call, but I’ll fold to large river aggression given how vulnerable my hand is.

I have Ace-high (Ah Th) on a board of 8s-9c-4h-Kh-Js. The pre-flop 3-bettor (Elon) and the other caller (Minni) have checked through every street since the flop. While I missed my heart flush draw and haven't improved to a pair, Ace-high has some showdown value in a three-way pot that has been checked all the way down. Attempting a bluff into two opponents is risky, especially since they might be trapping with a weak pair or holding a better Ace-high like AQ. By checking back, I reach showdown for free and can potentially win the pot if both opponents were on draws or have weaker non-paired hands.

3-handed on the button with no preflop action shown, indicating an unopened pot. 6s9h is a playable hand from the button with good position, suitable for a standard open-raise to steal blinds or build the pot. Raising to 3x the big blind (300 total) is a balanced size given deep stacks and opponents' tendencies to raise themselves but defend variably.

Facing a 3bb open from the button in a 3-handed pot with A♣2♥ in the small blind: this hand is worth defending but not a good 3‑bet candidate out of position. A2o is often dominated by the button’s opening range and plays poorly OOP postflop, so I prefer to keep the pot small and realize equity rather than folding away or turning it into a small, marginal 3‑bet. I’ll call and see a flop, preserving chips and flexibility postflop.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Six offsuit. Elon raised from the Button, and Minni called from the Small Blind. Although I am getting good pot odds to call (200 to win a total pot of 900), T6o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position against two opponents. Given the hand's low equity and poor playability, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have A♣2♥ on Td Ah Jh — top pair with a very weak kicker. Elon opened from the button, so his range is wide and includes many worse A‑x hands, J/T combos and also some strong holdings (JT, AJ, AT, KQ straight). Out of position I want to protect my pair and extract value from worse aces and single‑pair hands while pricing out J/T equity. A half‑pot bet is the right balance: big enough to charge and fold out many turn‑outs, but not so large that I overcommit OOP. I’ll bet ~1/2 pot (350). If he raises big I’ll fold or re‑evaluate depending on sizing (his raise will mostly represent two‑pair/sets/straights); if he calls I’ll proceed to turn with pot control and re‑assess.

Heads-up on the flop with 6s9h on Td Ah Jh board. I have no pair, no flush draw (offsuit connectors missing the board entirely), no straight draw (9-high with no gutshot or open-ender). The board is Ace-high with two hearts, favoring Minni's calling range which likely includes Ax, flush draws (hearts), straight draws like QT/KQ, or sets. Preflop, I opened from button and she defended from SB, then donk-bet into me, which is a polarized range: value or draws/bluffs. My equity is very low (<20%) against her range, and while pot odds are ~3:1 effective (calling 350 into 1400), implied odds are poor postflop with my hand strength. Raising as a bluff has fold equity but risks her having Ax or draw and calling/stacking off. Best play is to fold and preserve stack for better spots. Position doesn't help enough here with zero showdown value.

Ten-deuce offsuit is a weak holding with little showdown value, but I'm on the button with deep stacks and good opportunity to steal the blinds. I want fold equity and to deny opponents good pot odds to see a cheap flop, so I will raise to 300 (a 3x BB open) rather than just complete. If I get a 3-bet from a strong range I will fold this hand; if called I will use position to c-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously on dry boards or against heavy resistance.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Deuce offsuit. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. While Ace-Deuce has some value in a 3-handed game, it is a very weak hand to play out of position. Calling 250 more to play a pot out of position against the Button and potentially the Big Blind (Elon) is generally a losing play with this hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

5c2d is one of the worst possible hands: offsuit, low cards, disconnected, no straight or flush potential worth defending. Facing a button raise to 300 (200 to call into 450 pot, ~3:1 odds), but out of position postflop with trash hand. No reason to defend; standard fold to preserve stack and avoid difficult spots OOP.

Holding Ace-Six offsuit on the button in a 3-handed game is a standard opening hand. I have the advantage of position for the rest of the hand. I will raise to 300 to attempt to take down the blinds or play a pot in position.

Preflop in SB facing a button raise to 300 (3x BB). Hand is 97d suited, a speculative suited connector/gapper with good playability for straights, flush draws, and two overs. Button ranges are wide, so favorable equity realization. Pot odds are excellent: 250 to call into 450 pot (pot becomes 700, ~2.8:1). SB position allows multiway pot potential with BB, and postflop playability is strong. Folding too tight; 3-betting not ideal without better strength or squeeze spot. Call to realize equity.

Call. Kc8c is worth defending from the big blind versus a 3bb button open, especially with the small blind already in — I get good pot odds (200 to win 900 ≈ 22%) and the hand has playability (backdoor clubs, king-high potential). Postflop I’ll act last in a 3-way pot, so I can control pot size and look for good flops to continue. A small 3-bet to isolate is possible but marginal here; folding is too weak. I call the 200 additional (300 total committed).

Middle pair with a weak kicker (9d7d on K-9-4 rainbow-ish board with two spades) in a multiway pot from SB, first to act. Board is king-high with a spade flush draw and straight draw possibilities (9-4). Button raiser (Flash) has wide range including kings, draws, overcards; BB defender (Minni) wide calling range. My hand has some showdown value but vulnerable to domination (K9, A9, better 9x) and raises. Pot is 900, deep stacks relative. Betting risks folding out equity or getting raised off hand; checking controls pot, induces bluffs/checks from weak hands, allows pot control. Plan to check-call medium bets or check-fold to aggression, realizing equity multiway cheaply. Folding preflop was too tight; now play passively postflop.

I have top pair (K) with a mediocre kicker (Kc8c) and backdoor club equity on a 4s Kd 9s board in a 3-way pot. Both opponents checked, so betting is for value and protection: I want to charge overcards and spade/straight draws and deny free cards. A small-to-medium bet will get called by worse kings, broadway combos and floating hands while keeping the pot manageable vs potential raises. I size to ~1/3 pot (300) to extract value from worse and price in draws; if I face a sizable raise I'll re-evaluate (likely fold to a polarizing large raise, call or 3-bet only vs clear bluffs). If called I plan to continue for value/protection on safe turns.

I raised pre-flop with Ace-Six offsuit and both blinds called. The flop is King-Nine-Four with two spades. I have the Ace of spades, giving me a backdoor nut flush draw. Elon (SB) checked, and Minni (BB) bet 300 into a 900 pot (a 1/3 pot bet). While I missed the flop, I have one overcard (the Ace) and the nut flush blocker. However, calling a bet into two opponents with only Ace-high on a King-high board is marginal. Given that I have position and the bet size is relatively small, I could consider a float, but against two opponents, one of whom has already shown aggression (Minni), it's safer to fold and wait for a better spot, as my hand has very little equity if called or raised further. Actually, given the pot odds (300 to win 1200) and the fact that I am in position, a call is theoretically possible if I believe I can outplay them on later streets or if an Ace/Spade improves my hand, but consistently calling with weak holdings leads to chip bleed. I will fold.

Heads-up now after Flash folds. I have middle pair (9s) with a weak 7 kicker on a K♦9♠4♠ board, plus backdoor diamond flush draw. Vulnerable to domination by K9, Kx, or better pairs, but BB's wide defending range includes overcards, weak kings, draws (spades like A♠x, JTs), and bluffs. Pot odds excellent: 300 into 1200 (~4:1, needing ~20% equity, which I have ~35-45% vs typical BB range). OOP, calling maintains pot control, realizes equity cheaply, induces bluffs on later streets, and keeps fold equity alive for turns. Raising risks bloating pot OOP with marginal hand or facing 3-bets from value; folding too tight given price and playability. Plan: call, evaluate turn (implied odds if improves, fold to heavy aggression if check-raised). Consistent with preflop speculative call and flop plan to check-call medium bets.

Heads-up on turn OOP with middle pair weak kicker (9d7d) on Kd9s4s2h board. Pot 1500, deep stacks (mine 18k, opp 12k). Board draw-heavy (spade FD, gutshots like 5-3, T5 etc.), turn 2h blank for most draws. Opp BB defended wide pre/multiway, donk-bet small flop (1/3 pot) after my check, repping wide range: Kd/9x/draws/Ax/high cards/bluffs. My hand has ~35-40% equity vs that (blocks 9s, backdoor diamond), showdown value, but vulnerable to domination (K9,Kx,A9). Betting thin value risks check-raise from better or folding out worse (draws/overcards); no strong enough for value, fold equity low OOP. Checking induces bluffs/checks from worse (missed draws, Ax), controls pot, realizes equity cheaply if check-behind or call small bet. Consistent with preflop speculative call and flop check-call plan. Plan: check, call small/medium bets (~500-1000) with equity/price/implied, fold to large overbets/shoves from value range.

I have top pair (K) with a mediocre 8-kicker in a heads-up pot of 1500. Elon checked the turn, so this is a value/protection spot: I want to charge spade draws and worse Kx/pocket pairs that called the flop while keeping the pot size under control. A half-pot bet (~750) extracts value from worse hands and denies free cards to draws; it’s small enough to call with many worse hands but large enough to fold some equity combinations. If Elon raises big I’ll re-evaluate (typically fold to a polarized large raise, call or proceed cautiously vs a small/medium raise), and if he just calls I’ll plan my river play based on the card and his action.

Facing a 750 bet (1/3 pot) into 2250 on turn OOP heads-up with middle pair weak kicker (9d7d) and backdoor diamond FD on Kd9s4s2h board. Pot odds excellent: 750 to call into 3000 (~4:1, need ~20% equity; I have ~35-45% vs BB's wide defending range including Kx/9x/draws/Ax/high card bluffs, especially after small flop donk and blank turn). Vulnerable to domination (K9/Kx/A9) but blocks 9d, has showdown value, and playability for river (catches 7/9/T/3/5/D for 2p/straight/better pair/FD). Minni's BB range wide from multiway defense; small sizing suggests wide value/thin value/draws/bluffs, not nuts. Raising bloats pot OOP with marginal hand, low fold equity vs calling station range; folding too tight given price/depth/implied odds (stacks 18k/11k). Calling consistent with preflop speculative play, flop check-call plan: realizes equity cheaply, induces bluffs, controls pot, keeps options open on river (can fold to agg if blank or bet small if improves). Plan: call, check river unless major improve; fold to river overbet/value.

River OOP heads-up with middle pair weak kicker (9d7d) on Kd9s4s2h6c board, pot 3000, eff stacks ~11k. Hand has ~30-40% equity vs villain's BB range (Kx/9x/Ax spades/missed draws/weak pairs/bluffs) from wide multiway defense + small donk flop/turn bets, with showdown value blocking 9s but vulnerable to domination (K9/Kx/A9), straights (3-5 now complete), sets. Betting thin value or bluff risks check-raise from better Kx/2p/straights or folding out worse Ax/draws (low fold equity vs defender), bloating pot OOP with marginal hand. Checking best: induces check-behind from worse high cards/weak 9x/Ax, pot control OOP, realizes equity cheaply if villain checks or bets small. Consistent with preflop speculative call, flop/turn check-call plan to control pot and realize equity multiway/deep. Plan: check, call small/medium bets (~1000-2000, good price/SDV), fold to large overbets/value raises.

I have Kc8c on a 4s Kd 9s 2h 6c board — top pair with a mediocre kicker. Heads-up after I was the aggressor on flop and turn and Elon called both streets; his river check looks like pot control or missed draws. There are a number of worse hands that will call a small river bet (worse Kx, single pocket pairs like 9x/4x/6x, some turned straights are rare) while the hands that beat me (sets, two pair, or K with a better kicker) will often either check-back or fold to a small sizing. I want to extract value without folding out the weaker calls, so I make a modest value bet ~1/3 pot. This charges worse Kx and medium pairs and keeps the pot manageable if I’m ahead. If Elon raises large, I’ll fold given my weak kicker and line; if he just calls we go to showdown and I often win. Total bet: 1,000 chips (≈1/3 pot).

River OOP heads-up vs BB after check. Pot 4000, facing 1000 bet (~1/4 pot sizing, into ~3000 prior). Hand: middle pair 9s weak kicker (97d) on K♠9♠4♠2♥6♣ board, showdown value, blocks 9d, ~30-40% equity vs villain's wide multiway BB defending range (Kx/9x/Ax/spades/missed draws/weak pairs/bluffs) polarized by small flop/turn donks and blankish 6c river completing low straight poss. (3x/5x). Pot odds ~4:1 (1000 to win 4000+), need 20% equity - easily met. Raising bloats pot OOP marginal/no fold equity vs defender; folding too tight given price/SDV/implied from prior play. Call realizes equity cheaply, consistent with preflop speculative call + check-call flop/turn plan for pot control/playability deep stacks (~17k/10k eff). Plan post-call: hand over unless chopped.

On the button first to act preflop in a 3-handed game with deep stacks (my 162 BB, opponents similar/84 BB). T7o is a playable hand from this spot: suited no but has straight potential (T987, 87654), dominated broadway blocker, and position allows realization. Standard open-raise range from BTN vs blinds is very wide (~50%+ hands); folding too tight early (hand 7/150). Raising 3x (300 total) standard size: builds pot, takes initiative, denies equity to blind defenses. Limp bad (invites multiway), fold too passive. Expect blinds defend wide but my position exploits postflop.

I’m on the small blind with Th8d facing a BTN open to 300 (pot 450, I must call 250; min‑raise to 500). T8o is a marginal, unsuited, medium connector with poor playability out of position. Calling 250 into 450 requires ~35.7% equity to break even right now; versus a BTN open range T8o will be behind much of the time and is often easily dominated. Elon has shown aggressive postflop tendencies, so I’d be forced into difficult decisions OOP on later streets or pay to see flops multiway if the BB defends. I’ll conserve chips for better spots and fold this marginal hand preflop.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 3h. Elon has raised to 300 from the Button. While the pot odds are 2.25:1 and the hand is suited, 93s is extremely weak and will be difficult to play out of position against Elon, who has shown significant aggression in previous hands. Defending such a wide range from the Big Blind often leads to difficult spots on later streets where I am forced to fold or call down with marginal holdings. I'll wait for a better spot to engage.

Button with A♣J♦ — AJo in a 3‑handed pot is a strong open. I want initiative and fold equity, and to isolate the blinds while keeping the pot a reasonable size against deep stacks. Standard sizing to 3xBB (300 total) is appropriate: it’s in line with table tendencies and makes marginal hands pay to continue. If I face a small 3‑bet I can call or flat depending on size; a large 3‑bet I’ll likely fold. Plan to leverage position postflop and c‑bet favorable textures.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack of Clubs and Five of Diamonds. Minni opened for a standard raise of 3x the big blind from the Button. J5o is a very weak hand, especially when out of position against a deep-stacked aggressive player. There is no reason to defend this hand in a 3-way game; it is a clear fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

K3o is a weak offsuit hand with a poor kicker. Facing a button raise from Minni, who has been active, but I'm OOP postflop. Pot odds are favorable (200 to call into 450, ~30% needed), but equity vs wide button range (~35%) doesn't justify OOP play with a hand that struggles postflop (few strong draws, dominated often). Standard BB defense folds this. Preserve stack for better spots.

On the button in a 3-handed game, my opening range should be quite wide. Queen-Five suited (Qs 5s) is a playable speculative hand with decent post-flop potential and positional advantage. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot immediately or play a controlled pot in position.

Pocket sevens is a strong hand preflop, roughly top 8-10% range. Facing a button raise from Flash, who opens wide with position on everyone. In small blind, out of position, but 77 plays well multiway or heads-up. Calling gives decent pot odds (250 to win 450+implied), but OOP set mining is tough. Prefer 3-betting for value to isolate or build pot against wide calling ranges. History shows Flash calls 3-bets sometimes but folds others. Sizing to 900 (3x the raise) is standard pot-building size with deep stacks. Avoids min-raise trap.

Flash opened to 300 and Elon 3-bet to 900; the pot is 1300 and I must call 800 (calling would make the pot ~2100, so I need ~38% equity). I hold 9♠6♥ — offsuit, poor connectivity and largely dominated by both a button open and a small‑blind 3‑bet range. Calling leaves me out of position in a three‑way pot with very little chance to realize equity; a squeeze/4‑bet would only be sensible with much stronger holdings or suited connectors. Preserve a healthy stack for better spots and fold.

I opened the action to 300 with Qs5s from the button. Elon, in the small blind, has 3-bet to 900. While I have the advantage of position, Queen-Five suited is a relatively weak hand to face a 3-bet with, especially given that Elon has shown aggression in previous hands. Calling 600 more puts me in a spot where I will likely have to hit the board hard or bluff to win, and Elon's stack size allows him to apply significant pressure. Since I'm 80 big blinds deep and facing a 3-bet from a player who seems active, I will fold this speculative hand and wait for a better opportunity.

73o is one of the worst possible hands, with poor connectivity, low cards, and offsuit. Even on the button facing a single big blind after small blind likely folded (pot 150, to call 100 aligns with SB folding their blind), the playability postflop is terrible OOP. Fold equity exists for steals, but this hand performs poorly if called or floated. Opponents like Flash defend BB sometimes (e.g., Hand 3 checked down but called pre), so better to preserve deep stack (166x BB) for premium spots in 3-handed play. Folding trash maximizes EV over 150 hands.

9♣3♦ is pure junk — very poor playability and easy to be dominated. Pot is 150 and it costs me 50 to call (I’ve already posted the 50 SB), so the call offers tempting raw pot odds, but out of position versus the BB I’ll face difficult decisions postflop with almost no equity realizability or fold equity. Raising here with these cards is overly loose and thinly disguised as a bluff against a shorter-stacked, potentially sticky opponent. Better to conserve chips and wait for a playable hand in position. Fold and preserve stack for better spots.

Q♥J♥ on the button is a strong, positionally advantaged hand — suited Broadway with good playability postflop. Standard exploitative line vs the two blinds is an open-raise to seize initiative, isolate, and charge the blinds for equity. A 3x open to 300 is a compact, effective size: it gives us fold equity against the small blind's wide defending range, reduces multiway scenarios, and keeps SPR manageable with deep stacks. Plan: c-bet favorable flops, realize equity in position if called, and re-evaluate vs any sizeable 3‑bet (fold or call depending on sizing and opponent tendencies).

I am in the Small Blind with J6 offsuit. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. J6 is a weak hand to play out of position against a button open, especially with the Big Blind (Elon) still to act and having a very deep stack. Folding here is the standard and most disciplined play to avoid difficult post-flop situations with a marginal hand.

Facing a button open-raise to 300 (3BB) in the big blind with TJo offsuit. 3-handed play with position post-flop (acting second heads-up). Button ranges are wide (~40-50% of hands), pot odds excellent (200 into 450, ~4.2:1, need ~19% equity but have much more vs. wide range). TJo has strong playability: broadway potential, straight draws, overcard value. Folding too tight in BB defense spot; 3-betting unnecessary as hand prefers set-mining/flop realization over polarized range. Call to realize equity multi-street with position.

Flop of Td Ad 5d is extremely wet: three diamonds to the Ace, possible flushes, nut flush draws, wheel straight draws (A54), broadway draws. Hold Th Js for top pair (Tens) with Jack kicker, no diamond, no straight draw. Vulnerable hand OOP (act first heads-up vs button). Button open range wide 3-handed (~40-50%: Ax, broadway, suited connectors, any two). Hits her range hard: Ax common, diamond draws frequent. Betting thin value risks raises from better Ax, sets, draws, flush; folds worse hands. Check-fold to large cbets (Ax+, flush), check-call small cbets (draws, weak Ax, bluffs) to realize equity (~40-50% vs her range). Pot control essential with marginal made hand on scary board. Check-raise too strong, unbalances range. Preflop call realized equity well; continue controlled play.

I opened pre with Q♥J♥ to 300 and called heads-up by the BB. Flop A♦ T♦ 5♦ is pretty unfriendly for my hand — I have two overcards and only a King (4 outs) to complete Broadway; no hearts and no backdoor flush. Elon checked, so I have position and some fold equity. A small continuation bet here (~1/2 pot) makes sense: it represents an Ace/strong diamond and will fold a lot of unpaired broadways and weaker hands that called his blind, while keeping the pot controllable if called. If he calls I can float and evaluate the turn (barrel harmless bricks, fold to heavy aggression), but I will respect a significant check‑raise since my equity is thin. Betting 300 is a cheap way to pressure his wide checking range while preserving stack depth for later streets.

Facing a small 1/3 pot cbet (300 into 950) on a draw-heavy Td Ad 5d flop with top pair tens and jack kicker (Th Js), OOP heads-up vs button's wide range (~40-50%: Ax, broadway, suited connectors). Pot odds excellent (~3.2:1 effective, ~24% equity needed; hand crushes bluffs/missed draws/weak Ax, has ~40-50% vs range). Vulnerable to Ax+, flush draws, but small sizing suggests draws, weak pairs, bluffs rather than strong value (monsters might bet larger or check-raise). Check-folding top pair too tight vs small bet; check-raising unbalances range (too strong for thin value, invites floats). Calling realizes equity multi-street, controls pot, induces bluffs on later streets while blocking broadway Ts. Preflop call played correctly; continue disciplined postflop.

Board is Td Ad 5d 2d: four diamonds, meaning any single diamond in Minni's wide button range (~40-50% hands) gives her a flush. Our Th Js is top pair tens with jack kicker, but absolutely crushed by flushes (frequent: Adx, Kds+, Qds+, suited connectors with d), better aces, sets. Turn 2d completes diamond draws that hit her range hard and worsens position dramatically. Preflop call and flop call were correct realizing equity vs small bet, but now OOP on monster board, donk betting thin value gets raised by flushes/better Ax, called by draws; no fold equity. Check preserves stack, induces bluffs/checks from weak Ax/bluffs/missed draws, controls pot, allows fold to value bets (any reasonable size). Check-behind showdown possible with marginal made hand. Deep stacks favor discipline over forcing action.

Board: Td Ad 5d 2d (four diamonds). My hand QhJh has no diamond and no pair — just two overcards to the Ten and poor showdown equity vs ranges that called my flop bet. Elon called the flop on a 3-diamond board, so any single diamond in his hand is now a made flush; he could also have Ax, a Ten, or a pair. Betting the turn here would be a thin bluff that gets called or check‑raised by many hands that already beat me. I want to control the pot and avoid being forced off or check‑raised by a made flush, so I will check and see his action. If he checks back I get to see a river cheaply; if he bets, I will fold to large/committed sizing and only consider calling very small bets that look like probes/bluffs.

River Ah pairs the board's ace, creating potential two-pair combos (Ax), trips (pocket AA unlikely), or full houses, while diamond flushes remain crushing (frequent in button's wide ~40-50% range: Adx, Kds+, suited diamonds). Turn check-behind after flush completion suggests Minni may lack nuts (value might bet turn for value/protection or check-raise flop), hitting bluffs/missed draws/weak Ax/draws. Th Js tops pair tens/J kicker has showdown value vs worse pairs (e.g., Jx, Tx weak, naked draws), but betting thin value risks raises from Ax+/flush, called by better; no fold equity OOP on scary river. Check controls pot, induces bluffs/checks from ~50%+ of range (missed draws, weak pairs), allows cheap showdown or fold to large value bets. Preflop/flop/turn play realized equity well; continue disciplined pot control deep-stacked 3-handed.

Board runs out Ad Td 5d 2d Ah. I hold QhJh — no pair, no diamond, no straight. On the flop there were three diamonds and the turn put a fourth diamond on board, so any single diamond in an opponent’s hand became a made flush on the turn. Elon called my flop c-bet and then checked the turn and river. That line is consistent with either a slow-played strong made hand (a diamond flush or a disguised two-pair/trips/full-house candidate) or a non-diamond Ace/Ten/medium pair that is pot-controlling. The river pairing the Ace further increases the number of hands that beat me (A‑x, any flush, or full houses if someone had an Ace with a diamond or a pocket pair). I have essentially zero showdown value and very little fold equity — a river bluff is high variance and unlikely to fold enough of Elon’s range to be +EV. With no bet to call and a big effective stack behind, the correct exploitative line is to check and fold to a value-sized river bet. I will check and concede the pot unless Elon gives me a very small probing bet that I can reasonably call (which is unlikely given the board texture).

I am on the button with a pocket pair of sixes (6s6h). In a three-handed game, this is a very strong hand to open with. Standard professional strategy dictates a raise to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds or play a heads-up pot in position. My previous opening raises have been to 300 (3x the big blind), so I will maintain that consistency.

Preflop in SB facing a standard button raise to 300 from Flash (wide opening range ~30-40%). My hand is A8o, which has good pot odds to call (250 into 450, ~1.8:1 immediate odds, building to ~700 pot heads-up or more). A8o is a standard SB defending hand vs BTN open due to ace blocker, playability when hitting top pair, and ability to make strong hands. OOP is a disadvantage, but stacks are deep (77bb effective), so postflop maneuvering possible. Folding is too tight; 3-betting is not ideal as A8o is not well-suited for it (better for value or bluffs like suited connectors). Will call and see flop, planning to cbet or check-raise based on board texture.

I’m on the big blind with 4♣9♣ (4h9c) facing a button raise to 300 and the SB calling — a 3-way pot. Pot is 700 and I must call 200, meaning I need ~22% equity to justify a call. 4-9 offsuit is a weak, poorly-connected hand that will be dominated by much of both opponents’ ranges and has very poor postflop playability out of position in a multiway pot. There are minimal implied odds and few good turn/river realizations. A raise/3‑bet here would be thin and ill-advised given the action and stack sizes. Folding preserves my stack for better spots.

Flop hits my Ac8d very well for top pair (pair of aces) with an ace kicker, effectively TPTK on this 2d Ah Jc rainbow board. The board is dryish but has backdoor straight draws (e.g., KT, QT) and possible Jx hands in villain's wide BTN opening range. OOP as SB heads-up, checking is optimal: it induces bluffs/cbets from Flash's wide range (misses like KQ, suited connectors, pocket pairs below J), sets up check-raise for value/protection, and controls pot if checked back. Leading risks getting check-raised by better (AJ, AJ suited, sets) or folding out worse; check-raise sizing can be planned to ~1200-1500 vs cbet. Deep stacks (77bb eff) favor realizing equity multiway postflop, but heads-up OOP check plays to position disadvantage. Folding or checking through weak never; betting too thin value here.

I am the pre-flop aggressor with pocket sixes on a board of A-J-2. This board heavily favors a caller's range if they have any Ace or Jack, which are common in the Small Blind's calling range. My pair of sixes has some showdown value but is very vulnerable to overcards and better pairs. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and can potentially see a cheap showdown or evaluate the turn. Betting doesn't accomplish much here as better hands won't fold and worse hands are unlikely to call, except for perhaps draws like KQ or QT.

Turn card Kd is a significant one, pairing no immediate hands but completing straight draws (e.g., QT from Flash's wide BTN range) and improving Kx hands (KJ, KT, KQ) to top pair or better, as well as two-pair combos like AK/AJ. My Ac8d remains TPTK but now faces more vulnerable equity OOP heads-up after check-check flop, which suggests Flash missed flop (overpairs below A, gutshots, backdoors) or has showdown value like Jx/TT/99/Ax worse than mine. Pot 700 with deep effective stacks (~77bb), no need to bet thin value here as it risks folding out worse hands (e.g., Jx, bluffs) while inviting raises from improved hands (sets, straights, two-pair). Checking retains pot control, induces bluffs from Flash's missed range on scary board (he may bet turn as semi-bluff/draw), and allows check-call/raise vs bet sizing. Betting ~400-600 would be too thin and polarizes my range awkwardly after check-check; folding impossible with TPTK. Will check, planning to call reasonable bets (~500-1000) or raise larger ones for value/protection.

I hold pocket sixes on a board of A-J-2-K. This board is very dangerous for my hand as it contains three overcards to my pair. However, Elon has checked twice (flop and turn), suggesting he might have a weak hand or is playing a marginal made hand like a Jack or a weak King cautiously. Betting here doesn't provide much value because any hand that calls will likely have me beat, and I can't easily represent a hand strong enough to fold out an Ace or a King. Checking back allows me to maintain pot control and see a free river, keeping the possibility of winning at showdown if Elon is holding a smaller pair or a missed draw.

River 4s is a blank that doesn't significantly change the board, pairing nothing relevant and adding a spade flush draw that's unlikely given check-check flop and turn. My Ac8d gives top pair (Aces) with a weak 8 kicker on a board with possible straights (QT), two-pair (AK, AJ), sets (22, AA, JJ, KK unlikely after checks), or Jx/Ax worse. Heads-up OOP after mutual check-check streets, Flash's range is capped: missed draws (KQ, suited connectors), Jx, mid-pairs (TT/99), or Ax worse than mine (A-rag). Betting thin value (~400-700) risks folding out worse (Jx, bluffs, weaker Ax) while inviting calls/raises from betters (straights, two-pair) and polarizes my range awkwardly post-check-check. Checking induces bluffs/value bets from Flash's ~60-70% missed range (BTN wide open), allows cheap showdown if checked back, and realizes equity by check-calling reasonable sizes (~500-1400, <2:1 pot odds). Folding impossible with TPTK; leading suboptimal OOP on blank river in small pot (700, ~23bb eff for Flash). Deep stacks favor pot control and extracting value via calls. Plan: check, call ~50-75% pot bets, raise >pot for protection/value/protect against bluffs.

The board contains three overcards (Ace, Jack, and King) to my pocket sixes. Elon has checked on the flop, turn, and river after calling my pre-flop raise from the Small Blind. This line typically indicates a weak hand or a marginal made hand looking for a cheap showdown. My pocket sixes have some showdown value against complete air or lower pocket pairs like 33 or 55. Betting is unlikely to fold out a better hand (like a weak Jack) and is unlikely to be called by a worse hand. Therefore, the best play is to check back and see the showdown.

Preflop on the button in 3-handed play with an unopened pot (pot 150 suggests SB likely folded, leaving blinds money). My hand 4d6s is offsuit with one-gapper potential but marginal. However, button position is powerful, and opening ranges are very wide 3-handed (50%+ of hands). Raising steals dead money frequently, and if called, I have position for postflop playability. Standard open to 3x BB (300) based on my previous raising patterns. Limping is suboptimal as it doesn't apply pressure; folding too tight long-term.

I call to 300 total (putting in the extra 250). A♠9♥ is strong enough to defend a 3bb button open from the small blind: it has decent showdown value, blocks many A‑x combos, and gets ~1.8:1 pot odds (~36% equity needed) given the current pot. I'm out of position vs both players, so I don't want to inflate the pot with a light 3‑bet — calling keeps the pot manageable, preserves maneuverability post‑flop, and lets me realize equity on A‑high/9‑high flops or play draws. I'll evaluate the flop and respond to Elon's continuation bet or the BB's action accordingly.

I am in the Big Blind with Td 3h. Elon raised to 300 from the Button and Minni called from the Small Blind. Although I am getting decent pot odds (200 to call into a pot of 700), T3o is an extremely weak hand that performs poorly multi-way and out of position. I'll fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

Check. I hold A♠9♥ — a pair of nines with an Ace kicker (decent showdown value) but it’s effectively a second/middle pair on a K‑9‑7 rainbow and I’m out of position versus Elon's button opening range. Leading out OOP would bloat the pot and put me in tough spots against Kx/overpairs and potential check‑raises. Checking controls the pot, keeps bluffs and worse pairs in, and lets me realize equity while blocking many A‑x combos (I hold the As). Plan: call small-to-medium continuation bets (~≤½ pot) to extract from worse hands and fold to large-sized overbets or check-raises unless the turn improves me; if Elon checks back I’ll often take the initiative on the turn or go to showdown.

Flop is Kc 9s 7d, rainbow, king-high board that hits my button raising range well (Kx, broadways, Ax). My 4d6s is pure air, no pair/draw, but position on Minni (SB caller, checked) allows a profitable continuation bet. Her check suggests weakness, and small bet applies fold equity vs her wide calling range while keeping pot manageable deep-stacked (SPR ~20). Betting 1/2 pot (350) balances value/bluffs, consistent with prior small sizing patterns. Check too passive, invites free cards/check-back.

I call 350. I have A♠9♥ on 9♠‑7♦‑K♣ — second pair (nines) with a very strong Ace kicker. Elon opened from the button and fired a relatively small C‑bet (350), which is exactly the size I want to call with a hand that has decent showdown value and blocks a lot of A‑x bluffs. Raising OOP with a vulnerable second pair would bloat the pot and put me in uncomfortable spots vs Kx, overpairs or sets; calling controls the pot, keeps worse nines, 7x and some bluffs in, and lets me reevaluate on the turn. Plan: if he continues with a large turn barrel or a check‑raise on this street I will fold; if the turn is a blank I may lead or call moderately depending on sizing, and if I improve to two pair or pair the Ace I'll become more aggressive. Given stack sizes and the modest bet, a straight call is the optimal, low‑risk line to extract value and gather information.

Elon checked the turn after a small flop C‑bet. I hold A♠9♥ — second pair with the best possible Ace kicker. This is a hand with decent showdown value but also one that can get thin value from worse A‑highs and underpairs and can charge any backdoor draws. Taking the initiative now is better than passively checking to showdown: a modest half‑pot bet extracts from worse hands and keeps the pot manageable OOP. I will bet ~50% pot (700 into 1400). If he folds I take the pot; if he calls I re‑evaluate on the river; if he check‑raises strongly I will usually fold because second pair OOP is unlikely to fare well vs a turn check‑raise.

Facing Minni's turn lead of 700 into 1400 (pot now 2100) on Kc9s7d5d board with 4d6s (open-ender: 3x/8x for straight, 8 outs ~17% to river; backdoor diamond flush draw for added equity/playability). Position on button, deep stacks (SPR ~6 post-call). Her check-call flop c-bet (small sizing) then turn lead suggests possible mid-pair, weak Kx, or incomplete draw; less likely monsters given history of check-calls. Pot odds 4:1 require ~20% equity, but draw + position worth more. Raising semi-bluff exploits fold equity vs her wide calling range (passive tendencies in prior hands), gets value from worse (Ax/Kx/draws), protects, and builds pot for river jam potential if improves. Call too passive (misses FE); fold weak given equity/POT. Size to ~3x her bet (2100 total street) consistent with aggressive 3-handed button play, prior sizings. Minni folds often enough in history (e.g., Hand 2/4/5).

Fold. Elon’s line (button open, small flop c‑bet, then checking the turn and turning my 700 into a 2,100 check‑raise) is very value‑heavy on this K‑9‑7‑5 rainbow board — likely Kx, sets or big pairs — and contains relatively few credible bluffs. The pot is 4,200 and I must call 1,400 more; although the pot odds are reasonable, his action polarizes to hands that usually dominate my second pair (A9). I block some A‑x bluffs by holding the A♠, but not enough to justify calling or risking a bluff‑raise OOP. Consistent with my earlier plan to fold to a strong check‑raise, I fold to preserve stack and look for a better spot.

On the button in a 3-handed game, 5♦4♣ is marginal but has decent post‑flop playability and gains a lot from position. The table has been using ~300 opens, and a raise here buys the initiative and fold equity vs SB/BB. Pot is 150 with 100 to call — I’ll open to the standard 300 to either take the pot immediately or see a flop in position where I can apply pressure. If faced with a sizable 3‑bet (Elon has shown willingness to 3‑bet), I’ll fold this hand; against a small 3‑bet I’ll consider pot odds and stack interaction but generally avoid getting too sticky with 54o out of position. In short: open to 300 to leverage position and steal the blinds while preserving a clear plan vs aggression.

I am holding AK offsuit in the Small Blind. This is a premium hand, especially in a 3-handed game. Minni opened from the button for 300. I should 3-bet here for value and to gain initiative, as playing AK out of position is much easier as the aggressor. A standard 3-bet size from the small blind against a 3x open is around 3.5x to 4x. I will raise to 1100 to put pressure on the button and discourage the big blind from entering the pot with a wide range.

Preflop in the big blind facing a button raise to 300 and a small blind 3-bet to 1100, with a pot of 1500 and 1000 to call. My hand 5h3c is unconnected offsuit trash with no high card value or straight potential, performing poorly against typical 3-bet ranges (pairs, high broadways). Pot odds require ~29% equity to call (1000 into 2500), but 53o has <20% vs a tight 3-bet range and even less multiway if Minni continues. No fold equity on a call, out of position postflop, better to fold and preserve stack for better spots in a long 150-hand game.

I opened to 300 to leverage position, but Flash’s 3‑bet to 1,100 is a standard, non‑trivial size from the small blind. 5♦4♣ is a marginal offsuit connector — playable in position vs limps/opens, but it has poor equity and is frequently dominated versus a SB 3‑bet range (pockets, broadways, some suited connectors). Calling 800 more into this pot commits chips with weak equity and creates difficult multi‑street decisions; 4‑betting to 1,900 as a bluff is too thin (no strong blockers and Flash can call or shove with better hands). With healthy stacks and many hands left to play, preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is the +EV line. Therefore I fold.

I am on the button with a premium pocket pair (JJ). In a three-handed game, this hand is extremely strong. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to build the pot and take control of the hand. Given Elon's high aggression and Minni's willingness to call or fold under pressure, I want to isolate one of them or win the blinds immediately.

Pocket Aces is the nuts pre-flop, the best possible starting hand. Facing a button raise from Flash, who has been aggressive in prior hands, I expect a wide range. As SB, I'm out of position, but AA plays well multi-way or heads-up. Standard play is to 3-bet for value to build the pot, isolate, and charge draws/off-suit hands. Raising to 900 (3x the open) is a balanced size given deep stacks (19k effective vs. Flash's 7.75k). Minni in BB may call light, but we'll realize equity post-flop. Folding or flat-calling is -EV.

I’m in the Big Blind with Qh8d facing a small‑blind 3‑bet to 900 after the button opened to 300. Pot is 1300 and I must call 800 more (900 total) — requiring roughly 38% equity. Q8o is a marginal, often‑dominated hand with poor postflop playability out of position against a likely value‑heavy 3‑bet range (pairs, AK, AQ, suited broadways). A min 4‑bet to 1500 would be a thin bluff with poor blockers and would commit chips unnecessarily. Given opponent tendencies (Elon shows aggression and often takes big lines) and my stack preservation goal, folding is the correct, low‑variance play.

I hold Pocket Jacks on the button, which is a very strong hand in three-handed play. Elon has 3-bet my opening raise from the Small Blind. Based on Elon's aggressive tendencies observed in previous hands, such as his frequent 3-betting and post-flop pressure, I will 4-bet to 2400 for value. This allows me to take control of the pot and charge his wider 3-betting range. Given my stack size, I am comfortable playing JJ for my entire stack if Elon decides to shove.

Pocket Aces remains the nuts pre-flop. Flash's 4-bet from the button after my 3-bet is polarized: premium hands like KK/QQ/AK or bluffs suited broadways/AX suited/gappers. My equity is over 75-80% against his range. Pot odds to call 1500 into 3400 are excellent (3.2:1), but OOP heads-up with a huge pot post-flop is tough, even with AA. Shoving all-in for 8050 total (2400 his bet + his remaining 5650) maximizes value: gets calls from worse (KK/QQ/AK), folds bluffs, and denies equity realization. His effective total stack ~8050 vs pot ~3400; he can't fold worse without regret. History shows Flash aggressive but folds to heavy aggression (e.g., Hand 14 re-raise). Standard play with AA OOP vs short-stack 4-bet.

I hold Pocket Jacks on the button. I initiated the hand with a raise to 300, and Elon 3-bet to 900. I then 4-bet to 2400. Elon has now 5-bet jammed for effectively my entire stack (8050). In a three-handed game, Pocket Jacks is an extremely strong hand, and Elon's playstyle throughout the previous 14 hands has shown high aggression and a willingness to apply maximum pressure. While his range could include AA, KK, or QQ, it also contains AK and potentially several smaller pairs or bluffs given the dynamic. Since I have already committed 2400 of my 8050 chips and the pot odds are very favorable (I need to call 5650 to win a total pot of 16200), folding JJ here would be too tight in this format. I am committed to the hand.

Preflop on the button with 4c9h offsuit, a weak gapped hand with poor playability postflop. In 3-handed play, button range is wide, but 49o is too weak to open or limp given opponents' history of defending blinds reasonably. Folding preserves stack for better spots. No value or strong bluff equity here.

I'm heads-up from the small blind with 7♥5♥ — a suited, connected-ish hand with decent postflop playability. The pot is 150 and I only need to put in 50 more to see a flop (total contribution = 100), giving me ~4:1 immediate pot odds and strong implied odds with both stacks deep. Raising to 200 to attempt a steal is possible but unnecessary and risky out of position with a marginal hand (it also invites 3-bets). I will defend the blind by calling, see the flop, and proceed based on texture: continue with hearts/strong draws or pairs, fold to heavy aggression when I miss.

I am in the Big Blind with Qc8c. The Small Blind (Minni) has limped into the pot. Given that Minni has frequently raised to 300 when entering hands previously, a limp likely indicates a wider or weaker range. Since I have a solid suited hand and will have position for the remainder of the hand, I will raise to 400 to take initiative and extract value from her weaker range.

Heads-up out of position with 7♥5♥ — a suited connector with good postflop playability. The raise to 400 is not large relative to the stacks, and calling 300 more gives me reasonable immediate pot odds plus excellent implied odds with both stacks deep (I have 12.7k, Flash ~15.8k). Raising OOP with this marginal hand is unnecessary and will often put me in uncomfortable spots against stronger hands. I will call to see a flop and play postflop: continue with any hearts, pairs, two‑pair or better, and strong draws; be prepared to fold to heavy turn/river aggression when I miss. I will call the 300 required (total committed after my action = 350).

I completely missed this flop with 7♥5♥ on 8♠ J♦ 2♠: no pair, no spade, only a tiny backdoor heart/one-card straight possibility (only a 6 helps). I’m out of position versus the preflop raiser and have very little equity or fold equity to justify barreling into the lead. Best line is to keep the pot small: check and concede to aggression. If Flash checks back I’ll take a free turn card and re-evaluate; if he bets I’ll fold unless the price or read justifies a call/float.

On this flop of 8s-Jd-2s, I have middle pair (8s and 8c) with a Queen kicker. Minni has checked to me. This board is relatively dynamic with a spade draw and some straight possibilities (T-9). Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and Minni just called my raise from the small blind, my range has a significant advantage here. Betting allows me to protect my hand against overcards, extract value from draws or weaker pairs (like small pocket pairs or deuces), and maintain control of the hand. A bet of roughly half the pot should clarify where I stand.

I completely missed this flop with 7♥5♥ — no pair, no spade, and no realistic draw. Flash’s 450 into a 1250 pot (~26.5% break-even) makes calling marginal: I have only three 7s and three 5s (≈24% to pair by river) and tiny runner-runner straight chances. He opened preflop and barreled the dry J-8-2 with two spades, so his range heavily contains made pairs and spade draws that dominate or have fold equity against me. Out of position, with no blocker value and limited implied odds, this is a clear fold to preserve stack and wait for a better spot.

I’m on the button with K♣4♦ (K4o off-suit) and both blinds posted (pot 150, to-call 100). With deep stacks (~124BB) and only two opponents, button position is highly valuable — this hand is marginal but has good positional steal potential. I’ll open to a standard 3xBB (300 total) to apply pressure and deny cheap flops. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet (hand is often dominated), but if called use positional advantage to c-bet thin on dry flops or play pot-control and fold to heavy resistance on coordinated/wet boards.

I am in the Small Blind with 7d 3s, which is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. Minni raised to 300 from the Button. Folding is the only viable option here, as calling out of position with trash cards is a recipe for losing chips.

Preflop in BB facing a button raise to 300 (200 to call into 450 pot). Qs9s is a strong speculative hand with suited connectors, good playability for straights/flushes, and decent equity against a wide button opening range. Pot odds are excellent (~4.5:1), and postflop I'll act first heads-up but can realize equity well. Folding weak, raising too strong OOP vs button. Standard defend.

Flop board is 3h-Ah-7h, extremely coordinated with flush draws, ace-high, and potential straights. My Qs9s has no pair, no flush draw (spades), no straight draw (gaps don't connect), and queen-high has minimal showdown value OOP. Preflop pot odds were good for speculative defend, but flop misses completely. Vs button raiser's wide range, villain likely bets frequently (Ax, flush draws, bluffs). Checking to induce bluffs or realize fold equity isn't ideal OOP with no hand; best to check-fold to any reasonable bet, preserving stack. No value bet or semi-bluff. Pot odds irrelevant since acting first with air.

I opened preflop and Elon just flat-called. Flop A♥ 7♥ 3♥ and he checked — I hold K♥4♣: no pair but a strong heart draw (9 outs, ~35% to hit by river) and I block some K♥ combos. This is a very coordinated, Ace-high heart board where my hand is a draw rather than a made hand, so I want to control the pot and realize equity cheaply rather than bloat the pot and face a check-raise. I’ll check to see the turn; plan is to call a reasonable single-barrel bet to chase the flush (or fold to heavy aggression), and to ramp up only if I improve or get clear info (heart or a harmless card) on the turn.

Turn board Jc is a relatively blank card for my specific hand, adding straight possibilities (e.g., 8-10, 9-10) but not helping Qs9s at all—no pair, no flush draw (spades), no straight draw (Q-9 doesn't connect). Still queen-high air OOP. Villain checked flop on a highly coordinated 3h-Ah-7h board, suggesting possible weakness (missed draws/aces or trap), but betting pure air OOP risks spew against a wide button range that can call light or raise. Check preserves pot control, induces bluffs if villain bets, and avoids bloating pot with nothing. Plan to check-fold river unless massive bluff spot or check-check to showdown if villain weak.

Board on the turn: 3♥ A♥ 7♥ J♣; my hand K♥4♣. I have no pair but a four-flush (one card to make the nut-ish heart flush). From the turn I have 9 outs to hit on the river (9/46 ≈ 19.6%). Elon flat-called preflop then checked the flop and the turn — a passive line that can indicate missed hands, pot-control with an Ace/top pair, or a cautious slowplay, but it makes it less likely he already has a two-heart made flush (he would often value-bet that). Betting now would be a semi-bluff that can fold out worse A-high or medium holdings, but it also bloats the pot when I only have ~20% to improve and exposes me to a check-raise or a river decision facing a big bet. With deep stacks and position I prefer to realize equity cheaply and keep the pot manageable. I’ll check and see a river card. Plan: if Elon leads on the river I will call reasonable-sized bets that give correct pot odds to chase the flush (fold to very large overbets); if I complete the flush I will extract value; if the river bricks and he checks, decide whether a small bluff or a showdown attempt is appropriate depending on the card.

River 9c pairs my 9s, giving me middle pair (9s) with queen kicker, which has decent showdown value against a button range that checked flop and turn on a coordinated board (3h-Ah-7h-Jc). No strong draws completed for me (spades flush draw never hit), and board remains scary with nut flush possible (hearts) or strong Ax/Jx. OOP after double check-check streets, villain shows weakness by not betting earlier; betting thin value risks folding out worse while getting raised by better, and pure bluff unnecessary with showdown value. Checking preserves pot control, realizes equity by going to showdown cheaply, induces bluffs if villain bets, and is standard with marginal made hand OOP on dangerous board. Pair likely good often vs checked-back range (missed draws, weak pairs), but we don't want to bloat pot.

I opened preflop and on the 3♥ A♥ 7♥ flop I had K♥4♣ — a four-card heart draw — and I checked to control the pot. The turn J♣ and river 9♣ both brick for my draw, so I’m left with K‑high. Elon called preflop then checked flop, turn and river (a very passive line). That line reduces my fold equity on a river bluff — he’s likely to check‑call with A‑x, medium pairs or showdown‑thin hands rather than fold. The pot is only 650 and I don’t want to risk chips bluffing a missed draw into a passive caller. I’ll check and take the showdown (conserve stack and avoid a low‑EV bluff).

I am on the button with Ace-Three offsuit (Ah 3s). This is a standard opening hand in a 3-handed game to put pressure on the blinds. A standard raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is appropriate here to build the pot and potentially take down the blinds immediately.

Preflop in SB facing button raise to 300 (call 250 into 450 pot). Holding 5h9h, a weak suited gapped connector. Poor post-flop position, BB yet to act and could squeeze. Button range is wide but still ahead of this hand most of the time. Pot odds require ~36% equity to call (250/700), but 95s has poor implied odds OOP and low showdown value. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities.

Button opened to 3bb (300). I’m in the BB with pocket deuces and effective stacks ~120bb. Pot is 450 and it costs me 200 more to call (300 total). With 22 the most +EV line vs a single BTN raiser is to call and set-mine — Flash’s button opening range is wide so implied odds are strong if I hit a set. A small raise (to 500) doesn’t help much here and 3-betting or folding would be too exploitative given my hand’s nature. Plan: call to see a flop; fold to heavy aggression on very unfavorable boards, but extract value/raise when I hit trips. Therefore I will call (total commit 300).

I’m OOP with a small pocket pair (22) on a dry J♣ 3♥ 5♣ flop. Flash opened wide from the button preflop, so my hand’s best line is set-mining — realize equity cheaply rather than lead out. Checking keeps the pot small, lets me call a reasonable c-bet to see a turn for my set, and avoids bloating the pot when I’m currently behind any Jx. I’ll check and call small-to-medium bets to pursue a set; only raise if I hit trips or face extremely weak timing tells. Therefore: check.

On a flop of Jc-3h-5c, I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker. Minni checked to me as the pre-flop aggressor. While middle/bottom pair has showdown value, this board is somewhat draw-heavy with the club flush draw and various straight possibilities (4-6, 2-4). Betting here accomplishes two things: it protects my hand against overcards and it extracts value from draws or weaker hands. A bet of around half the pot (300) is a standard continuation bet size that allows me to maintain initiative.

Call 300. I’m OOP with pocket 2s on a very dry J♣-3♥-5♣ flop facing a small BTN c-bet. This is a classic set-mining spot: Flash opened wide preflop and my deepest +EV line is to realize equity rather than bluff/raise. Calling 300 into a 950 pot gives ~4.2:1 immediate odds and with ~118bb effective the implied odds to extract big value if I hit a set are strong (≈8.5% to make a set by river). Raising here just bloats the pot with a weak hand and surrenders my ability to realize equity; heavy turn aggression without improvement can be folded to, but if I hit trips I will extract value. Therefore I call to see the turn.

Check. I have a small pocket pair (22) and did not improve on the turn. The board is J♣-3♥-5♣-4♣ — now three clubs on board, so there are made flushes and strong draws possible from the Button, who opened wide preflop and barreled the flop. My long-term +EV plan is set-mining: realize equity cheaply rather than bloating the pot out of position. Betting here would just bloat a pot I’m likely behind in and give up implied odds if I hit a set. I’ll check to keep the pot controlled and either see a free or inexpensive river or call a small probe bet; only raise if I actually hit trips or face an extremely weak, thin bet line on the river.

The turn card (4c) is quite dangerous as it completes both a potential flush and various straight draws (any hand with a 2). Having bottom pair and a gutshot straight draw, my hand has some showdown value but is vulnerable. Since Minni checked, I will check behind to take a free river card, control the size of the pot, and avoid being check-raised on a board that likely improved her range or completed her draws.

I called preflop/flop to set-mine with 2♣2♥ and never improved. Board is J♣ 3♥ 5♣ 4♣ 7♠, pot 1250 and there is currently no bet to face. My pair of twos has almost no showdown value — it loses to any Jx, any pair above deuces, sets, and made club flushes. Flash’s line (BTN open, flop c‑bet, turn check) is consistent with a wide range that includes both marginal one‑pair hands and bluffs. Out of position and with no meaningful blocker, a river bluff here is thin EV and risks a lot of chips for little fold equity. Best line is to check and take the free showdown; if Flash now fires a bet I’ll fold to meaningful pressure. Preserving chips and avoiding a low‑probability bluff is the +EV play.

I have bottom pair (pair of 3s) on a board where a flush and various straights (like 2-6 or A-2) are possible. After I checked back the turn, the river is a 7 of spades, which doesn't significantly change the board texture. Minni has checked for the third time, indicating weakness or a desire to reach showdown cheaply. My hand has some showdown value against missed draws (like high-card clubs or gutshots), but it is unlikely to get a better hand to fold or a worse hand to call if I bet. Therefore, checking behind to realize my showdown value is the most prudent play.

Preflop on the button in a 3-handed game with A8o, pot 150 (blinds posted, likely SB folded leaving dead money), to call 100. A8o is a strong enough opening hand from the button with position on the BB. Standard play is to raise for value, fold equity, and to steal the blinds/dead money. Consistent with previous opening sizes in history, raise to 300 (3x BB). Deep stacks allow for postflop play if called, as ace-high has good playability.

Facing a standard button open to 300 from the Button while I’m in the small blind with K♣5♠ (off-suit) I’m dominated by a large portion of the raiser’s range (KQ, KJ, KT, AK, broadway combos and pairs). There’s no suitedness or strong connectivity — very poor playability and few backdoors — and I’ll be out of position postflop against both opponents. Calling 250 into a 450 pot gives me ~36% immediate pot equity required, which K5o rarely has versus a sane opening range, and 3-betting as a bluff with this hand is a leak long-term. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot rather than playing a dominated marginal hand out of position.

K9o is a standard defending hand in the Big Blind against a Button open, especially in a 3-handed game. Elon's Button opening range is likely wide, and with pot odds of 2.25:1 (200 to call into 450), I have sufficient equity to see a flop. While I will be out of position, the strength of the hand relative to a Button range warrants a call.

On a board of Qh-Jd-2c, I have missed completely with my K9o. This board texture heavily favors Elon's Button opening range, which includes many Broadway combinations (AQ, KQ, QJ, JJ, QQ, etc.). As the Big Blind, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading here would rarely be successful given the range advantage Elon holds. If Elon bets, I will likely fold unless the sizing is extremely small, as I have no significant draws or made hands.

Heads-up on the flop after preflop raise from button and villain check from BB. Holding ace-high with no pair or strong draw, but with position and a blocker to top pair/top of range. The board is relatively dry (low 2, JQ broadway cards, rainbow), so villain's calling range from BB (loose cold call) often has weak pairs, draws, or floats that we can barrel profitably. C-bet half-pot for value against worse ace-high/overpairs to QJ, to deny equity to gutshots/flush draws, and frequent folds from air. Sizing 350 matches historical small bet sizes and keeps pot controllable with deep stacks (~11bb pot post-bet).

I have completely missed this flop with King-high. The board texture (Q-J-2) is very favorable for Elon's button-opening range, which contains many high cards and pairs. With no pair, no straight draw, and no flush draw, I have very little equity to continue. Even if I hit a King, it might not be enough if Elon has a Queen or a better King (like KQ).
