After investing ~1700 (preflop 300 + flop 400 + turn 1000), SPR ~3:1 her end, she's pot-committed for medium strength but IP can value bet or check. Our c-bet/turn bet line reps strength (sets, two-pair, QK/AQ/AK, clubs), but Ace caps villain somewhat. OOP river check for pot control: induces value bets from Ax we fold to, bluffs (rare but possible per history e.g., Hand 42/46/49 Minni rivers), checks behind worse Qx/pairs/draws. Thin value bet (e.g., 1700-2200 half-over pot) lacks callers (weaker fold, Ax calls/raises); bluff jam too polarized/inefficient post-two calls low fold equity. History: Minni calls multi-street but checks rivers often (Hands 11/17/20/25/28/55/61 check-checks). Checking maximizes EV: showdown value vs ~20-30% range, avoid spew.

I hold Q♠J♥ — top pair with a medium kicker on 3h Qc 2c 8s Ad. Elon opened from the SB and barreled flop (400) and turn (1000) then checked the Ace on the river. That line is mixed: it includes value (Ax, Qx, some pocket pairs) and a healthy number of turn-bluff/semi-bluff combos. Betting thin risks being called by all the hands that beat me (Ax, Qx with better kickers) and can induce a check-raise from a polarized value range; a larger bet polarizes my range unnecessarily and commits a big portion of my stack with a marginal kicker. With pot = 3,400 and zero to call, the lowest-variance, +EV play is to check back and take my showdown equity. I preserve my stack, avoid bloating the pot when likely behind to Aces/better queens, and still beat many of his missed barrels.

Preflop on the button in an unopened or limped pot (pot 150, to call 100), 3-handed play. 4c5d is a playable hand with straight potential and good position. Opponents from history are passive callers, but raising steals initiative, builds pot in position, and has fold equity vs blinds/limper. Standard open-raise sizing of 3x BB to 300. Consistent with previous opening strategy.

Fold. 9♠3♥ is a garbage hand to defend out of the small blind against a button open. The pot is 450 and it costs me 250 more to call (250/(450+250)=~35.7% equity required) — 9‑3o does not have anywhere near that vs an opening range, and playing out of position against the opener (and the big blind behind) will be difficult. Min-raising or 3-betting with this holding is nonsense; preserve my 4,900 stack for better spots in position or with real equity.

I am in the Big Blind with K4o. Elon has raised to 3x from the Button and Minni has folded. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win 450), K4o is a very weak hand to play out of position against a button opening range. In 3-handed play, ranges are wider, but K4o still suffers significantly from poor playability and dominated kickers. Given my large stack lead, I prefer to fold and wait for better opportunities rather than defending a marginal hand out of position against Elon.

I’m on the button with J♦4♣ and it’s folded to me (pot 150, to call 100). In 3‑handed play the button should open a fairly wide stealing range — I have position and a Jack blocker to some of opponents’ broadway holdings. I’ll open to a standard 3x size (300) to get fold equity and to keep the pot manageable if called. If I face a large 3‑bet I’ll fold this weak offsuit; if I get one caller I’ll c‑bet favourable flops but proceed cautiously on coordinated or Ace/face‑heavy boards. Raise to 300 gives the best immediate EV here.

I am in the Small Blind with 3h 5h. Minni opened for 300 from the Button. While suited connectors have potential, 3-5 is quite low, and I will be playing out of position against both the Button and potentially the Big Blind. Given my significant stack lead and the 46BB stack of the opener, I don't need to take a speculative, out-of-position risk with this hand.

KTo offsuit in the big blind facing a button raise to 300 (minraise size). Button ranges are wide, especially with only 3 players left. KTo has good blockers (K blocks KK) and solid equity against a typical button opening range including broadways, suited aces, connectors, etc. Pot odds are favorable: calling 200 more into 450 pot (~2.25:1 direct, better implied). Defending the BB wide here is standard to realize equity OOP, though position is not ideal postflop. 3-betting risks too much OOP with a non-premium hand and deep effective stacks (mine 93bb, villain 46bb). Folding is too tight. Will call and play postflop, looking to improve or outplay.

Flop Ad 9h 7h completely misses my KTs Th (king-high with backdoor heart draw at best). Button range (Minni) from wide open includes many Ax, pairs, sets, flush draws, and straight draws that crush me. OOP acting first with no showdown value or strong draw, pure air. Equity too low (~10-20% vs polarized range) to continue profitably. Pot odds irrelevant pre-decision; check-fold is standard to avoid spew OOP. No donk value/bluff justification given board texture and position. Preserve stack for better spots.

I check. I opened wide preflop with J♦4♣ to steal the blinds and Elon defended from the BB. The flop A♦ 9♥ 7♥ heavily favors a BB defending range (lots of Ax, 9x/7x, pocket pairs and heart draws) and it’s an Ace‑heavy, two‑heart board that gives me no pair, no heart and very poor redraws. A continuation bet here has low fold equity and will often get called by better hands, so barreling would be building a pot where I’m rarely ahead. In position I prefer pot control and to let Elon show his hand — if he checks back I get a free turn card; if he leads out I’ll fold to a sizable bet and only consider calling a very small probe if I read it as a pure bluff.

Turn board 3c is a relatively blank card that doesn't complete the heart flush draw or obvious straights, but adds some gutshots (e.g., 6-5, 10-6, J-4). My KTs Th remains pure king-high with no pair, no live draws (backdoor heart draw dead), and minimal showdown value OOP. Preflop, defended wide vs button minraise (standard in 3-handed). Flop checked through (villain's check-behind suggests possible weakness: no Ax, weak draws, or traps, but unlikely nuts given history). Pot 650, villain 46bb effective. Bluffing OOP first-in on scary Ad9h7h board risks too much equity denial; villain's range still crushes me (Ax+, sets, 2p+, strong draws like J10, T8). Thin value bets unnecessary (no top pair/top kicker). Checking preserves stack, realizes equity cheaply to river (villain may check back again or small bet we can fold), and avoids spew. From hand history, Minni often continuation bets flops but checked here, indicating caution. Prior flop plan was check-fold if bet, now free card aligns. Optimal to check and reassess river.

Check. I opened wide preflop and the flop/turn (A♦ 9♥ 7♥ / 3♣) is very unfriendly to my J♦4♣ — no pair, no heart, poor redraws. Elon defending from the BB will have lots of Ax, 9x/7x, pocket pairs and some heart draws; he checked both flop and turn which looks like caution or a medium-strength holding. Betting the turn has very low fold equity versus that range and would bloat a pot where I am usually behind. With position and a 4600 stack I prefer pot control and to take a free river card to realise equity or seize a better bluffing spot later. If he checks the river I can evaluate the specific card and potentially bluff a small amount on a good blank; if he leads into me I’ll fold most sizable bets and only continue to very small probes. For now, check.

River 8h completes the obvious heart flush draw on a scary Ad-9h-7h-3c board, pairing nothing and adding straight possibilities (e.g., J10, 10-6, 5-6). My KsTh is pure king-high: no pair, no flush, no straight, minimal showdown value OOP. Preflop defended wide vs button minraise (standard 3-handed), flop/turn checked through (villain's check-backs suggest no Ax/no cbet range, weak/medium hands or traps, not nuts). Villain's button range still crushes: flushes (any 2h), Ax, sets, 2p, J10 str8. King-high has blockers (K blocks AK/KQ/KK; T blocks JTs/T9s str8 draws) but thin equity (~15-25% vs calling range). Pot 650 (6.5bb), villain eff 46bb. Bluff-betting first OOP risks spew: villain calls wide with ace-high/draws/TP, raises all-in with nuts. No fold equity given history (Minni calls stations postflop). Checking maximizes EV: realizes equity cheaply (win vs pure air/missed draws ~20% time), induces bluffs/value bets we fold profitably (preserves stack), avoids reverse implied odds. Optimal passive line with air vs competent range.

I check. The river (A♦ 9♥ 7♥ 3♣ 8♥) completes a three‑heart board and gives very little to my J♦4♣ — no pair, no heart, few useful blockers. Elon called preflop and then passively checked flop and turn; that line usually represents medium strength (Ax, 9x/7x, single pair, or missed draws) or a slow‑played value hand. A bluff here has thin fold equity because many of his hands that check carry showdown value and will call a small river probe (A8, 88/77/99, 9x, heart draws that paired or made the flush). Betting risks bloating the pot out of position with effectively zero equity; checking preserves stack and forces Elon to show aggression if he has a value hand. If he now leads I will fold to any meaningful bet and only consider calling a very tiny probe, so the correct action is to check to showdown.

I am on the button with K5o. In a three-handed game, King-high hands are generally strong enough to open for a raise from the button to steal the blinds or play in position. I have a significant chip lead, which allows me to apply pressure to the smaller stacks of Elon and Minni. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here.

T2 offsuit is an extremely weak hand with no high card value, connectivity, or suitedness. Facing a button raise from Flash, who likely has a wide range, calling from the small blind out of position is -EV. Poor implied odds, dominated often post-flop. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

I’m in the big blind (45bb) with 4♦5♦ facing a button open to 300 (SB folded). Pot is 450 and it costs me 200 to call (total commit 300). 4♦5♦ is a playable suited connector heads-up: good flop equity and implied odds to hit straights/flushes, and it plays well multi-street. The call requires ~31% equity (200/(450+200) ≈ 30.8%), which this hand can achieve vs a wide button opening range. I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand by 3-betting or shoving — that’s inviting big-pot decisions against a much larger stack. Plan: call, see the flop, and proceed based on texture (c-bet or check/fold/float depending on opponent reaction).

I’m OOP in the big blind with 4♦5♦ on 8♠7♦Qc. I have only a gutshot to a 6 (4 outs) plus backdoor diamond possibilities (10 diamonds left) — roughly 13 combined outs, ≈27–28% to improve to a made straight/flush by the river. Pot is 650 and there is no bet right now, so I should control the pot size OOP and see a turn card rather than bluff-c-bet or inflate the pot with marginal equity. Break-even call size on this flop is about 250 (b/(650+b) ≈ 0.28), so I’ll check and plan to: (a) call small continuation bets (≈≤250) because pot odds are acceptable, (b) fold to larger bets/raises that deny correct odds, and (c) if checked to me I’ll see the turn for free and continue only with improved equity (6 or diamond) or a favorable runout/opponent action. Check to realiz e equity and avoid bloating the pot OOP.

I am in position as the pre-flop aggressor after Minni checked the flop. The board (Qc-8s-7d) is fairly dry but has some middle-card connectivity. I have King-high with a backdoor straight draw. While I don't have a made hand, a continuation bet of about half the pot is standard here to take down the pot immediately or maintain the initiative against Minni's likely wide calling range from the big blind. If she calls, I still have overcards and some potential, but if she folds, I win the pot now.

I’m OOP in the BB with 4♦5♦ on 8♠7♦Qc. Pot is 1000 and Flash bets 350; calling 350 would require ≈350/(1000+350)=25.9% equity to break even. My realistic by‑river equity to make a straight or flush is ~19.8% (≈16.5% to hit a 6 by the river for the straight + ≈4.2% to complete a two‑card diamond backdoor flush, overlap small) — well under the pot‑odds threshold. Raising as a semi‑bluff OOP with no pair and only backdoor outs is too risky versus a deep, aggressive button (I’d be bloating the pot without strong fold equity). Preflop I planned to call small c‑bets (≈≤250) and fold to larger ones; this 350 bet is above that. Fold and preserve stack for a better, more profitable spot.

3-handed on the button, first to act pre-flop facing the big blind. A3o is a standard opening hand in this spot: ace-high with a blocker, decent playability post-flop for top pair or draws. Raising to 3x the big blind (300) is standard to steal blinds or build the pot. Opponents have shown folding tendencies in past hands, and my stack (9700) allows flexibility. Limping is suboptimal, folding too tight for position and game flow to maximize stack over 150 hands.

I have 9♦6♠ in the small blind facing Elon’s raise to 300. Pot is 450 and I must call 250 (to 300) or reraiseto at least 500. 9‑6 offsuit is a weak, poorly-connected hand that will often be dominated by a button opening range and has very limited postflop playability out of position — especially if the big blind comes along and it becomes a multiway pot. Calling requires ~35.7% equity (250 into a 700 pot) which this hand is unlikely to have vs a realistic open range, and 3‑bet bluffing is unattractive here (poor blockers, bb can still call, risk of getting 4‑bet). With a healthy ~42.5bb stack I’d rather conserve chips for better spots, so I fold and surrender the small blind.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Seven of hearts. Elon raised to 300 from the Button, and Minni folded. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450. In a 3-handed game, Q7s is a standard defending hand against a Button opening range. I am getting roughly 2.25:1 odds on a call and my hand has good playability and suit connectivity to see a flop, despite being out of position.

On the flop of 7d-8h-Kh, I have middle pair (sevens) and a backdoor flush draw with the Queen of hearts. Elon, the pre-flop raiser, is likely to continue on this board quite frequently. My hand is currently strong enough to check-call a standard continuation bet to see how the turn develops. I will start by checking to the aggressor.

Flop is K♥8♥7♦, a king-high board with a backdoor flush draw and straight draws possible (e.g., 56, 9T). My A♠3♣ is ace-high with no pair or draw, but the ace blocker reduces villain's AK/AQ combos. Heads-up in position after villain checks the big blind defense range, which is wide. Checking behind realizes equity passively but misses value from worse hands (e.g., dominated Ax, gutshots) and fold equity from draws/bluffs. A small continuation bet maintains range balance, exploits position, and applies pressure on villain's likely weak checking range (no strong K, sets unlikely to check). Bet sizing ~60% pot (400) builds the pot modestly while leaving room for turns/rivers, aligning with aggressive play from history to maximize stack long-term.

On a flop of 7d-8h-Kh, I have middle pair (Sevens) with a Queen kicker and a backdoor heart flush draw. Elon's 400 continuation bet into a 650 pot is a standard sizing that he would use with both value hands (like Kings or better) and bluffs/semi-bluffs. My hand is too strong to fold at this point, as it beats all of his air and many of his semi-bluffs. I will call to see the turn and re-evaluate based on the next card and his subsequent action.

On the turn, an Ace appears, which is a card that significantly favors the pre-flop aggressor's (Elon's) range. I currently have third pair with a weak kicker and no real draw. I will check to Elon to see how he reacts to the Ace. If he bets, I will likely have to fold given the board texture and how well it hits his likely holdings (Ax, Kx, or big draws).

Turn board pairs the Ace, giving me top pair top kicker (Aces with 3♠ kicker) on a coordinated board (K♥8♥7♦A♦) with heart flush draw completed potential and straight draws possible (e.g., 65, 96, T9). Flash's check-check line suggests weakness: likely missed Kx (KQ, KJ unlikely to flat flop c-bet), weaker Ax (A9-A5), flush draws that didn't hit perfectly, or floats. My preflop raise and flop c-bet polarizes my range, and this bet continues value from my strong Ax range while protecting against draws. Checking induces bluffs but realizes equity too passively; villain's deep stack (25k) and history of calling then folding (e.g., Hands 16,18,39) suggest betting extracts value from worse (ATs, K9s, draws) and denies equity. Size ~2/3 pot (1000 into 1450) balances value/protection, builds pot for river, exploits position, aligns with aggressive image from history (e.g., Hands 13,15,38). Stack (9k) supports without overcommitting.

The Ace of Diamonds on the turn is a very unfavorable card for my hand, as it significantly strengthens the Button's perceived range (which contains many Ax and Kx combinations). After calling the flop bet with middle pair, my hand has now been demoted to third pair. Elon's bet of 1000 into a 1450 pot represents significant strength. Given the board texture and my weak relative hand strength, I do not have sufficient equity to continue calling. Folding is the most disciplined and correct play here to preserve my stack.

Button with J♠4♦ is a weak holding, but I have position and a decent stack (≈42BB). This is a prime spot to attempt a steal rather than limp/fold. I’ll open to a standard 3x BB (300) to apply pressure to the blinds and take the initiative postflop. If I face a sizeable 3-bet I’ll fold this hand (it’s dominated and not worth getting involved out of position); if called I’ll play aggressively in position — c-bet on dry boards, check/control on dangerous boards, and fold to strong resistance without improvement.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Nine suited (Qd 9d). The Button (Minni) has raised to 3x the big blind. Minni is relatively short-stacked with just under 4,000 chips. While Q9s is a strong hand, calling is often preferable to 3-betting in this spot to keep the pot manageable and see a flop with a hand that has good playability. If I 3-bet, I might face a shove from Minni's shorter stack, which would force me to fold a hand with significant equity. Calling allows me to play the hand post-flop and provides great pot odds.

Preflop in BB with TsJc, suited connectors with good playability. Facing a button raise to 300 from Minni (loose aggressive raiser from history) and a SB call from Flash (deeper stack). Pot is 700, calling 200 more (already posted 100 BB), pot odds ~3.5:1 (22.2% equity needed). TJ suited has ~30-40% equity multiway against typical button opening range and calling range, plus strong implied odds postflop with straight/flush potential. No fold equity with raise, and 3-bet too strong for this hand. Positionally OOP but multiway pots favor speculative hands like this. Effective SPR post-call ~25 with Minni's 3950 stack, good for realization. Call to see flop.

I have Queen-Nine of diamonds on a King-Eight-Seven board with two hearts. I have no direct pair or draw, only a backdoor straight opportunity. Being first to act out of position, checking is the standard play here. I want to see how the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) and the Big Blind (Elon) react to this board before committing more chips with a weak hand.

Flop of 7h8hKc completely misses my TsJc. No pair, no flush draw, only a weak gutshot to the 9 (4 outs) for a straight. OOP in a 3-way pot of 900, Flash has checked but Minni (aggressive button) likely to bet frequently with position and backdoor draws or made hands. Bluffing here multiway is low EV with poor fold equity against loose callers like Flash and Minni's range. No value, weak implied odds. Check and plan to fold to a bet, preserving stack for better spots.

I opened from the button preflop but this flop (7♥8♥K♣) misses J♠4♦ entirely: no pair, no hearts, almost no clean equity. Both opponents checked, and this is a multiway pot, so my fold equity is low — a c-bet will be called by most pairs and draws and I’ll be thin to continue if raised. The board is somewhat wet (two hearts, connected small cards) so I should control the pot and preserve my ~39–40BB stack for better opportunities. I’ll check and see a turn; only continue aggressively if I pick up a real piece (pair, good draw) or face very weak action to exploit.

The turn pairs the seven on a board of 7h-8h-Kc-7s. I currently hold Queen-high (Q9d) with no direct draw or pair. Since the flop checked through, it's possible everyone missed this board or someone is playing a marginal hand slowly. However, without a hand or a strong draw, I have no reason to lead out and bloat the pot, especially with a short stack like Minni still in the hand. I will check and see how the other players react.

Turn board pairs the 7, creating trips possible for hands that hit the flop, and the board remains scary with hearts flush draw, king high, and now paired low cards. My TsJc still completely misses: no pair, backdoor spade flush draw at best but irrelevant, and gutshot to 9 remains weak (4 outs). Multiway pot with Flash checking again (loose passive tendencies from history) and Minni who checked flop but has position. No value to betting a bluff here as ranges are wide, fold equity low multiway against Flash's calling station style and Minni's aggression. Preserve stack, check to pot control and realize any minimal equity at showdown or induce bluffs on river. Folding not possible as checked to us.

I missed: J♠4♦ has no pair and no heart draw on 7♥ 8♥ K♣ 7♠. This is a three‑way pot and the turn paired the board, which significantly reduces my fold equity (opponents can have K, 7, 8, or various draws). Both opponents checked flop and turn — that can indicate weakness but also can be a trap (slowplayed sets) or I’ll simply be called by any decent pair or draw. With ~39–40BB effective and a 900 chip pot, I don’t want to bluff into two players on a paired/wet board with no equity. Plan is to control risk: check and fold to meaningful aggression, re‑evaluate on the river only if I pick up real equity or face a very small bet I can exploit.

The board is 7h-8h-Kc-7s-3c and the action has been checked through on both the flop and the turn. I hold Queen-high (Q9d). While it's possible everyone has missed, it's highly likely that at least one opponent has a small pair or Ace-high which beats me. Since the pot is small and everyone has shown weakness, checking is the most prudent play to reach a showdown for free. If I bet, I'm unlikely to get better hands to fold and I'm rarely getting called by worse.

River board completes with 3c, pairing nothing relevant for me. TsJc remains total air: no pair, no completed draw. Multiway pot checked all the way around, indicating capped ranges but still villain ranges include weak made hands, flush draws that missed, king-high, or bluffs. Bluffing here multiway on river offers poor fold equity; Flash (loose passive/calling station from history) unlikely to fold showdown value, Minni (aggressive but positionally aware) can call/raise wide. No value, no blockers to nuts (e.g., no Kh, no heart blockers), low EV bluff. Check to realize any minimal showdown equity (high card jack) or induce a bet we can fold to cheaply, preserving stack for better spots. Goal is long-term stack maximization over 150 hands.

I missed this board with J♠4♦ — no pair, no heart blocker. This is a three‑way pot and both opponents checked flop and turn, which reduces my fold equity and also leaves plenty of hands (Kx, 7x, 8x, slowplayed sets) that will call a bluff. The turn paired the board (7s) and the river 3c didn't change that dynamic; betting into two players on this paired/wet board with no real equity is a low‑EV play. Pot is 900 while I have ~39–40 BB (3950), so preserving chips for better spots is preferable. I'll check and fold to meaningful aggression rather than bluff into two opponents.

I am on the button with 92s. While this is a 3-handed game and the button should open a wide range of hands, 92s is near the bottom of all starting hands. Given that Minni is quite short-stacked and may be looking for an opportunity to shove, I prefer to fold this weak hand and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

Preflop in SB vs BB (Flash folded). Held ATo offsuit, a strong hand warranting a raise for initiative and dead money. Standard sizing 3x BB (300 total) given blinds 50/100 and Min Raise To 200. Minni's 38.5BB stack can call/shove ranges we beat; postflop position advantage if called. Folding or limping suboptimal with this hand in position advantage spot. Aggressive play aligns with past successful opens.

Facing Elon's 3BB open to 300 from the small blind, I'm in the big blind with KcTh and 38.5 BB effective. To call is 200 more (pot 400), so calling gives me ~33% immediate equity — KTo is a playable hand heads-up and performs well versus a wide SB opening range. I'm slightly out of position postflop (SB acts last), so I don't want to bloat the pot with a marginal 3-bet; a raise would commit more stack and force tough decisions out of position. Calling keeps pot size manageable, realizes equity, and allows me to proceed postflop with plans: play aggressively when I pair a K/T or get strong draws, otherwise control the pot and fold to heavy pressure. I will call the 300 to see the flop.

Preflop: Raised ATo oop as SB for initiative with dead money and position advantage postflop if called. Flop: Ad Qs 3s gives me TPTK (As Tc), very strong hand. Board has spade flush draw and possible straight draws (e.g., KT, JTs), so bet for value against worse Aces, Queens, and to charge/protect against draws. OOP heads-up vs BB caller, but hand strong enough to c-bet 2/3 pot (400) for value/thin value and denial. Minni's BB calling range includes Ax, Qx, suited broadways/connectors we dominate or have equity vs. Bet sizing leaves her room to continue profitably with worse.

Board Ad Qs 3s, Elon (SB) c-bets 400 into a 1000 pot. I hold KcTh — no pair but decent equity to improve: Ks and Ts to pair plus four Js to make Broadway (about 10 total meaningful outs to improve to at least a pair/straight; ~38% to hit by river; J-only straight ~16.5%). Calling 400 here (final pot ~1800) requires roughly 22% equity to break even. Elon’s small c-bet from the SB suggests a fairly wide continuation range, so calling to realize equity and see one more card is the +EV line. I’m out of position, so I won’t bloat the pot with a bluff-raise; I will call the flop, re-evaluate on the turn (press for value if I pair or make the straight, fold to heavy continued aggression if I miss).

Turn 8s completes many spade flushes from villain's BB calling range (spade flush draws were prominent on flop with Qs3s). However, my AsTc gives top pair top kicker (paired Ad) + nut flush draw (As Qs 3s 8s, 9 outs to nuts ~20% equity even vs made flush). Blocks villain's nut flush combos. Villain's range for calling preflop raise + flop cbet: Ax (dominated), Qx pairs, two-pair, sets rare, spade draws (now flushes or strong draws). OOP with pot 1400 and villain's stack 3250 (SPR ~2.3), jamming extracts max value from worse Ax/Qx/lesser flushes/draws while denying equity. History shows Minni calls light (e.g., Hand 1 massive pot, Hand 20, Hand 61). Betting smaller risks check-behind or facing raise; shove unexploitable with our equity (~60%+ vs calling range). Goal: maximize stack long-term by realizing huge EV spot.

I’m facing a huge turn raise from Elon: pot 4,650 and he’s putting me to 3,250 (my entire remaining stack). That requires ~3,250 / (4,650 + 3,250) = ~41.1% equity to justify a call. I hold KcTh on Ad Qs 3s 8s — no pair and no spade. My clean outs are limited: 3 kings + 3 tens + 4 jacks = ~10 outs, ≈ 10/46 = 21.7% to improve on the river. Even allowing for some additional chop/wins when he’s bluffing, my total real equity versus a large, polarized raising range is far below the ~41% needed. Elon’s line (SB open, small flop c-bet, then a very large turn raise on the third spade) strongly suggests value hands — sets, two-pair (A8), or made spade flushes — and his history at the table shows he’s willing to put big money in with real value. I’m out of position and would be committing my entire stack with a hand that’s usually a clear underdog. Calling (or jamming) here is -EV; I’ll preserve my stack and fold.

3-handed on the button with 4h5h, a suited connector with good playability and position. Standard to open-raise from button with this hand against short SB and deep BB. History shows consistent 300 opens pre-flop. Minni short at 32bb may shove light, but hand has good equity and implied odds vs calling ranges. Raise to standard 300 (3x BB).

Facing Elon's button open to 300 with the big blind already in, this is a multiway pot and I'm out of position. 6♣7♣ is a suited connector with decent flop play and implied odds — for a small additional investment (250 chips, ~8% of my 3,200 stack) it's correct to see a flop rather than fold or 3-bet. I won't inflate the pot with a raise given my speculative holding and two deeper stacks behind me; plan is to proceed cautiously postflop (continue if I hit a strong pair/draw, otherwise exercise pot control and fold to heavy pressure).

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Seven offsuit. The Button raised to 3 and the Small Blind called. The pot is offering me very good odds (3.5 to 1) to see a flop. While A7o isn't a premium hand, it performs well enough in a three-handed dynamic, especially when the Small Blind is quite short-stacked (less than 30 big blinds). I will call to see the flop and play cautiously post-flop.

I check. I don’t have a made hand yet, but I have a very strong drawing hand: 6♣7♣ on K♣ J♦ 8♣ gives me a four‑to‑a‑flush plus an open‑ended straight draw. Unique outs ≈ 9 clubs + 3 non‑club fives + 3 non‑club nines = 15 outs (≈15/47 ≈ 32% to hit the turn, ≈54% by the river). This is a multiway pot and I’m out of position against two deeper stacks, so my fold equity is limited and I want to avoid bloating the pot OOP with only a draw. Checking preserves pot control and lets me realize equity for free; if someone bets a small sizing on the turn I’ll likely call to continue with the draw, but I’ll fold to large pressure that prices me poorly or looks like I’m behind to a made K/club ace combination. If checked to me on the turn/river and I improve, I’ll take a more aggressive line.

On this 8c Jd Kc board, my Ace-high is quite weak, and the board texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range (Elon) as well as Minni's calling range. Since I have no draws and limited showdown value, I will check and likely fold to any significant aggression from my opponents.

3-handed on button with position after check-check. 4h5h is air but my button opening range hits this board decently with broadways and AxK. Opponents defend wide pre but checking suggests no top pair or strong draw (KcJc8d board has flush/straight draw potential). Standard c-bet to deny equity/realize fold equity, deny draws free card. Minni short (~30bb) may shove but hand has some fold equity. Bet ~2/3 pot for value from medium strength and protection.

Board K♣ J♦ 8♣; I hold 6♣7♣ — a strong drawing hand: four‑to‑a‑flush plus an open‑ended straight draw. Unique outs ≈ 15 (≈32% to hit on the turn, ≈54% by river). Pot is 1500 and Elon bets 600 (pot becomes 2100). Calling 600 requires ~22% equity to break even (600/(2100+600)=22%), so my draw is +EV to continue. I’m out of position and this is a multiway pot — raising OOP with a draw here is thin (limited fold equity, likely called or 3‑bet by the deeper stacks), so I don’t want to inflate the pot unnecessarily. I’ll call to realize my equity, preserve stack (2950 -> 2350 if called), and re‑evaluate on the turn: be aggressive if I complete a strong hand (flush/straight/pair with good kicker), fold to large raises or shove pressure that prices me off, and call small/medium turn bets to continue with my drawing equity.

On a board of 8c-Jd-Kc, my Ace-high has very little value and no significant draws. Elon's 600 bet and Minni's call indicate that at least one, and likely both, of them have connected with this board. Given that this board texture strongly favors typical opening and calling ranges, I am significantly behind and have no reason to continue.

Check. The turn (6♠) paired my 6 and converted my flop-only draw into a made pair plus a live 4‑to‑a‑flush (I have 6♣7♣, board K♣ J♦ 8♣ 6♠ → 9 club outs to hit a flush on the river, ~9/46 ≈ 19.6%). Pot is 2,100 and my effective stack is 2,350; I'm out of position. Elon’s flop lead polarized toward Kx/top pairs and continuation bluffs, so betting OOP here would bloat the pot when many of his value hands (Kx, K♣) still beat me. Checking controls the pot, lets me realize equity for a modest cost, and keeps weaker hands (and bluffs) in that I can extract value from or call down. Plan if Elon bets: call small-to-medium bets (reasonable pot‑odds and my pair+draw justify calls — e.g. bets in the ~600–1,000 range), be cautious or fold to large pot‑sized overbets (>~1,200) that demand equity I likely don’t have, and re-evaluate on the river (bet for value if I hit the flush or a safe showdown value line if he’s unlikely to fold).

Turn board 6s adds a spade, completing no immediate draws but improving some straight draws (e.g., 4-5-7 now has straight outs, 9-10 broadway still live). Board remains drawy with clubs prominent. My 4h5h is pure air: no pair, no flush draw, no straight draw (gutshot at best unlikely). Minni checked flop and turn, called flop c-bet into a pot where my button range is strong (KQ, KJ, K8s, JTs, sets). Her twice-checking suggests weakness: no top pair likely, perhaps middle pair (J8, 88), stubborn draws, or floats. With 2350 stack (~23bb effective, ~1k behind after contributions), position, and history of folding to aggression (e.g., Hand 13, 20, 26), this is a prime spot for a second barrel bluff. My range credibly has KT, QT, AJ suited, two-pair combos. Bet 1400 (~2/3 pot) polarizes range, gets value from worse Ax/draws, folds out Jx/88/99, denies equity. If shoved, easy fold as stacks committed minimally. Checking risks giving free card or facing river bet; bet maximizes fold equity and pressure short stack.

Elon has shown aggression (button open, small flop C‑bet, then a larger turn bet). Pot is 3,500 and I must call 1,400 (pot odds = 1,400 / (3,500+1,400) ≈ 28.6%). My hand (6♣7♣) on K♣ J♦ 8♣ 6♠ is a made pair (sixes) plus a four‑card club draw. Unique clean river outs ≈ 9 clubs + 3 sevens + 2 sixes = 14 outs → 14/46 ≈ 30.4% to improve to flush/two‑pair/trips. That equity slightly exceeds the break‑even threshold, so a call is +EV here. Raising to the all‑in min (2,350) is thin OOP: Elon has a deep stack and is likely to call with a lot of his value range (Kx, Jx, etc.), so my fold equity is limited and I’d be committing my stack with only a marginal advantage. Folding surrenders ~3,500 chips that I have good equity to win. Plan: call the 1,400 to realize equity, leave myself ~950 behind to navigate the river (bet for value if I complete the flush or get a clean two‑pair/trips; fold to heavy pot‑sized river pressure if I miss and he barrels).

Board K♣ J♦ 8♣ 6♠ 2♥ — I hold 6♣7♣: a pair of sixes (no flush). Pot is 4,900 and my remaining stack is 950. Elon opened, c-bet the flop and barreled the turn, then checked the river. That line is consistent with Kx/Jx or medium pairs that can pot-control on the river, or missed draws checking behind; in any case a large portion of his value range still beats my pair. I don’t have enough fold equity OOP to bluff profitably (betting my short stack all‑in is thin and likely called by better hands), and a small value bet would be asking hands that beat me to fold — unlikely. Checking to showdown preserves my stack and realizes the modest showdown equity of my hand. Therefore I check.

River 2h is a relatively blank card that doesn't complete any new obvious draws (hearts are clean with only one on board, no straight completes for common holdings). Board is Kc Jd 8c 6s 2h: possible clubs flush draw missed, broadway draws like Q10 bricked. Minni has checked flop, turn, and river after calling two barrels into a strong button range (KQsuited, KJ, K8s, JTs, sets, two-pair combos like K8, J8). Her range is capped: likely Jx (JT-QJ), 88-99 (overpairs crushed), stubborn Ax high, missed draws (Q10, club draws). With only 950 left (~20% pot), position, and history of folding to aggression (e.g., Hands 13, 20, 26, 34, 50), this is a mandatory thin value/bluff spot. Checking gives up on the pot we built through aggression and risks splitting if she has Jx. Betting ~full pot size (950) polarizes our range (nuts or air), denies her equity/showdown with medium strength, and maximizes fold equity against her weak calling range. If called, we lose 950 to a better hand occasionally, but EV+ due to frequent folds from her capped range. Her tiny stack makes pot odds terrible for hero calls.

Pot = 5,850, Elon bets 950. Calling 950 into a 5,850 pot means I need ~950/(5,850+950) ≈ 14% equity to break even. I hold 6♣7♣ on K♣ J♦ 8♣ 6♠ 2♥ — a made pair of sixes with no flush (I missed the river). Elon’s line (button open, flop c-bet, larger turn bet, small river bet) is value‑heavy (Kx, Jx, sets) but the tiny river sizing is consistent with thin value/blocker bets and some missed draws/bluffs. Even though many of his combos beat my pair, there are enough unpaired hands and lower pairs in his plausible river range that my low required equity is satisfied. Folding here would surrender a large pot with decent chance to win; I’m effectively getting very good pot odds to see showdown. I commit the rest of my stack and call the 950.

In a 3-handed game the button should open very wide. 6♠4♦ is a weak hand but is playable in position — better to take the initiative and try to steal the blinds than to limp and give everyone good odds. I’ll open to a standard 3x BB (300) which is in line with table norms: it preserves fold equity, denies the BB cheap odds, and keeps the pot manageable with a marginal holding. Plan: fold to a substantial 3-bet (I have poor equity vs a 3-bet range), but if called I’ll use positional advantage — c-bet frequently on dry, uncoordinated flops and exercise pot control or check/float on connected or high-card boards. This maximizes long-term EV while avoiding large confrontations with a weak hand.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Deuce offsuit. Against a standard button open from Minni, Ace-rag is a marginal hand to defend. Given the stack sizes and the 3-max format, I have a choice between folding, calling, or 3-betting. 3-betting is an option to isolate the button, but calling allows me to see a flop cheaply while maintaining my significant chip lead over the other players. I will call the additional 250 chips to see the flop.

In the big blind facing a button min-raise to 300 and a small blind flat call, the pot is 700 and I need to call 200 more for great implied odds (3.5:1). Q9o is a marginal but playable hand in this spot due to position multiway, decent high card value, and potential for straight/flush draws postflop. Folding is too tight given the price; 3-betting is unnecessary with this hand as it plays better multiway. Opponents' stacks allow for postflop maneuvering, and history shows Minni raises wide from button. Calling to realize equity and control pot size.

On a mid-range, connected board of Ten-Eight-Six, my Ace-high has very little value and I am out of position against two opponents. The board likely connects well with both the pre-flop raiser's range and the Big Blind's defending range. Betting into two players here with no draw and only one overcard would be an unnecessary risk. The standard play in the Small Blind is to check to the aggressor.

On the flop of 6d-8c-Ts with Qs9d, I have Queen-high with a gutshot straight draw to the Broadway (needing a Jack) and backdoor spade flush draw. The pot is 900, I've invested 500 total preflop (BB + call), and stacks are deep enough (my 7300, Minni 6500, Flash deep). Flash checked SB, so action to me before button Minni, who raised preflop from button (wide range possible). This hand has 8-11% equity to improve to a strong hand and blocks some of Minni's calling range. However, betting risks getting raised by better hands or draws, and checking keeps the pot controlled while retaining fold equity if Minni bets. Multiway history shows passive play postflop often, so checking induces bluffs or free cards. Preference to check and re-evaluate turn, realizing equity passively given the multiway pot and my vulnerable draw.

I opened preflop with 6♠4♦ and the flop comes 6♦‑8♣‑T♠ — I’ve got a small pair (6s) but two overcards and a somewhat connected/high‑card texture. Both opponents checked to me in a three‑way pot, so this is a board where my earlier plan was to exercise pot control / check‑float rather than barrel with a vulnerable one‑pair. Betting here would gain some fold equity but also risks a check‑raise or bloating the pot when I’m likely behind to tens/eights or overpairs; I prefer to see a turn for cheap and use position to call small bets or take the pot later. If I face a sizable bet/raise I’ll fold; if everyone checks the turn I’ll re‑evaluate and may make a small delayed c‑bet.

On the turn, an Ace hits the board, giving me Top Pair. Since the flop checked through among all three players, it is unlikely that anyone currently holds a powerhouse like a set or two-pair, as those would typically bet for protection or value on a connected board like 6-8-T. My Top Pair with a weak kicker is likely the best hand at the moment. I will bet 500 into the 900 pot to extract value from any Ten, middle pair, or straight draw (like 9-7 or 7-5) and to take control of the hand. If I am raised, I will have to proceed with caution given my poor kicker, but for now, betting for value is the most logical step.

Facing a 500 bet from Flash into a 1400 pot on the turn with board 6d-8c-Ts-Ah and holding Qs9d for Queen-high and a gutshot straight draw to the Jack (4 outs, ~8% equity to the river). Pot odds are about 2.8:1 requiring ~26% equity, which this hand lacks against Flash's SB donk-bet range after checking the flop. Flash's check-flop bet-turn likely represents Ax (especially weak Aces improving to top pair), sets, or strong draws that hit the Ace. My hand blocks some broadway but has shown down weak previously and unlikely to be good. Minni still to act multiway adds pressure. Folding preserves stack for better spots; calling risks stacks deep with poor implied odds given reverse implied with Q-high if I hit. History shows passive lines multiway, but this bet warrants folding marginal draw.

Flash leads a small turn bet (500 into a 1400 pot) after a flop of 6♦‑8♣‑T♠ that was checked through. I hold a small pair (6♠4♦) — vulnerable to A/T/8 and overpairs but with decent showdown value and good price to call. Pot odds: calling 500 to win ~2400 requires ~21% equity, which my pair can realize against a fair portion of his stab/bluff range. Raising is thin and can get me committed vs many hands that already beat me; folding surrenders too much equity for a cheap price. I’ll call 500 to control the pot, realize equity, and re‑evaluate on the river (fold to big value bets, consider calling/small raises only with improvements or clear bluffs).

I have Top Pair with the lowest possible kicker (Ace-Deuce). On the turn, I took the lead when the Ace appeared, and Minni called. The river 3 of clubs is a brick that shouldn't change the relative strength of our hands. Since Minni called my turn bet, she likely has an Ace or a strong Ten. Given my weak kicker, betting again is risky as I'm mostly called by hands that beat me (better kickers like A4-AK) and I might force out hands I'm beating (like a Ten). Checking is the best play here to reach a cheap showdown or to induce a bluff from missed straight draws like 9-7 or Q-J.

I check to showdown. I have a vulnerable one‑pair (6s) on 6♦‑8♣‑T♠‑A♥‑3♣. Flash led small on the turn (500 into ~1400) then checked the river — that line is consistent with medium‑strength value (Ax, T, 8) or a small probe/bluff/pot‑control. A river bet will only get value from very few worse hands (small pocket pairs or stubborn high cards) while risking a raise from hands that already beat me. Pot is 1,900 and I still have 6,000 behind; I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand. Checking preserves showdown equity and avoids committing more chips when I’m often behind, so I’ll check and see the showdown.

In a 3-handed game, King-Eight offsuit (K8o) is a strong enough hand to open from the button. It dominates many of the hands the blinds might defend with. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard opening size to put pressure on the shorter stacks in the blinds (Elon and Minni) and take control of the pot early.

A6o is a weak offsuit ace from the small blind facing a button raise. Button ranges are wide, but calling out of position commits us to tough postflop decisions with poor playability: weak kicker on ace highs, limited straight potential, and dominated by better aces. Stack sizes are deep (72bb), but folding preserves chips for better spots. No need to defend this wide OOP preflop.

Facing a button open to 300 from the big blind with 9♣2♦ — a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability and no useful blockers. It costs 200 to call into a 450 pot (breakeven equity ≈ 30.8%). Versus a button opening range I'm frequently dominated and will be out of position postflop, so calling is a marginal -EV play and a light 3‑bet bluff would be thin against this deep, aggressive opponent. Preserving my 5.9k stack is the priority here — fold.

Preflop on the button with K2o offsuit. This is a very weak hand with poor playability, low kicker, and offsuit. In a 3-handed game, button ranges are wide, but K2o is not in a profitable opening range due to its domination potential against calling ranges and lack of equity multiway. Facing blinds with no action yet, folding preserves stack for better spots. No need to bluff raise into potentially sticky opponents like Minni and deep-stacked Flash. Prioritize premium hands to build stack over 150 hands.

Heads-up vs the big blind with Qh6s. Pot is 150 and I only need to commit 50 to see a cheap flop, but in heads-up this hand is playable and belongs in my open-raise range. I want to take the initiative and apply pressure rather than limp and allow the BB to dictate terms. I’ll raise to the standard 2bb (200 total) to isolate and steal the blind; if Flash 3-bets I’ll fold this tucked Q6o, and if called I’ll proceed carefully postflop (c-bet favorable dry flops, check or pot-control on dangerous A/K/Q-heavy boards).

I am in the Big Blind with King-Trey offsuit. The Button folded and the Small Blind (Minni) put in a min-raise to 200. I need to call 100 more into a total pot of 300, giving me 4:1 pot odds. Additionally, I will have position throughout the rest of the hand. King-high hands are generally strong enough to defend the big blind against a small blind open in this situation.

I was the preflop raiser and this flop (7h Td 4c) is very dry — no obvious straight/flush and no A/K that would dominate my hand. I hold Qh6s: one clean overcard (Q) plus a backdoor heart. As the aggressor I should apply pressure to fold out small pairs, floats and unpaired hands from Flash's defending range. I’ll make a standard c-bet of ~50% pot to buy the pot now and retain fold equity. Plan: bet 200 to force folds; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (continue on a heart or Q, pot-control or check vs A/K or heavy action). If Flash check-raises large I will respect it and fold Q-high since my showdown value is limited.

In this blind-vs-blind battle, I have King-high on a Ten-high dry board (7h-Td-4c) after calling a pre-flop min-raise. Minni has led out for half the pot. While my range is wide, King-Trey offsuit is extremely weak here with no direct draws and very little backdoor potential. Minni has shown a tendency to follow up on her bets in previous hands, and continuing with just King-high out of position would likely lead to a difficult decision on the turn. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I'm on the Button with 7♦9♥ in a 3-handed pot. This hand has decent postflop playability in position and the table standard open size has been ~300, so an open-raise here is the most +EV play: it applies pressure to the blinds, preserves fold equity, and lets me play the flop in position. Pot is 150 and the call is 100; I'll raise to 300 (a normal 3x BB open). If the BB (Elon) 3-bets big I'll fold — his 3-bet range is often strong and I don't want to play a marginal offsuit hand out of position. If I'm called, I'll use position to c-bet favorable flops and control the pot in multiway spots. Raising to 300 is a small fraction of my 6100 stack and is the right aggressive, position-based play here.

I am in the Small Blind holding Queen-Six offsuit. The button (Minni) has raised to 3BB. Q6o is a weak hand that does not play well out of position, especially against a raise. While I am getting decent pot odds to call, the lack of playability and the risk of the Big Blind (Elon) squeezing makes this a standard fold in a professional strategy.

A7o is a strong defensive hand from the BB against a button raise, which is likely wide. Ace blocker reduces opponent's premium ace range, and the hand has good playability postflop with straight potential and dominates weaker aces. Pot odds are excellent (calling 200 into 450, ~31% equity needed), and villain's range is broad enough for positive EV call. Calling keeps the pot multiway potential low since SB folded, allows realizing equity in position postflop where I act first but can control pot size. No need to 3-bet as hand prefers set-mining or floating flops rather than polarizing. Folding too tight given dynamics and stacks.

Top pair with Ace kicker (TPAK) on a coordinated flop with heart flush draw and possible straight draws (e.g., 56, J9, 89). Minni's button opening range is wide, including Ax, Kx, suited connectors, pairs below 7s, and bluffs. Betting extracts value from worse hands (weaker Ax, 88-66, T9s, flush draws) and denies equity to draws/improvers. Checking risks giving free cards or facing a raise from villain's strong range. ~60% pot bet balances value and protection while keeping stack committed reasonable (6950 deep). History shows Minni calls frequently, supporting thin value bets. Avoids check-raise trap potential.

I have 7♦9♥ on 7♥ T♣ 4♥ — I paired the 7 (middle pair) and hold a heart blocker (9♥) on a two-heart flop. Elon led out for 400 into a 1050 pot; calling 400 gives me pot odds of ~27.6% (400 / (1050+400)). My pair is well ahead of a large portion of Elon's calling/c-bet range (missed overcards, many broadways) and has reasonable equity vs that range. A small raise to 800 is an option to deny equity, but it’s thin here — I’ll get called by better hands (T‑x, 7x, pocket pairs) and fold out only weaker holdings I already beat; it also bloats the pot out of position and risks a sharper 3-bet. I’ll call to control the pot, keep worse hands and bluffs in, use position to see the turn and re-evaluate.

Turn card Jh completes the nut flush draw (hearts) and improves straight draws like 89 to a straight (7-T-J with implied 8-9). Board is very draw-heavy: 7h-Tc-4h-Jh offers flushes, straights (89, potentially others), two-pair combos (J7, JT, J4, T4, 74). My Ad7s is top pair top kicker (pair of 7s with Ad), strong in my BB defending range which includes 7x, Ax suited/off, sets (77 blocked somewhat), overpairs. Flop bet/turn bet line represents strength well. Minni's button call on flop (vs BB c-bet) includes: weaker 7x (unlikely), Tx, 4x, Ax high, heart flush draws (Ahx, suited connectors hearts), gutshots (56, 89). History shows Minni calls frequently but folds bluffs sometimes (e.g., Hand 20 river). Checking OOP risks: free river card to draws/improvers, check-raise from made hands/draws, or missing value. Betting extracts from worse (Tx, Ax, missed draws turning into floats), protects vs overcards/river draws, denies equity. Size ~2/3 pot (1000 into 1450) balances value/protection without overcommitting (29bb bet, stacks 65bb/54bb). Blocks nut flush draws (Ad). Positive EV given her calling tendencies.

I hold 7♦9♥ — middle pair with a single heart on a 7♥ T♣ 4♥ J♥ board. Elon’s turn bet is 1,000 into ~1,450 (pot 2,450), so calling 1,000 to win a 3,450 pot requires roughly 29% equity. I still have reasonable equity vs a wide turn-barrel range (worse pairs, Tx, Jx, missed overcards and bluffs) and my 9♥ blocks some two‑heart flush combos, but the turn heart does enable made flushes (two hearts in his holecards) that dominate me. Raising here is thin — it bloats the pot out of position and will often be punished by a genuine flush or a check‑raise; folding forfeits good pot odds and the chance to extract value from worse. I’ll call 1,000 to keep the pot controlled, realize equity in position, and re-evaluate the river (fold to strong river action unless I improve to two‑pair/full house or hit the flush).

River Kc is a blank for my TPTK (Ad7s pairing 7h), but it improves Kx hands (KQ, KJ, KT for pairs or two pair with Tc) and possibly backdoor draws, while completing no new obvious draws (hearts flush open since flop, 89 straight made on turn). Board remains scary: 7h-Tc-4h-Jh-Kc allows straights (89), flushes (Ahx), sets (77/T/44/JJ/KK post-river), two pairs (JT/J4/T4/74/KT/KJ/K4 etc.). My double-barrel line (flop 400, turn 1000 into growing pot) polarizes me as strong (sets, two pair, straights/flushes) or bluffs (missed draws), but TPTK fits value portion. Minni's button raise-call-call range: strong made hands (sets, two pair, slowplayed premiums), hit draws (hearts Ahx blocked by my Ad somewhat, 89 straight), floats/overpairs that stuck around. History shows Minni calls bets frequently (e.g., Hands 20,28,34,61,63,70) but folds rivers sometimes. Pot 3450, my stack 5550 (she 4400). Value betting thin (1700-2000) risks calls from better (straights/sets/two pair Kx) and folds from worse (Ax, weaker pairs, missed draws turning to bluffs). Checking induces bluffs from missed draws (56, 89 folds turn?, weaker hearts), extracts thin value bets from Tx/4x/Ax/Kx worse, and showdowns beat bluffs/weak pairs. Free card impossible (river). EV+ to check given scary board and her capped range capping my value calls; rep strength sufficient from line. Avoids spew into superior hands.

I have middle pair (7♦) with a heart blocker (9♥) on 7♥ T♣ 4♥ J♥ K♣. Villain (Elon) called preflop, c‑bet the flop (400) and barreled the turn (1000) — then checked the river. That line is consistent with medium‑strength hands (T‑x, J‑x, K‑x) or some two‑heart combos that already made a flush; it also allows for missed draws or thin value hands checking Kc for pot control. Betting river for thin value is poor: most hands that will call beat my pair, and a bluff risks being raised or called by hands that still have outs or better. I block only single‑heart combos (I don’t block two‑heart flushes), so I don’t have the key blockers to credibly represent a nut heart flush. With no realistic way to extract value and a real risk of facing a raise, the lowest‑variance +EV play is to check back and take my showdown. If Elon bets after my check, I’ll evaluate sizing and my read, but I’m prepared to fold to large river aggression.

I am on the button with Ace-Ten of Diamonds, a very strong hand in a three-handed game. My stack is much larger than both opponents, particularly Minni who has only 43 big blinds left. Raising here allows me to take control of the pot and leverage my positional advantage against the shorter stacks in the blinds.

Preflop in SB facing a button raise to 300 (3x) from Flash, who opens wide from late position based on history. A9s is a strong 3-bet candidate: suited ace with good blockers (A blocks AA/AK), high equity vs calling range, and postflop playability despite OOP. Calling OOP is loose and gives up initiative; folding is too tight for this spot. Standard 3-bet size ~3x the raise to 900 isolates BB (short stack 4300) and pressures Flash's wide range. Pot will be ~1750 post-3bet, good for my stack (8950 effective with Flash).

Fold. Facing a preflop 3‑bet to 900 after the button opened to 300; pot is 1300 and I must call 800. My hand 9♦2♠ is extremely weak, dominated by almost any reasonable 3‑bet range from the small blind and has very poor playability out of position. Calling 800 requires ~38% equity (800/(1300+800)=38%) to break even — 9‑2o has far less vs a 3‑bet range. A raise/4‑bet with this hand would be pure bluffing and overcommits the stack; shoving is unnecessary and reckless. Preserve chips for better spots and fold preflop.

I have Ad Td on the button, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. Elon's 3-bet to 900 is consistent with his previous behavior, where he has shown a willingness to 3-bet both value hands (JJ, ATs) and bluffs (Q5s). Because I have position on Elon and a hand that plays very well post-flop, calling is the most effective play. It allows me to control the size of the pot, keep his bluffs in his range, and utilize my positional advantage on later streets without committing too much of my stack pre-flop.

Flop Tc3d6c is a favorable board for my Ac9s: I have the nut flush draw (Ac with two clubs on board), providing ~35-40% equity against Flash's 3-bet calling range from the button (which includes overpairs like JJ+, AK/AQ/AJ suited/off, suited broadways, suited connectors like JTs/98s/87s/76s that flop pairs/draws). The board is somewhat drawy (flush draw, gutshots like 54), but low and uncoordinated otherwise. As the preflop 3-bettor OOP, checking risks giving Flash free cards or check-raise spots, while a c-bet applies pressure, denies equity to his floats/draws, builds the pot when ahead vs worse Ax/pairs, and leverages fold equity (~30-40% folds from his range). Pot 1900, effective stacks good for semi-bluff. Sizing 1300 (~2/3 pot) balances value (from T9/Ax/66 etc.) and bluff, keeps pot manageable OOP, and sets up turn play. Flash deep (25k+), but history shows he folds to aggression often (e.g., Hand 51,67). Avoid check to maintain initiative.

I have top pair, top kicker on a relatively dry board of Tc-3d-6c. After the pre-flop 3-bet sequence, Elon's 1300 lead on the flop is a standard continuation bet. With my stack and the current pot size, the stack-to-pot ratio is low (around 1.7), making this a high-commitment situation. However, calling is preferable to raising at this stage because it keeps Elon's bluffs (like AK, AQ, or club draws) in his range and allows me to utilize my position. Raising would likely only get action from hands that beat me (sets or overpairs) or other strong Tens that I currently dominate. I will call and evaluate the turn.

Turn Jd is a reasonably neutral card for my range but bricks my hand—no pair, no new draws, still holding the nut club flush draw (~9 outs, ~18% equity heads-up). Board Tc3d6cJd is drawy (clubs, diamonds now, gutshots, open-enders like 45/T9 now straight), favoring my perceived strong 3-bet/c-bet range (sets, two-pair, overpairs, combo draws). Flash's flop call range: pairs (TT+/66/76/Tx suited), weaker Ax/club draws (KQc/JTc etc. worse than mine), floats, some straight draws. He likely floats/turns weak pairs/draws often from button in 3-bet pots per history (folds to aggression postflop frequently, e.g., H43,51,67,69). Pot 4500, my stack 6800 (SPR ~1.5); checking risks giving a free river card to his equity hands or check-raise all-in, while betting maximizes fold equity (~40-50% vs his range) and gets called by worse (inferior flush draws like QJc/KQc/J9c, smaller pairs). Shoving 6800 builds max pot when ahead (~55% equity vs calling range weighted to medium strength/draws), denies equity, and low SPR makes semi-bluff optimal—no merit to smaller bet (he shoves/raises strong, calls medium, I still OOP). History supports aggression works vs Flash (deep but folds rivers/turns to bets). Check-fold or check-call suboptimal OOP with monster draw.

I have second pair (Tens) with an Ace kicker, and the turn card (Jack of Diamonds) has given me the nut flush draw. Elon has moved all-in for 6,800. The pot is currently 11,300, and with his bet, it becomes 18,100. I need to call 6,800 to win a total pot of 24,900, meaning I need approximately 27.3% equity to break even. I have 9 outs to the nut flush (about 20.5% equity) and 2 outs for three-of-a-kind (another 4.5%), giving me roughly 25% equity against a hand like AJ or KJ. Considering Elon's aggressive history and the possibility that he is semi-bluffing with a club draw or a smaller pair, my combined equity and the pot odds make this a mathematically sound call.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with Ah2s — a strong heads-up hand. Pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to call, but against one opponent I want the initiative and position postflop. Raise to 2x the big blind (200 total) to get fold equity, define Flash’s wide defending range, and keep enough effective stacks (I have ~42.5bb) to play postflop. If he 3-bets small I’ll consider calling and playing in position; I’ll fold to large 3-bets that make continuing unprofitable. If called, plan to c-bet many dry flops and proceed cautiously on Ace/paired boards.

I am in the Big Blind with 8-5 offsuit. Minni, who is playing with a relatively short stack of 4100 chips (41 BB), has min-raised from the Small Blind/Button. While the pot odds are 3:1 to call (100 more to win 300), 8-5 offsuit is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially against a short stack that is likely to be aggressive. I have a significant chip lead overall and can afford to fold marginal hands and wait for better spots to exploit my opponent's stack size.
K9o is a strong hand to open from the button in a short-handed game. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the big blind and take control of the hand pre-flop.

Heads-up vs a button opener to 300 with 6c4h: this is a marginal offsuit hand but calling only costs 200 (total commitment 300), which is cheap versus my 4,300 stack and the 400 pot. Flash’s opening range from the button will be wide, so 6‑4o has some equity and can flop disguised pairs/straights. I won’t 3‑bet out of position with this weak hand, so I’ll call to see a flop and play cautiously OOP — folding to strong continuation bets on dry boards and only continuing with made hands or strong draws. Calling preserves stack and keeps post‑flop options open.

Check. I have no pair and the Ace on board heavily favors the opener’s range, but I do have a disguised straight draw (3-4-5-6) with 8 outs to a straight and a tiny backdoor heart possibility. Pot is 600 and my stack is 4,100 — I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand. Heads‑up the button will c‑bet a wide range, so checking gives me the option to call a small continuation bet (e.g. half‑pot) where my ~31% equity to river justifies a call, see a free turn if he checks, and fold to large barrels. I’ll check and evaluate turn action based on sizing and whether I pick up real equity or a cheap showdown.

I am on the button with K9o. I raised pre-flop and Minni called from the big blind. The flop is A-5-3 rainbow. Minni checked to me. While I missed the flop, this is a board that favors my pre-flop raising range more than Minni's defending range. I will place a continuation bet to represent the Ace and try to take the pot down immediately. Minni's stack is quite short (4100), so I need to be careful about committing too much if they show resistance, but a standard 1/2 pot bet should be effective here.

Call 300. Flash’s flop c-bet is small (~1/3 pot) and I have a clean open‑ended straight draw (4 and 6 with 3‑5 on board = 8 outs, ~17% to hit on the turn, ~31% to river) plus a backdoor heart possibility. Calling 300 into the ~900 pot gives me ~25% pot odds, so calling is +EV and cheap relative to my 4,100 stack. Check‑raising OOP with only draw equity would bloat the pot and is unnecessary; folding would be too tight given the odds. Plan: see the turn — fold to a large turn barrel, call small/medium turn bets to realize equity, and only escalate if I hit or the action/reads justify a bluff.

I’ve just completed a 6-high straight on the turn (2-3-4-5-6 with my 6♣4♥). Heads‑up this is an extremely strong hand: it currently beats single A‑pairs and pocket pairs; only a river that pairs the board and gives my opponent a full house/quads can beat me, and that’s relatively unlikely. Flash opened wide and c‑bet small on the flop, then checked the turn — a line consistent with pot control/weak Ax or marginal holdings. Betting for value here is the right play: it extracts calls from worse A‑x, single pocket pairs and other hands that will often call a half‑pot, while denying a free river that could turn into a full house for a few narrow combos. I’ll size to about half the pot to get called by worse but keep the pot manageable relative to my 3,800 stack. If Flash check‑raises heavily I’ll re‑evaluate (a large turn check‑raise is usually very strong and could represent sets/full houses), but a standard call or small raise I’ll continue against. Bet 600 into 1200 to build value and protect my equity.

I have King-high with no draw on an Ace-high board. I attempted a continuation bet on the flop which Minni called, suggesting she has a piece of this board (likely an Ace or a pair). Her lead on the turn for 600 into 1200 indicates strength or a potential straight with a 4. Given her short stack of 3200, she is likely committed to the hand, and bluffing her off a pair of Aces or even a 5 is unlikely to work. I have zero equity here, so I will fold and preserve my chips.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with T7s — good postflop playability and flush/straight potential. Against a single big blind I should be opening wide to take advantage of position and apply pressure; limping/calling hands like this is passive and allows the big blind to dictate the action. A standard 3x BB open (300 total) gives solid fold equity while keeping the pot manageable with 49.5BB effective. Plan: c-bet most favorable flops, control pot size on junky boards, fold to clear heavy 3-bet aggression if I lack equity. Raise to 300 to play the hand in position and maximize EV.

I am in the Big Blind with Jc 7d. Minni, who has a short stack of 4700, raised to 300 from the Small Blind. The pot is currently 400, and it costs me 200 more to call. I am getting 2-to-1 odds on the call. While J7o is a marginal hand, against a wide opening range from the button/small blind, it is strong enough to defend, especially considering the pot odds and the potential to outplay or outdraw Minni on the flop.

I have middle pair (Jacks) on an Ace-high flop. In heads-up play as the Big Blind, I should check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they react to this board. Betting into the Ace might force out hands I beat while getting called by better, so checking is the most standard and flexible play here.

Heads-up on the button after my 3x open; I hold Tc7c — no pair but some backdoor club/runner-runner equity and a ten that can pair. The flop Ac Js 5d is an ‘ace-high’ board that hits the caller’s range (Ax, broadways, pocket pairs) often, but Flash checked, which is exploitable. As the preflop aggressor I should c-bet a reasonably small size to fold out many hands that missed and to keep the pot manageable when I have little showdown equity. I’ll make a one-third pot continuation bet (200 into 600). If I face a significant check-raise I’ll give it up — I have too little equity to continue vs big aggression. If called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn: continue/raise only if I improve or the turn gives a good bluffing story; otherwise check back and control the pot.

I have middle pair (Jacks) on an Ace-high flop. The bet size from Minni is small (1/3 of the pot), and I am getting 4-to-1 pot odds to continue. While Minni could have an Ace, her range also includes many bluffs, draws, or weaker pairs. Raising is unnecessary and would likely only get called by better hands. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable and see how the board develops on the turn.

On the turn, the board pairs with a second five, which is mostly a blank card for both ranges unless one of us has a five. I still have middle pair with Jacks. Since I checked and called a small bet on the flop, continuing with a check on the turn is the most appropriate play to control the pot size and observe Minni's next move. This allows me to reach showdown or evaluate her bet size if she continues her aggression.

Turn pairs the board (Ac Js 5d 5h) and my Tc7c has missed — my backdoor club flush opportunity is dead once the turn was a heart, leaving only a one-card chance to pair on the river. Flash called my small flop c-bet and now checks; that range includes Ax, Jx, pocket pairs and the occasional slowplayed 5. Betting the turn has very poor fold equity and risks getting called or check‑raised by hands that dominate me, so I should control the pot and preserve stack. I’ll check back, see a free river, and only bluff or bet if the river gives a believable story or I actually improve (pair/pair+).

On the river, the board is Ac-Js-5d-5h-Kh. I have a pair of Jacks with a weak 7 kicker. Minni's check on the turn suggests she likely doesn't have a strong Ace or a set. However, the King on the river is an overcard that could have helped her range (like Kx suited). My hand has decent showdown value against her missed draws or smaller pocket pairs, but it is not strong enough to bet for value, as I'm unlikely to be called by many worse hands. Checking allows me to see a showdown or evaluate if she decides to bluff.

I missed — Tc7c has no pair or flush on the river (Ac Js 5d 5h Kh). I was the preflop aggressor and made a small flop c-bet that was called; both players checked the turn and the opponent checked the river, showing weakness or pot control. The King on the river gives me a credible bluff story (I could credibly have Kx) and a small bet can fold out missed broadways and some Ax/Jx hands that are unwilling to call a river stab. Pot is 1000; a bet of 300 (~1/3 pot) buys good fold equity while keeping my risk limited — the break‑even fold frequency is only ~23% for this size. If I get called I will fold at showdown (I have no showdown value). A small river bluff here maximizes my chance to take down the pot without risking much of my 4500 stack.

On the river, the board is Ac-Js-5d-5h-Kh. I have a pair of Jacks with a weak 7 kicker. Minni's check on the turn strongly suggests she does not have a strong Ace or a set. While the King on the river is an overcard, her small bet of 300 into a 1000 pot (offering me 4.33-to-1 odds) looks like either a thin value bet with a hand I might beat (like a lower pair) or a cheap bluff with a missed draw. Given the excellent pot odds and the fact that I have second pair on a board where the aggressor slowed down on the turn, I have a clear call.

I am in the Small Blind/Button in a heads-up situation against Minni, who has a relatively short stack of 4,100 chips (41 big blinds). My hand, Ten-Deuce offsuit (T2o), is one of the weakest possible starting hands. While it is common to play a wide range of hands from the button in heads-up play, T2o falls into the bottom 15% of hands and is generally a standard fold to avoid playing out of position or committing chips with a marginal hand against an opponent who might be looking for an opportunity to shove or play aggressively with their remaining stack.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) I should be opening very wide — 4♣2♥ is garbage but still fine to raise in position to steal the BB and take the initiative. A min-raise to 200 total is standard heads-up sizing (2x BB) — it preserves fold equity, keeps the pot small with my shortish 42BB stack, and allows me to play a hand in position if Flash calls. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold 4‑2o; if he calls I’ll c-bet favorable flops and exercise pot control on bad boards. Raising is +EV here versus folding or limp-calling. I will raise to 200 total.

I hold Ad 9h in the Big Blind. Minni, on the button, has raised to 2x (200 total). With only two active players and Minni's relatively short stack (40.5 BB), Ace-Nine offsuit is a strong hand that is well ahead of a typical button opening range. I will 3-bet to 600 to build the pot and take the initiative, as this hand performs well in a head-up situation and can either win the pot immediately or be in a strong position on many flops.

I opened wide to 200 in position as a heads‑up steal, but Flash 3‑bet to 600. 4♣2♥ has almost no equity vs a standard 3‑bet range and will be dominated on most flops. Calling an extra 400 (600 total) just bloats the pot with a very weak hand; a small 4‑bet to 1000 would be a thin bluff and commits too much out of line. With ~40BB effective and a deep, aggressive opponent, preserve the stack and wait for a better spot. Folding is the best +EV play here.

I am on the button with Qd 2d. This is a suited hand with some playability, although the deuce is weak. My opponent, Minni, is in the Big Blind with a relatively short stack (3,950 compared to my 35,900). Raising to put pressure on the short stack is a standard strategy, especially with suited connectors or gappers. A standard 3x raise is 300.

Heads-up vs Flash (button) who opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with T9o and effective stack ~39.5bb (3950). T9o is a very playable heads‑up hand with good equity and straight/connected potential. The pot is 400 and I only need to put in 200 more (300 total committed) — roughly 3:1 immediate pot odds — so calling is correct. 3‑betting to 500+ is possible, but OOP with a marginal hand against a deep, wide‑opening opponent is thin and bloats the pot unnecessarily. I’ll call to see a flop, keep the pot manageable, and play postflop according to texture: pursue straights/top pairs and fold to heavy action when I miss.

Check. On a Q♠ Q♣ Q♥ flop with T♥9♦ I have no pair, no backdoor draws and essentially zero realistic equity — only one queen remains (Q♦) so villain holding a queen is rare (~4%). Against any pocket pair or a held queen I’m crushed; my only hope is that Flash is purely bluffing. Leading or bluffing out of position here is a very thin line: it bloats the pot against a very deep opponent with wide calling ranges and poor fold equity. Pot control is optimal — keep the pot to 600 and see how Flash reacts. Plan: check and fold to any meaningful bet; if he checks back I’ll re-evaluate turn/river for very targeted bluffs, otherwise preserve my ~37.5bb stack.

I have Quad Queens, which is the absolute nuts on this board since I hold the only remaining Queen (Qd). The board is extremely dry (Qs-Qc-Qh), and it is very unlikely that Minni has connected with it at all. Betting now might force a fold from her entire range. By checking, I am slow-playing my hand to allow her to potentially catch a pair on the turn or attempt a bluff, with the ultimate goal of extracting as much as possible from her 3,750-chip stack.

Board is Q♠ Q♣ Q♥ K♠, I hold T♥9♦. I have no pair, no draw and essentially zero equity versus any reasonable continuing range — the only way to win is if Flash is bluffing. Pot is 600 and my stack is ~3750 (≈37.5bb) vs his ~35650, so preserving stack and pot control are priorities. Checking keeps the pot small and lets Flash show aggression (which I can fold to). Plan: check the turn; fold to any meaningful bet (only consider action vs an extremely tiny probe). If he checks back I’ll re-evaluate the river for a very targeted bluff on a favorable card, otherwise preserve chips.

I have the absolute nuts with Quad Queens. I checked the flop to slow-play and give Minni a chance to catch up or bluff. The turn is a King of Spades, and Minni has checked again. If I bet now, I likely fold out her entire range except for exactly a King. By checking back a second time, I am further disguising the strength of my hand, making it look like I have no interest in the pot. This maximizes the chance of Minni bluffing the river or calling a value bet if the river card improves her hand slightly.

Final board Q♠ Q♣ Q♥ K♠ 3h. I hold T♥9♦ — no pair, no draw, essentially zero showdown equity versus any A‑ or K‑high and completely dominated by any pocket pair or queen. Flash opened preflop and then checked flop and turn; that line can be either a weak missed hand or a slow‑played strong hand, so I have no reliable fold equity to bluff him off better showdowns. Pot is small (600) and my stack is ~3750 (~37.5bb), so preserving chips and pot control is correct — this is not a spot to bloat the pot with air. I’ll check and take showdown; if Flash had bet I would fold to any meaningful bet because my hand has almost no equity.

I have the absolute nuts with Quad Queens. I have slow-played the hand to this point, checking both the flop and the turn to encourage Minni to either bluff or improve to a second-best hand. Minni has checked for the third time on the river. Since the board is extremely dry (three Queens and a King), it's difficult for her to have a strong calling hand. I will place a value bet of 300 (half the pot) in hopes that she interprets this as a late attempt to steal the pot and calls with a weak pair or even Ace-high.

Board Q♠ Q♣ Q♥ K♠ 3♥ with T♥9♦: I have zero pair and no realistic showdown equity versus any reasonable continuing range (Kx, any pocket pair, or a queen). Flash’s line (open pre, check flop and turn, small 300 river stab into a 600 pot) can be either a thin value probe or a bluff, but calling 300 to win 1200 requires ~20% equity — I don’t have it. Raising as a bluff out of position with no fold equity is a negative‑EV play and I should preserve my ~37.5bb stack for better spots. Fold and wait for a cleaner opportunity.

Heads-up on the button with Tc Jd — a strong opening hand in this spot. Pot is only 150 and calling 50 is cheap but passive; raising takes the initiative, builds the pot when we have a range advantage and gains fold equity against the BB’s wide defending range. My stack (~37bb) is deep enough to play postflop, so I’ll open to a standard 3x BB sizing to 300 to maximize value and pressure without overcommitting.

I am in the Big Blind with T3o, which is a very weak hand. Minni has raised to 3BB from the Small Blind/Button. Looking at Minni's stack, they only have 34.5BB left (3450 chips), which makes them very short-stacked in this game. While I have a massive stack, there is no reason to defend such a poor hand against a standard raise from a short stack who is likely looking for a commit or fold situation. I'll fold and wait for a better spot.

I have been dealt pocket Jacks (Js Jd) on the Button/Small Blind. My opponent, Minni, is in the Big Blind with a relatively short stack of 3,750 chips. Pocket Jacks is a premium starting hand, particularly in a heads-up or short-handed situation. Standard play is to raise to build the pot and potentially take down the blinds. Given the stack depths, I want to apply pressure but also leave room to play post-flop if called. I will raise to 300.

Facing a 300 open from the button with 6♣2♥ in the big blind, this is an easy fold.
