Heads-up I’ll be out of position postflop and 6-2o has virtually no playability or showdown equity against a typical open-raising range. Calling 200 to see a flop gives me ~3:1 immediate pot odds (need >25% equity), but 6-2o rarely meets that vs a realistic button range and I’ll be folding to most continuation bets or getting dominated when I do pair. A reraise/shove is pure bluff and will be called too often by a deep-stacked opponent. Preserve the short ~3,750 stack for a better spot—fold.

Heads-up on the button with Ad4s — a good heads-up hand with an ace-blocker and position. I want to take the initiative, pressure the blind, and avoid being passive by just calling 50. I’ll open-raise to 3x the BB (300 total) which is a standard sizing here: big enough to fold out weaker holdings and keep the pot manageable relative to our 37BB stack. Plan: if Flash 3-bets big I’ll strongly consider folding to preserve fold equity; if he calls I’ll c-bet favorable flops and play carefully on ace-high or coordinated boards.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 3d. Minni, who is currently short-stacked with about 34.5 big blinds, has raised to 300 (a 3x open) from the Small Blind. While I am getting 2:1 odds to call, 8-3 suited is a very weak hand that realizes equity poorly. Given Minni's short stack, she is more likely to commit her remaining chips on the flop, making it difficult to play a speculative hand like this out of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot to use my significant chip lead.

I have been dealt a pocket pair of sevens in the small blind. This is a very strong starting hand, especially given that Minni (the Big Blind) has a relatively short stack of 3750 compared to my stack of 36100. I want to build a pot and put pressure on the short stack. A standard open-raise is appropriate here to take control of the hand pre-flop.

Heads-up vs the button/SB open to 300. I’m BB with K♦4♥ and ~37.5bb effective. K4o is marginal but defensible heads-up; calling 200 into a 400 pot is cheap and keeps the pot manageable. A 3‑bet would be thin with a weak kicker and would bloat the pot out of position against an aggressive, very deep opponent. Plan: call to 300 total to see a flop; if I miss completely I’ll fold to large continuation bets, but I’ll continue/raise with a paired K, strong draw or good board texture.

I missed this flop with K♦4♥ — no pair, just a single diamond (backdoor only) and no real straight possibilities. I called preflop to keep the pot small and my plan was to fold to heavy aggression when I completely miss. Flash is the button/SB and opens wide heads‑up, so continuation bets are common, but I’m OOP with a weak hand and only marginal equity. Checking gives me pot control and the option to fold to a large c‑bet or call a small one to see a turn (I’ll call small bets up to ~1/3–1/2 pot to realize backdoor equity, but I’ll fold to sizeable bets/raises). No raise or bluff here — just check and proceed based on Flash’s action.

I have flopped a set of sevens on a very dry 3-9-7 rainbow board. This is an extremely strong hand, and my primary goal is to extract the maximum value from Minni, who is playing a relatively short stack of 3,550. Since the board is disconnected and lacks many draws, I want to keep Minni's range wide while building the pot. A standard continuation bet of half the pot (300) is effective here to get called by top pair (9x), middle pairs, or even some overcards, setting up a potential stack-shove on later streets.

Heads‑up, button opened wide and I defended the BB with K♦4♥ to keep the pot manageable. Flop 3c‑9d‑7s completely misses me — I have only K‑high and a single diamond backdoor (very few real outs). Flash’s bet is small (~300 into a 900 pot, ~1/3 pot), so calling is cheap: I’m getting roughly 20% pot‑odds on this call and keeping the pot controllable with ~35.5bb effective. Raising OOP with a weak kicker and almost no fold equity is thin and will bloat the pot against a very wide opponent; folding to such a small c‑bet is too tight. I’ll call 300 to see a turn, then re‑evaluate: fold to heavy turn aggression unless I pair the K or the board changes in a way that improves my equity or gives me a clear plan.

I defended the BB with K♦4♥ to keep the pot small. Turn 3c‑9d‑7s‑5h does not improve my hand — I have K‑high only and essentially no realistic draws. I have only three kings left (≈6.5% to pair on the river), so with one card to come my equity is far too small to profitably call a bet. The correct plan is pot‑control: check and save chips. If Flash bets on the turn I will fold to any reasonable sized bet (even a small bet is not correct to call — e.g. a 400 bet into the 1200 pot would require ≈20% equity to break even, much more than my ~6.5%). If he checks back I get to a free river and can re‑evaluate. Action: check now to keep the pot small and avoid bloating OOP with a marginal hand.

I have a set of sevens on a relatively safe board (3c 9d 7s 5h). Minni has checked to me again. My goal is to maximize value from her remaining stack of 3,250. A bet of 800 into the 1,200 pot (two-thirds pot) is a strong value bet that keeps her range wide enough to call with a 9, a middle pair, or a straight draw (like 86 or 64), while building the pot for a potential shove on the river.

I have K♦4♥ with no pair and essentially no draw (only three kings left → ~6.5% to pair on the river). Flash’s turn bet is 800 into a 1200 pot (pot is 2000 after the bet), so a call of 800 requires about 28.6% equity to break even. My equity is far below that, so calling is a large negative‑EV play. Raising with K‑high is thin and would bloat the pot OOP against a wide but aggressive opener. Stack preservation and pot control dictate folding here.

Heads-up on the button with AhTh suited — a strong hand to take the initiative. Pot is 150 and I only need 50 to call, but I want to isolate the big blind, build value vs worse aces/broadways, and gain fold equity. With ~32BB effective, a standard 3x BB open to 300 is a balanced size: enough to pressure the BB while keeping the pot manageable against possible 3-bets. I’ll raise to 300 total.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Queen offsuit, which is a premium hand in a heads-up situation. My opponent, Minni, is on a relatively short stack of 2950 chips. I want to build a pot and put pressure on their stack immediately. A 3-bet to 900 is a standard size that forces Minni to either commit a third of their stack to see a flop or fold.

Facing Flash’s 3-bet to 900 after my open to 300. Pot is 1200 and I must call 600 more (900 total) with AhTh and ~29.5BB effective. ATs is too strong heads-up to fold — it plays well in position (postflop), has good top-pair potential, backdoor flush/straight equity, and an Ace blocker. A 4-bet or shove would commit a large chunk of my stack and will often be called by better hands (QQ+/AK), so I prefer to keep the pot manageable and realize equity. Plan: call, use position to evaluate the flop and continue aggressively when I connect or use pot control when missed.

I 3-bet pre-flop and the flop comes Ace-high, which is very favorable for my perceived range. I will place a continuation bet of 600 (1/3 pot) to capitalize on this range advantage and try to take the pot down immediately. If Minni raises or shoves, I can comfortably fold my King-high as her stack is short and such a move would indicate a strong hand or a significant draw.

Call 600.  Quick math: pre-flop pot was 1800, Flash bet 600 making the pot 2400; calling 600 would make the pot 3000, so I only need ~20% equity to break even. I have AhTh — top pair with a very good kicker plus the nut-heart blocker and a 9‑out nut-flush draw (≈19% to hit on the turn, ≈35% by river). Against a 3‑bettor’s range (AK/AQ, big pairs, some suited connectors) this hand has plenty of equity and frequently is the best hand or has strong redraws. Raising here (min to 1200 or shoving) would commit a large portion of my relatively short effective stack and will often get called or 4‑bet by better Aces/pairs; I prefer to keep the pot manageable and use position to extract value or fold later. Plan: call to realize equity and play the turn in position — bet/raise for value if I improve or get a favorable action, otherwise control the pot or fold to polarized aggression.

I have King-high on a board with an Ace. I've already 3-bet pre-flop and made a continuation bet on the flop, both of which Minni called. Minni's remaining stack is 1750 chips, while the pot is 3000. Given her low stack-to-pot ratio, she is highly unlikely to fold any pair or even a draw if I bet. Since my hand has no made value and very little equity to improve, I will check. If Minni moves all-in, I will fold and preserve my remaining stack, as she is likely committed with an Ace or a better hand.

Flash checked to me on the turn (board 2h Ad 7h 5d). I hold AhTh: top pair with the best kicker plus a 9‑out heart draw to the river (9/46 ≈ 19.6%). Pot is 3000 and my remaining stack is 1750 (≈17.5BB) while Flash is very deep. Given his line (preflop 3‑bet, small flop bet, then check turn) he can have a lot of Ax, some big pairs, or medium hands — and a turn bet or shove here will often be called by hands that beat me. Betting or shoving now would commit a large fraction of my short stack and frequently get called by better Aces/sets, while checking keeps the pot controllable and preserves my ability to apply maximum pressure on the river if the situation is favorable. I also hold the Ace of hearts which blocks the nut heart combos. Plan: check the turn, see the river, and then (a) if I hit the flush or a very safe river appears, extract value or shove; (b) if the river is a blank and Flash shows weakness, use my stack to pressure; (c) if he bets a reasonable size on the river I will often call with TP+K given pot odds, but fold to polarized large sizing if the river completes scary boards and he jams. For now the clear, lowest-variance line is to check the turn and evaluate the river.

On the river, the board is Ad-Tc-7h-5d-2h. I have King-Queen offsuit, which has zero showdown value against any of Minni's likely calling range (pairs or Aces). However, Minni is on a very short stack of 1,750 chips with a pot of 3,000. Given the low stack-to-pot ratio, she is mathematically committed to call with almost any piece of the board. My check on the turn likely signaled weakness, but it also preserved my stack. While I could attempt to shove to push her off a weak hand, the likelihood of a fold from a short-stacked player who called a 3-bet and a flop bet is very low. I will check and hope she checks back with a worse high-card hand, though I expect to lose this pot most of the time. I will fold if she bets her remaining chips.

River decision with AhTh on 2h Ad 7h 5d Tc: I have top pair with the best kicker and I block a lot of Ace combos (Ah). Flash was the preflop 3-bettor (strong range) but his line — a small flop bet then checking turn and river — shows weakness/pot control. Checking back is safe but surrenders thin value vs worse Aces and medium pockets that are plausibly in his calling range; shoving would overcommit my short stack and get called by better Aces/sets too often. I want to extract thin value while keeping risk controlled, so I will value-bet about 1/3 pot to target worse Aces, medium pairs and some bluffs. Size = 1000 chips: small enough to fold to a large raise without committing the rest of my stack, big enough to get calls from many worse hands. If he raises big (polarized), I’ll fold; if he calls I’m often ahead or close enough to accept showdown.

On the river, the board is Ad-Tc-7h-5d-2h and I have King-high with no showdown value. I checked both the turn and the river, signaling weakness. Minni has bet 1000 into a 3000 pot, leaving her with only 750 chips. Since I have no pair and no way to beat even a weak pair or a pocket pair she might have called with, and because she is effectively pot-committed given her tiny remaining stack, a bluff is unlikely to work. I must concede the pot.

I have been dealt a premium pocket pair, King of Diamonds and King of Spades, on the button. With only two players active and Minni holding a relatively short stack of 4,650 (approximately 46.5 big blinds), I should aim to build the pot immediately for value. A standard raise to 300 is appropriate to induce action or take down the blinds, while also positioning myself to potentially play for stacks if Minni chooses to defend or re-raise.

Heads-up vs Button open to 300; I’m in the big blind with Th4h. Pot is 400 and I must call 200 more to reach 300 total. In heads-up the opening range is wide and T4s has playable equity (plus backdoor heart/straight possibilities) and good postflop playability. With ~46 BB effective I prefer to realize equity postflop rather than 3-bet OOP with a marginal hand or fold a lot of equity. Call to see a flop; plan to continue with pairs or strong draws, float on favorable textures, and fold to heavy aggression when missed.

Flop: 4s 6s 8h. I hold Th4h — bottom pair with a heart backdoor but vulnerable to two spades on board and any 6x/8x. Opponent opened wide heads‑up, so his range contains many overcards and spade draws as well as some paired hands. Pot is only 600 and I’m ~44.5 BB deep; I don’t want to inflate the pot OOP with a marginal made hand. Checking controls the pot, lets me realize equity cheaply, and induces bluffs or small continuation bets I can call. Plan: check and call small–medium c‑bets to see turns; fold to heavy aggression (large raise/pot‑sized shove) because my hand has little fold equity or redraws. If villain checks behind I’ll re-evaluate the turn and look to extract thin value on safe cards or take showdown.

I hold pocket kings on a low, connected board of 4s-6s-8h. While this board can be dangerous due to straight and flush draws, my overpair is currently the best hand in most scenarios. Minni checked to me, so I will place a value bet to extract chips from top-pair hands, smaller pocket pairs, and draws. A bet of 400 into the 600-chip pot is a standard sizing that builds the pot while providing protection against various draws.

Heads‑up vs a wide button open and a small flop c‑bet. I have T♥4♥ on 4♠6♠8♥ — bottom pair with two hearts in hand (a very slim backdoor flush path) and reasonable showdown value against a wide range of overcards and spade draws. The bet is small: calling 400 into a 1,000 pot gives pot odds of ~28.6% (400/(1000+400)), which my hand likely meets versus a wide c‑betting range. Raising OOP with a vulnerable bottom pair is unattractive — it bloats the pot, gets me into uncomfortable spots vs spade draws or stronger made hands, and won’t fold out many worse pairs or equity hands. Plan: call the 400 to realize equity and re‑evaluate on the turn. If villain checks the turn I can proceed to extract thin value or pot control; if villain continues with small/medium bets I will call again; I will fold to heavy aggression (large pot‑sized raise or shove) unless I improve. Therefore I will call.

Board: 4♠ 6♠ 8♥ A♣. I have T♥4♥ — bottom pair with a weak kicker and a useless heart backdoor. The Ace on the turn hits a big chunk of the button’s wide heads‑up range (A‑x, many overcards) and some of his bluffs (spade semi‑bluffs now have showdown equity), so my hand loses a lot of fold equity and is vulnerable to bigger pairs and completed draws. Pot control OOP is the priority: raising here bloats the pot with a marginal hand and will get me into uncomfortable spots against spades/As/6x/8x. I’ll check to keep the pot manageable, induce bluffs, and realise equity cheaply. Plan: if Villain checks back I take showdown; if he bets a small–medium size (roughly ≤ ~2/3 pot) I will call and re‑evaluate on the river; I will fold to pot‑sized or larger aggression unless I improve. Check to preserve stack and make a cleaner decision on the river.

I hold pocket Kings, and the Ace of Clubs on the turn is a scare card. However, Minni has checked to me twice on a draw-heavy board. While the Ace is in her range (Ax), my Kings still have significant value against her draws (spades, 57, 79) and middle pairs (8x, 6x). I will bet small here to extract value from those draws and pairs while keeping the pot manageable if she does have the Ace. If she raises, I will have a tough decision, but a check behind might miss value on a coordinated board.

Flash’s turn bet is small relative to the pot (600 into a 2000 pot). I have T♥4♥ — bottom pair with a weak kicker and only backdoor redraws. Calling 600 to a final pot of ~2600 requires ~23% equity; versus a wide heads‑up continuation range (overcards, spade draws, some A‑x, and occasional pairs) my hand has enough showdown equity to justify a call. Raising OOP with a vulnerable bottom pair would bloat the pot and put me in very uncomfortable spots versus A‑x, 6x/8x and spade draws, so I won’t inflate. I’ll call to realize equity, keep the pot manageable, and re‑evaluate on the river: call small/medium bets again or fold to pot‑sized/clear shove unless I improve.

Heads‑up vs Flash who opened and barreled (flop 400, turn 600). Board 4♠ 6♠ 8♥ A♣ Q♦; I hold T♥4♥ — bottom pair. Pot is 2600 and there is no bet to call on the river. Flash checking the river after double‑barreling polarizes his line: either missed spade/overcard bluffs or a medium/strong made hand (A‑x, 6x/8x, slow‑played pair) pot‑controlling. I have limited fold equity and little chance to get better hands to fold; many hands that will call a river bet beat me, and a raise would commit a large portion of my ~34.5BB stack into a ~26BB pot. Betting to “block” or thin‑value is low EV here because it will be called by better and rarely fold out hands that actually beat me. Pot control and a cheap showdown is the highest EV line: check and see the showdown rather than bloating the pot OOP with a marginal hand.

I have pocket Kings, which were a powerhouse pre-flop and on the flop. However, the board runout of an Ace on the turn and a Queen on the river is quite unfavorable. Minni has check-called three streets. While I might still have the best hand against missed draws (like 7s5s or 5s7s) or a stubborn middle pair (8x), it is very difficult to get value from worse hands by betting on this specific runout. Most hands in Minni's range that would call a river bet likely include an Ace or a Queen that hit the board. Since I have showdown value and want to avoid a potential check-raise from a trap or losing more chips to a superior hand, checking back to see the showdown is the most prudent play.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind). In heads-up play my opening range should be very wide — I want the initiative and to play postflop in position. 7♦2♦ is weak but suited and acceptable to open here. I’ll make a standard 2xBB open to 200: that requires me to add 150 more (total committed 200) and keeps the pot small while applying pressure. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold this hand; if he just calls I’ll see a flop in position and c-bet or check to control the pot depending on texture. Leaves me plenty of chips behind to manoeuvre.

I'm in the big blind holding Qs 3h. Minni, on the button, has raised to 200, which means I only need to call 100 more to see a flop. Given that Minni's stack is quite small (3,250 compared to my 36,450), I can afford to see a flop here even with a marginal hand like Q3 offsuit to see if I hit top pair. However, Q3o is a very weak hand and out of position. Given the pot odds (100 to win 300), calling is borderline acceptable in a casual sense, but strategically, it's often better to just let these go against a short stack to avoid difficult post-flop spots. Looking back at recent hands, I've been playing reasonably tight-aggressive. I will fold this weak hand and wait for a better spot, especially since Minni is short-stacked and might be looking to commit chips soon.

I am in the Small Blind with J2 offsuit, a very weak starting hand. Minni, the Big Blind, is playing a relatively short stack (34.5 big blinds). While I have a significant chip lead, J2o is not a hand worth playing, especially as the short stack is more likely to defend aggressively or commit their remaining chips. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with J♠3♥: this is a marginal hand but perfectly playable in position. I want to take the initiative, deny free flops, and keep the BB’s calling range wide, so I’ll open-raise to the minimum standard sizing (200 total = 2×BB). The raise is small enough to keep pots manageable with a speculative/marginal holding but gives fold equity. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll fold; if called I’ll c-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously on dry/overcard boards.

I hold a premium hand (Ace-King offsuit) in the Big Blind. Minni, who is currently short-stacked with 3,400 chips, has made a small 2x raise from the button. Given the strength of my hand and the stack-to-pot ratio, I want to 3-bet for value. A raise to 600 chips (3x her raise) is a standard play here to build the pot and potentially induce a shove from a shorter stack.

I opened to 200 on the button with J♠3♥ and Flash 3‑bet to 600. With ~3,400 behind (~34BB) this hand is easily dominated by a typical 3‑bet range (pairs, broadways, suited Aces) and has poor equity (roughly ~15–25% depending on his range). The pot is 800 and the call is 400, so I would need >400/(800+400)=33.3% equity to break even — J3o doesn't have that against a reasonable 3‑bet range. Calling would leave me OOP in a bloated pot with weak equity; a light 4‑bet or shove is too risky and likely to be called by better hands. I’ll preserve my 34BB stack and fold to the 3‑bet, waiting for a better spot to apply pressure.

I am on the Button with Five of Clubs and Deuce of Spades, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. My opponent, Minni, has a relatively short stack of 3,300 (33 big blinds). While I have a massive chip lead, there is no strategic reason to invest chips in a hand with such poor equity and playability, especially since a raise might provoke a shove from a short stack. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with Qc6c — a playable, suited hand that performs well in position. With 34 BB effective I want to take the initiative rather than limp/call; open-raising a wide range is standard heads-up. Raising to 300 (3x the BB) gives solid fold equity, prices out very weak hands, and keeps the pot size reasonable if called. Postflop I’ll c-bet many favorable textures and use position to pressure. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll often fold this holding; I may call smaller 3-bets depending on sizing and implied odds. Overall, raise to seize initiative and maximize EV from position.

I am in the Big Blind with 3h 2h. Minni, who is playing with a relatively short stack of 31.5 big blinds, has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. Although I am getting 2:1 odds to call, 32s is one of the weakest hands in poker and realizes equity poorly out of position. Given the stack sizes and the hand strength, folding is the most prudent play to avoid unnecessary losses with a marginal hand.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with J6o. Minni is in the Big Blind with a relatively short stack of 34.5 big blinds (3450 chips). While J6o is not a premium hand, it is a standard fold or occasionally a steal candidate in a head-up or three-handed game. However, looking at the previous hands, Minni has been quite active and aggressive from the Big Blind. With her short stack, she is more likely to either defend wide or shove. Raising J6o here doesn't provide enough value or fold equity against a 34BB stack that might be looking for a spot to double up. I will fold and wait for a better spot to exploit her stack size.

5♥2♦ is one of the weakest possible starting hands. Although heads‑up the button can open very wide, Flash (BB) is deep and plays a lot of hands/defends often in the logs — raising with 5‑2 will frequently get called or 3‑bet and leave me in dominated, marginal spots with 35.5BB effective. Calling 50 to see a flop is small, but post‑flop this hand has very poor equity and will force folds or tough turns/rivers. I’ll preserve my stack for a better spot and fold preflop.

I am in the small blind with a weak hand, 3s 8d. The opponent in the big blind, Minni, has a relatively short stack of 3,450 (34.5 BB). 3-8 offsuit is near the bottom of my range and generally not worth playing from the SB against a stack that can easily shove over a raise. I will fold.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with A♣3♠ — this is a very playable heads-up hand and well inside my opening range. I want the initiative and to use positional advantage postflop, so I’ll open-raise to a standard 3x BB (300) to get fold equity and define the hand. With ~35BB effective I won’t bloat the pot unnecessarily; if Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold A3o (not strong enough to call large 3-bets), but if he just calls I’ll C-bet favorable flops and play my Ace for value when it hits.

I have Ace-Queen offsuit (AQo) in the Big Blind. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind (Button). Minni has a relatively short stack of 3300. Given the strength of my hand and the shallow stack of my opponent, I should raise to put pressure on them and potentially get it all in pre-flop. A 3-bet to 900 or 1000 is standard, but since Minni's stack is only 3300 total, any significant raise effectively commits them. I will raise to 900.

I opened to 300 on the button with A♣3♠ and Flash 3-bet to 900. With ~33BB effective A3o is a standard defend/call heads-up — it has decent equity vs a wide 3-bet range and I retain positional advantage postflop. Calling 600 more (900 total) keeps the pot manageable, preserves fold equity and flexibility, and only requires ~33% equity to be +EV (pot 1200 -> call 600 to make it 1800). A 4-bet here would commit too many chips with an offsuit Ace; folding is too tight given Flash’s demonstrated aggression. Plan after calling: c-bet or take the lead on Ace‑high/dry flops for value, play cautiously on coordinated/connected boards, and fold to heavy aggression when I’m clearly behind.

I have Ace-Queen offsuit on a paired board of 4s-Td-Ts. I was the pre-flop aggressor with a 3-bet to 900, and Minni called. The pot is currently 1800 and Minni has 2700 chips remaining. While the board contains a pair of Tens, I still have two overcards and strong range equity as the 3-better. I will lead with a continuation bet of 600 (one-third pot) to put pressure on Minni's non-paired hands and to see where I stand. Given the shallow stack of my opponent, this bet maintains my initiative without committing too many chips if I face a shove.

I opened preflop with A♣3♠ and called Flash’s 3‑bet to keep the pot manageable and use position. Flop is 4♠ T♦ T♠ and Flash now bets 600 into the pot. I have Ace‑high (A♣3♠) and a very weak backdoor spade possibility (need both turn+river spades). This is a dry, paired board where many hands in a 3‑bet range (overpairs, TTs, AT, some bluffs) will continue—however Flash’s small sizing keeps a lot of missed hands in the betting range. Calling 600 to win ~3000 (pot after my call) requires only ~20% equity, so it’s a cheap way to realize showdown equity, keep position, and fold later if the turn-card lines strongly favor villain.

Raising here would commit too much with no pair and little fold equity vs value hands (sets/overpairs), and folding is too passive because I block A‑high combos and will beat a fair number of bluffs. Plan: call the 600, see the turn, and re‑evaluate — bet/extract value if I pair the Ace, or fold/produce minimal resistance if villain shows heavy aggression on safe turns.

The turn King of Spades is generally a very good card for my 3-betting range, as it connects with hands like AK, KQ, and AA/KK. However, Minni called both the 3-bet pre-flop and the continuation bet on a paired flop, suggesting they have a piece of the board (likely a Ten, a pocket pair, or a King). With Minni's remaining stack at only 2100 and the pot at 3000, any bet I make is effectively an all-in. I will check to see how Minni responds. If Minni checks back, I get a free river card to potentially hit an Ace, Queen, or a straight-completing Jack. If Minni shoves, I will have to decide if my Ace-high and gutshot straight draw have enough equity to call based on the pot odds.

Turn summary: I’m on the button with A♣3♠. Board is 4♠ T♦ T♠ K♠. Flash checked to me after a small flop c‑bet. I have Ace‑high plus a 4‑card spade draw — 9 outs to make a flush on the river (~9/46 ≈ 19.6%) and a couple of pair‑outs (A or 3). Pot is 3,000 and my stack is 2,100.

Decision logic: checking here surrenders fold equity and lets a wide chunk of Flash’s range see the river for free (and I’m behind to any made pair: tens, kings or most pocket pairs). Shoving would be overly committal with only ~20% to improve and against an aggressive, deep opponent is likely to be called too often. A small‑to‑medium turn stab is the best line: it (a) folds out some Kx/underpairs and bluffs that checked behind, (b) gives me fold equity while I still have live outs if called, and (c) keeps the pot manageable if called. I size to about one‑third pot to maximize fold equity while preserving room to fold to heavy action on the river.

Plan after this bet: if I get called I’m happy to see the river and will value bet/lead when I complete the flush or pair the Ace; if I face a large check‑raise I’ll fold (I don’t want to commit my stack with only one‑card outs).

I am currently holding Ace-high (AQo) and a gutshot straight draw (to a Jack) on a board of 4s-Td-Ts-Ks. After 3-betting pre-flop and continuation betting on the flop, Minni's call suggests she has a piece of the board, likely a Ten or a pocket pair. The King of Spades on the turn is a scare card that connects with some of my range, but Minni's bet of 1,000 into a 3,000 pot (with only 1,100 left behind) indicates extreme strength or a committed draw. Although I am getting 4:1 pot odds (needing 20% equity), my 4 outs for a straight give me only about an 8.7% chance to hit on the river. Even if my Aces are live (another 3 outs), my total equity is around 15%, which is not enough to justify a call against a range that likely includes trips (Tx) or a King (Kx). Given my significant stack lead and the likelihood that I am beaten, I will fold and wait for a better spot.
I am on the button with Qd 9d, a strong suited hand. Minni is in the big blind with a relatively short stack of 5,000 (50 BB). I will raise to 300 to exert pressure and play the hand in position.

Heads-up vs a button (SB) open to 300. I’m in the big blind with Qc8h, 50bb effective (stack 5000). The pot is 400 and I must call 200 more, so I’m getting ~33% pot odds. Versus a wide SB opening range Q8o has roughly ~40–45% equity — calling is +EV. A min-3-bet to 500 is a small, low-fold-equity move with a marginal hand and would leave me OOP on the flop; I don’t want to bloat the pot or face a large 4-bet with this holding. Plan: call 200 to see a flop, play cautiously OOP — fold to strong, non-giving action when I miss, and be aggressive if I pair the queen or pick up strong draws.

I missed this paired 9-9-5 flop with Qc8h — no pair and only a very weak backdoor club. Flash opened wide preflop so he c-bets a lot, but his range on this board contains many value hands that have me crushed (9x, 5x, pockets, overpairs) and will continue with pressure. With ~48–50bb effective and the pot already at 600, I want pot control out of position and to avoid bloating the pot with a marginal holding. Check to see his action; fold to meaningful aggression (I can call a tiny probe to see a cheap turn but will fold to standard/large c-bets). If I pair the Q or pick up a real draw on the turn I’ll re-evaluate.

I have flopped trips with a Queen kicker (9-9-9-Q-5). The board is 9c-5s-9h. My opponent, Minni, has checked. With such a strong hand on a relatively dry board, I want to build the pot while also giving her a chance to catch up or bluff if she has nothing. However, I shouldn't give too many free cards if she has a pocket pair or a 5. A small to medium-sized bet is appropriate here to get value from pairs or to induce a mistake. I will bet 300, which is half the pot.

Flash c‑bets a small probe (300 into a 900 pot). I’m OOP with Qc8h and have missed the paired 9‑9‑5 board — only a backdoor club and Q‑high. However the price is cheap: calling 300 to win ~1200 gives ~25% pot odds, and vs a wide SB opening/c‑bet range I can justify a float to see the turn. Raising here would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and has poor fold equity; folding is too nitty for this price. Plan: call 300 to see a turn, keep the pot controlled, and fold to meaningful turn aggression unless I pair the Q or pick up a real draw (club). If I improve on the turn I’ll re‑evaluate for value/pressure.

Call 300. I’m out of position with Qc8h on 9c–5s–9h–4s: I have no pair and only a very weak backdoor club, but Flash opened wide and is firing small probes (flop c‑bet and a tiny turn bet). Calling 300 into roughly a 900 pot gives ~25% required equity — cheap enough to float here to see the river versus a wide, aggressive range. Raising would bloat the pot OOP with almost no fold equity; folding is too tight for this price. Plan: call to see the river, fold to meaningful river aggression if I don’t improve, and if I pair the Q or hit a club I’ll extract value or apply controlled pressure depending on sizing.

I have trips with a strong Queen kicker on a 9-5-9-4 board. Minni has checked for the second time. I should continue betting for value to get paid by her pocket pairs, any 5, or possibly some flush draws that picked up equity on the turn. A bet of 800 into the 1200 pot puts pressure on her remaining 4500 chips and builds the pot for a potential river commitment.

I’m out of position with Qc8h on 9c–5s–9h–4s and Flash has bet 800 into a 2000 pot. Calling 800 to win ~2800 requires ~28.6% equity. I have only ~6 clean outs (3 queens + 3 eights) to pair on the river — roughly ~13% equity — well below the break‑even threshold. His line (open, flop c‑bet, turn barrel) is consistent with a high proportion of value hands here (9x, 5x, overpairs) and, given his deep stack and aggression, this sizing looks like a meaningful value/probe rather than a pure light bluff. Raising is a poor option with no real equity or fold equity to justify it. Folding preserves my stack and avoids turning a marginal float into a costly river decision. Fold.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening very wide. 5♦7♣ is marginal but entirely reasonable to raise for fold equity and to play the flop in position. I’ll make a standard small button open to 200 total — it applies pressure to the big blind while keeping the pot controllable with my ~44BB stack. Plan: fold to a large 3‑bet, call small-to-medium 3‑bets selectively, and if called use positional advantage to c‑bet favorable flops and control the pot when I miss.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Six offsuit. Minni, who has a very short stack of 4300 (43 big blinds), has raised to 200 from the Small Blind. I only need to call 100 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 400. Jack-Six is not a strong hand, but against a 2x open from the SB, I have enough pot equity to defend. However, Minni's stack size makes this a potentially tricky spot if I hit a marginal piece. Given the price, I will call and see a flop.

On this flop of 8c 8s 2h, the board is extremely dry. I have Jack-high. Minni, as the pre-flop aggressor from the small blind, will likely continuation bet many hands. However, she has checked. My hand has some showdown value against her air, but it's very weak. If I check back, I keep the pot small and can potentially see a free turn. Betting here doesn't accomplish much as I don't want to get check-raised by her short stack, and I can't represent an eight very easily after just calling pre-flop. Checking is the most prudent play to see how the turn develops.

I opened wide from the button preflop but this flop (8♣8♠2♥) completely misses my 5♦7♣ and is a paired board that favors the BB’s calling range (pocket pairs, 8x, and lots of single-pair/overcard showdown hands). I have essentially zero immediate equity (only a few outs to pair) and no meaningful backdoor draws, so any bet here is a pure bluff that’s likely to be called by a sticky opponent who has shown a tendency to call down. With 4,300 behind and a 400‑chip pot, I’d rather control the size of the pot and see a turn in position. Plan: check and re‑evaluate on the turn — fold to a clear value bet, consider a small delayed bluff only on favourable, uncoordinated turn cards or realize equity if I pair.

On the turn, the board is 8c-8s-2h-3h. I have Jack-high. On the flop, I checked and Minni checked behind, which suggests her range is somewhat weak or she is playing cautiously with her short stack. The 3h on the turn is unlikely to have improved her hand unless she has something like pocket threes or hearts, but most of her range remains high cards or small pairs. Since I am out of position and have limited showdown value, I will check again to see if she checks behind once more, allowing me to see the river for free. I don't want to commit chips as a bluff against a short stack who might be looking for a double-up with any decent pair or Ace-high.

I opened very wide from the button and the paired 8♣8♠ flop completely misses my 5♦7♣ and heavily favors the BB’s calling range (pocket pairs, 8x, and many single-pair/overcards). Flash checked the flop and the turn (3♥) — showing either weakness or a slowplay — but I have essentially zero value/semibluff equity (only six weak outs to make a low pair). With 4,300 behind and a 400-chip pot, betting now would be a pure delayed c-bet that is likely to be called by sticky hands and unnecessarily inflates the pot. I’ll check back to control pot size, realize my (small) equity, and re-evaluate the river: fold to a clear large turn/river value bet, call small probes that price me correctly, and consider a targeted delayed bluff on a favourable, uncoordinated river.

On the river 6s, I have improved to a pair of sixes. The action has been extremely passive with both players checking the flop and the turn. Minni's checks suggest a weak range, likely high-card hands or very small pairs. By checking, I allow her to bluff with her air or check back for a showdown. While I could bet for thin value against Ace-high, checking is the safest way to realize my showdown value with a marginal hand in a small pot.

River (8c 8s 2h 3h 6s) — I hold 5♦7♣ and completely missed. Flash checked flop and turn, which can indicate weakness or a slowplay, but this paired board heavily favors his calling range (pocket pairs, 8x, and many one-pair hands) and he’s shown a tendency to call down. Pot is only 400 and I have 4,300 behind; a bluff here has very little fold equity and is likely to be called. I’ll check back to control the pot, realize my tiny showdown equity, and preserve chips. If he bets, I’ll fold to a clear value bet and only consider calling very small probes that price me correctly.

I am on the button with KQs, a very strong starting hand in a heads-up or short-handed scenario. My opponent, Minni, has a relatively short stack of 4,200. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and exert pressure, as KQs plays very well against most of the hands Minni will call with.

Heads-up in the big blind vs a standard button open to 300. I hold Qd7h — marginal but playable in heads-up. Pot is 400 and I must call 200 more, which gives me 3:1 immediate pot odds (need ~25% equity). Versus a wide button opening range Q7o has enough equity to defend and realize equity postflop. A small 3‑bet to the minimum (500) would not gain reliable fold equity and would create uncomfortable OOP spots against a very deep and aggressive opponent, so I don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal hand. With ~42BB I can see a flop cheaply and fold to heavy pressure if I miss, or continue aggressively when I connect. I will call the 200 (total 300 committed) to defend the big blind and play the flop.

Check. I have Q7o on A-6-6: no pair, no meaningful backdoors, and the Ace on the flop makes me a huge underdog to most of Flash’s continuing range in heads-up play. Flash has opened wide preflop but is also clearly aggressive from the hand history (frequent c-bets and turns raises), so betting here would rarely get better hands to fold and would commit me OOP with a marginal holding. Pot control is priority: I’ll check and plan to fold to sizable pressure. Specifically: I will call very small probe bets (roughly ≤ ~1/3 pot ≈ 200) because of the pot odds and the chance to realize equity, but I will fold to medium/large continuation bets (≥ ~1/2 pot) or to a check-raise. If Flash checks back I can take a free card to the turn and re-evaluate (look to bluff only if I pick up an improved draw or a very favorable board/runout/read).

I have KQs on a 6c Ah 6s board. While I have missed the flop, the board is fairly dry. Minni checked, which is common as the pre-flop caller. I have two overcards and backdoor flush/straight possibilities. I will make a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to try and take the pot down now, as this board favors the pre-flop aggressor's range more than the caller's.

Flash has made a small continuation bet (300 into a ~900 pot ≈ 1/3 pot). Calling 300 gives me ~4:1 on a call (need ~20% equity). With Q7o on A-6-6 I have almost no showdown value versus Ax or 6x but against a wide button c-bet range I still beat a fair number of bluffs and weaker broadway hands. I’m out of position and raising would be a thin, expensive bluff with little fold equity and an uncomfortable spot if he presses. I’ll call the 300 to realize equity, keep the pot controllable, and fold to significant turn aggression unless I improve (pair or a real draw).

I’ve paired my 7 on the turn (Qd7h on 6c Ah 6s 7c) — second pair. Flash’s range (preflop open then small flop c‑bet) will contain a lot of A‑x (which beat me) and some 6x/sets (which crush me), plus bluffs. I’m out of position with a shallow effective stack relative to Flash’s deep stack, so I want to keep the pot under control and avoid bloating it with a marginal holding that is often behind.

Betting here won’t fold many Ax hands and can get me raised by sets — both bad outcomes. Checking keeps the decision simple: if Flash checks back I get to showdown cheaply and likely win; if he fires on the river I can call small/medium bets to realize equity and fold to large aggression. Plan: check, call small river bets (~≤1/2 pot) for pot‑odds and fold to big pressure or a check‑raise on the turn.

Action: check to control the pot and see the river cheaply.

On the turn, the 7 of clubs gives me the nut flush draw to go along with my two overcards. Minni checked again after calling my flop continuation bet. While she has shown some stickiness in previous hands, a second barrel here applies significant pressure on her range, which likely consists of weak Aces, pocket pairs, or mid-range connectors. If she calls, I have 12 outs (9 clubs + 3 Kings) that likely give me the best hand on the river. I will bet 800 into the 1,200 pot to maintain aggression and build the pot in case I hit my draw.

I checked the turn to keep the pot small with a marginal but improved holding (Qd7h → pair of 7s). Flash’s 800 into a 2000 pot (~40% pot) is a medium‑small bet that gives me ~2.5:1 pot odds (need ≈28.6% equity). Heads‑up from the button Flash opens very wide and c‑bets/continues aggressively, so my second pair actually has reasonable equity versus his continuing range (I beat a lot of missed broadways, Kx and pure bluffs; I lose to most Ax and any 6x). Raising here is thin — it won’t fold enough Ax and can get me committed OOP against stronger holdings — and folding would be conceding a lot to a bet I can profitably call given the odds. I’ll call the 800 to see the river, keep SPR low, and re‑evaluate. Plan for river: if Flash checks I’m happy to go to showdown; if he fires a small/medium bet (roughly ≤1/2 pot) I’ll call to realize equity; if he applies heavy pressure or jams I’ll fold and preserve my stack.

River: board 6c Ah 6s 7c 7s; my hand Qd7h = full house (sevens full of sixes). This is essentially the nuts on this runout — only rare holdings beat me: pocket A-A (≈3 combos), pocket 6-6 (quads, ≈1 combo) and A7 (Ace + the one remaining 7, ≈3 combos). Flash’s line (preflop raise, small flop c-bet, turn bet) means his range contains a lot of Ax, 6x, sets and bluffs — most of which lose to my full house and will call a value bet. With the pot at 2,800 and my remaining stack 2,900, the EV-maximizing play is to extract maximum value now rather than check and allow showdowns or give him a free card. I will shove all‑in to charge all of his calling hands (Axs, 6x, weaker pairs and bluffs) — the few combos that beat me are rare. Action: bet (all‑in) 2900.

On the river, the board has completed as 6c-Ah-6s-7c-7s. This board provides two pair (Sevens and Sixes with an Ace kicker). My hole cards (Kc-Qc) do not improve this hand, so I am playing the board: A-7-7-6-6. Minni has shoved her remaining 2,900 chips into a 2,800 pot. To call, I would be wagering that Minni is bluffing with a hand that also only plays the board (resulting in a split pot). However, given that she called both the flop and turn, her range is heavily weighted toward hands that beat the board, such as any Ace (making Aces and Sevens), a Six (making Sixes full of Sevens), a Seven (making Sevens full of Sixes), or any pocket pair 88 or higher. Since I can only hope for a split at best and am likely losing to her value range, the mathematically correct decision is to fold.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with J♠2♠: this is a weak but playable suited hand in heads-up where we should be opening a wide range to take the initiative. The pot is small (150) and calling 50 to see a cheap flop is an option, but raising gives fold equity and avoids letting the big, aggressive BB (Flash) see flops cheaply. With 56 BB effective I can play postflop if called — J2s has backdoor spade potential and some showdown value. I’ll open-raise to 300 total (≈3x the BB) to seize the lead; plan is to fold to a large 3-bet, and if called play straightforwardly (C-bet favorable flops, check/pepper pot control on dry or dominated boards).

I hold pocket nines, which is a premium hand in a heads-up situation. My opponent, Minni, has a relatively short stack of 5,400 chips (54 BB). I want to play this hand aggressively for value. I will 3-bet to 900 to build the pot and take the initiative before the flop.

Heads-up on the button I opened to 300 with Js2s and Flash 3-bet to 900. Js2s is weak but has suit/position value heads-up and folds too narrowly would be exploitable vs an aggressive 3-bettor. Effective stacks ~54BB; calling 600 more (900 total) keeps the pot under control, lets me realize equity and use the positional advantage postflop. A 4-bet bluff here would be thin with this holding and folding is too tight given pot odds (~3:1) and opponent’s likely mixed 3-bet range. Plan: call, then c-bet or apply pressure on favorable spade/connected flops, and fold to heavy action on A/K heavy boards or when I have no equity.

The flop of Ah-Kd-4c is quite dangerous for my pocket nines, as it contains two overcards that hit a typical 3-bet calling range very hard. Although I was the pre-flop aggressor, betting into this board is risky because if Minni has any Ace or King, she will likely not fold. With Minni's relatively short stack, I want to avoid bloating the pot when I am likely behind. Checking allows me to control the pot size and see how she reacts to the board.

I called Flash’s 3-bet preflop with Js2s and now see A♠K♦4♣ rainbow. This flop is terrible for my hand and for the caller’s range vs a 3-bet — he’s much more likely to have A‑x, K‑x or other strong broadway hands. I have no pair, only J‑high and only very remote backdoor spade/runner‑runner outs, so bluffing here is thin and a bet will often get raised or called by better hands. With position and the pot at 1,800 I’m going to control the pot and realize equity: check. Plan: fold to significant turn aggression unless I pick up a real draw or pair; if opponent shows continued weakness on later streets I can take the pot away or apply a targeted bluff.

The turn card is a Deuce of Clubs, which is mostly a brick. On the flop (Ah-Kd-4c), both players checked. This check-check sequence suggests that either Minni is also wary of the high cards or she is trapping with a marginal made hand. My pocket nines still have some showdown value against her non-paired hands or smaller pocket pairs. However, betting into an Ace and a King is still risky as I'm unlikely to get better hands to fold or worse hands to call. Checking again is the best way to control the pot and potentially get to a cheap showdown or see how she acts on the turn.

I improved to a single deuce on the turn (pair of 2s), but this is a very weak pair on A♠ K♦ 4♣ 2♣ vs a player who 3‑bet preflop. Flash checked both flop and turn — that line is consistent with pot‑control with A/K or a strong hand he’s slow‑playing, and it’s also how an aggressive player mixes in weaker hands. Betting now has poor risk/reward: I’ll either get called by better hands (Ax/Kx) or run into a check‑raise. With position and a marginal made hand I’ll keep the pot small and realize equity — check and see the river. Plan: call a small river probe if it’s reasonable, fold to large aggression, and if he checks back I’ll consider a small value bet on safe river cards or showdown.

The board has several overcards to my pocket nines (Ace and King). After the flop and turn both went check-check, it is unlikely that Minni has a strong hand like an Ace or a King, but she might still be holding a marginal hand. By checking the river, I am looking to reach a cheap showdown. My pocket nines have significant showdown value against her missed broadway cards or lower pocket pairs. Betting wouldn't accomplish much as better hands are unlikely to fold and worse hands are unlikely to call. I will check and see if she checks back or attempts a bluff.

I have a very weak made hand on the river: pair of deuces (2♠ with 2♣ on turn) with a Jack kicker. Villain 3‑bet preflop (to 900) and then checked flop, turn and river. A preflop 3‑bet range is weighted toward hands that beat me (AA/KK/QQ/AK/AQ and strong broadways) and many of those will simply check‑call a small river probe or call showdown — they won't fold to a small bluff. Betting here for thin value is likely to get called by better hands and folding out only a small portion of the range at best; bluffing has limited fold equity against a 3‑bettor who checked the whole hand. Pot is 1,800 and my stack is 4,800 — I should avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand. Best line is to take the free showdown: check and show if called. If Villain instead puts out a reasonable-sized bet I will fold to large aggression (and consider a call only to VERY small probes), but with the action currently checked to me I check back.

I am on the button with 72o. This is one of the weakest hands in poker. While Minni has a relatively short stack of 4700 (47 big blinds), I have a massive stack of 35150. Even though I have a positional advantage, 72o is not a hand I want to play, especially since Minni has been active in many pots. Folding is the standard and safest play here to avoid unnecessary losses with a trash hand.

Heads-up on the button with Jc3d (48 BB effective). In heads-up play the button opens very wide — J3o is marginal but is still inside a standard opening range. Limping (calling 50) surrenders initiative and often puts me OOP on flops with a weak holding; folding 50 is too passive for this stack size and game. I want the initiative and a cheap, controllable pot so I’ll open-raise to the minimum (to 200 total). This sizes the pot reasonably, applies pressure to the big blind, and keeps my options postflop: c-bet good textures or when I pick up equity, but fold to strong 3-bets or heavy turn action since this hand is easily dominated. If called, plan to play straightforwardly (c-bet favorable flops, check and pot-control on dry/unfavorable boards).

I am in the big blind with 82o, a very weak hand. Minni has raised to 200 from the button (small blind). To call, I only need 100 more chips into a pot that will be 400. However, Minni's stack is quite short (4650), and 82o performs poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Given the high blinds and the weakness of my hand, it is better to fold and wait for a stronger holding rather than bleeding chips by calling with garbage.

I am holding 7h 2c on the Button, which is the weakest possible hand in poker. While I have a significant stack advantage over Minni, who is in the Big Blind with less than 50 BB, there is no reason to enter the pot with such poor equity and playability. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here.

Heads-up on the button (I’ll have postflop position) with Q♥6♣ — a marginal but open-able hand in heads-up play. The pot is small and I have ~49.5BB, so I want to apply pressure and steal the blind while keeping the size low. I’ll make a standard small open to 2BB (200 total) to get fold equity and to play postflop in position. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold this hand; if he calls I’ll c-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously on obvious dangers. 2BB is low risk and maximizes my long-term EV in this spot.

I hold Ace-Jack offsuit in the Big Blind against Minni, who is on the Button with a relatively short stack of 4800 (48 BB). AJo is a premium hand in heads-up play. Minni opened with a min-raise to 200. I will 3-bet to 600 to build the pot and take control of the hand, leveraging my chip lead and the strength of my cards.

I opened to 200 on the button with Q♥6♣ to steal the blind, but Flash 3-bet to 600 (pot now 800) so I must call 400. That 3-bet is large enough that I need ~33% equity to justify a call (400/(800+400)). Q6o is frequently dominated and has poor equity and playability vs a typical 3-bet range (QQ+/AK and strong Broadway combos), and a small 4‑bet would be a thin, unprofitable bluff against this deep, aggressive opponent. I’ll preserve my ~48BB stack and fold to this 3-bet.

I am on the button with Ace-Nine suited, which is a premium starting hand in a heads-up or short-handed situation. My opponent, Minni, has a relatively short stack of 47 big blinds (4700 chips). I will raise to 300 to build the pot and apply pressure, as this hand plays well against most of her defending range.

Heads-up vs a button open to 300. I’m in the BB with Q♦T♦ and a 4,700 stack (~47bb). Calling costs me 200 more (300 total) into a ~400 pot, so I’m getting roughly 3:1 pot odds (~25% equity required). QTs has good equity and playability vs a wide button opening range and also has backdoor flush/straight potential. I’m out of position, so I’d rather keep the pot manageable and realize equity postflop rather than 3-bet OOP with a hand that plays better seeing a flop. A 3-bet to the min (500) won’t gain meaningful fold equity vs a deep, aggressive opponent and a larger 3-bet is unnecessarily committal. Plan: call, evaluate flop — if I pair or get strong draws I’ll play aggressively; on dry A/K-high flops I’ll control pot and fold to heavy pressure. Therefore: call (put 300 total into the pot).

Board: Ts Ah Qc. My hand: Qd Td — I have two pair (Qs and Ts). In heads-up vs the button’s wide opening range this is well ahead of most one-pair and missed broadway hands. I should be building the pot for value while keeping it controlled against the relatively small set of combos that beat me (AA, TT, AQ, AT, KJ/straight combos). Bet for value ~1/2 pot: 300 into 600. This size gets value from Ax, worse Qx and single-pair hands and denies free cards to some draws. If he raises a small-to-medium amount I’ll generally call and reevaluate on the turn; if he jams or raises to a very large, pot-committing size I’ll strongly consider myself behind and proceed cautiously (fold to extreme polarization depending on sizing).

I have top pair with a nine kicker on a highly coordinated board (Ts-Ah-Qc). Minni has led out for 300 into a 600 pot. This 'donk bet' could represent a variety of hands, including weaker Aces, straight draws, or even two-pair combinations. Given the wetness of the board and my medium kicker, raising would likely only get called by hands that beat me or have significant equity. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable, evaluate the turn, and keep her bluffs or weaker hands in the pot.

I have Q♦T♦ on Ts Ah Qc 5♦ — two pair (Qs & Ts). Pot is 1,200 and Flash (button) called my 1/2-pot flop bet, so his continuing range is wide: Ax, Kx, Jx, some Qx and occasional two-pair/set/straight combos (AQ, AT, KJ, QQ, TT, AA). The 5♦ is a blank that doesn’t change my relative strength much — I’m ahead of most single-pair hands and some worse Q/T combos, but behind the small set of two-pair/trips/straight combos. I should extract value and deny free rivers to drawing/underpair hands, so I will bet the turn for value. A ~2/3-pot bet pressures floating/one-pair hands and gets called by worse while keeping the pot manageable facing a big raise given my 4,200 stack. If Flash raises a small/medium amount I’ll call and reevaluate; if he overbet-shoves a pot-committing amount I’ll view it as a polarized value range (AQ/AT/AA/QQ/TT) and fold. Action: bet ~2/3 pot = 800 chips.

I have top pair with an Ace, but my kicker (nine) is relatively weak on this board containing a Ten and a Queen. Minni is showing significant strength by leading out on both the flop and the turn. However, with top pair in a heads-up-style situation, I cannot fold yet. Calling allows me to see a river card and evaluate Minni's final action. Since Minni's remaining stack is small, a call here effectively prepares for a potential showdown or a decision on the river, while a raise would likely only be called by hands that have me beaten (like AJ, AT, or a straight).

Board Ts Ah Qc 5d Qs with my Qd Td: I have Queens full of Tens (full house, QQQTT) — effectively the nuts vs most of Flash’s calling range. Flash opened pre, then called my 300 flop bet and 800 turn bet, so his continuing range is wide (lots of A‑x, Kx, Qx, some Tx, medium pairs). Very few combos beat me (mainly pocket Aces or the rare Q+A combo); many worse hands will call a large river bet. Betting is mandatory for value — checking surrenders value to weaker hands. I want to extract maximum value from Ax, Tx, weaker Qx and other holdings that will call a big bet. Because Flash is sticky (called flop and turn) and has a very deep stack, I’ll charge him the maximum I can: shove my stack to get called by worse hands and deny turnaround bluffs. Risk of running into the tiny number of hands that beat me is small compared to the value I can extract. Action: bet my remaining stack (all‑in) for 3400.

On a board of Ts-Ah-Qc-5d-Qs, I hold Ad-9d, giving me two pair (Aces and Queens) with a Ten kicker from the board. Minni has led out on every street, culminating in a river overbet that effectively puts her all-in. On this board, many hands in a standard calling or donk-betting range beat me: any Queen (trips), KJ (straight), and better Aces like AK or AJ (whose kickers are higher than the Ten on the board). If Minni has a lower Ace or AT, we split the pot. Given the strength and sizing shown by Minni, I am likely behind or at best splitting. In a high-stakes scenario with a significant stack advantage, making a hero call against a polarized overbet in this spot is mathematically disadvantageous.

Heads-up on the button with 2♥5♣ — a weak hand, but in heads-up play the button should open a very wide range to exploit positional advantage. Pot is 150 and I only need to put another 150 (total 200) to make a standard open. I’ll raise to 200 (small, non-giving sizing) to generate fold equity, keep the pot manageable if called, and play postflop in position. If Flash 3-bets I’ll fold — this hand has poor playability OOP vs aggression. Calling is too passive (gives BB cheap equity) and folding concedes my positional edge. Raise to 200.

I am in the Big Blind with 4 of Spades and 6 of Spades. Minni has min-raised to 200 from the Small Blind. Given the pot odds (calling 100 to play for a 300 pot) and the suited nature of my hand, this is a standard defend. Minni is playing a relatively short stack of 6000 chips, so I have good implied odds if I hit a strong draw or two pair, while the cost to see the flop is minimal.

I am in the Big Blind with 4 of Spades and 6 of Spades on a King-Jack-Nine flop. I have no pair and no straight draw. While I have a very weak backdoor spade draw, this board connects well with Minni's Small Blind raising range (which includes many Kings, Jacks, and Tens). I will check to see what the action is.

I opened very wide on the button with 2♥5♣ so my flop range is very strong and I should c-bet frequently. The flop K♥-9♣-J♥ misses my hand (only a tiny backdoor heart/fold blocker with 2♥), but Flash checked showing weakness. I’ll make a small, constructive continuation bet (≈1/2 pot = 200) to take the pot now against many missed hands and weaker pairs while keeping the pot controllable if called. If Flash check‑raises I will fold — I have no pair and only a backdoor draw. If called I’ll reassess on the turn (continue only with picks or when board texture improves).

I have 4s6s on a Kh-9c-Jh board. This flop connects very well with Minni's pre-flop raising range, as it contains three high cards and multiple straight draws. I have no pair, no straight draw, and no flush draw. There is no reason to continue with this hand as I have virtually zero equity and very little room to outplay the opponent on such a connected texture. I will fold.
In a heads-up situation, Queen-Six offsuit is a standard hand to open from the button (Small Blind). I will raise to 300 to put pressure on Minni in the Big Blind and take control of the pot early.

Heads-up in the big blind vs a 300 SB open, 9♠8♦ is a hand I want to defend. Calling (putting 200 more for a total of 300) gives ~25% pot odds to see a flop and preserves postflop flexibility with a connected hand that can hit straights and pairs. Flash is deep and aggressive in spots, so a small 3-bet to 500 would commit extra chips with a marginal holding; flatting keeps his range wide and lets me play positionally postflop. Plan: see the flop and continue aggressively when I connect (pair, two pair, straight or strong draws); on dry A/K boards proceed cautiously and fold to large aggression if I miss.

I defended 9♠8♦ preflop for playability, but this flop (A♠ 4♣ 10♦) is very unfavorable — I’ve missed completely (no pair, no real straight/flush draw). The preflop raiser’s range contains a lot of A‑x, T‑x and broadways that connect with this board, so my fold equity is low and bluffing is thin against a deep, aggressive opponent. Pot control is the priority: I’ll check to keep the pot small, preserve stack, and fold to significant aggression. If Flash checks back I can realize equity and look to improve; if he fires a small probe I may occasionally float, but I will give up to larger bets and only continue with a clear improvement (pair/two‑pair/strong draw).

On this dry Ace-high board, a continuation bet is standard to represent an Ace or a Ten. Since I raised pre-flop, I have the range advantage. I will bet 300 (half pot) to put pressure on Minni's marginal hands and likely fold out her air and low pocket pairs.

I defended preflop with 9♠8♦ for playability, but this flop (A♠ 4♣ 10♦) completely misses my hand. Villain bets 300 into a 600 pot (pot now 900); calling 300 gets me 300/1200 = 25% break‑even equity. I only have ~6 clean outs to pair by the river (≈24% to hit by river) and any pair I make will frequently be second‑best to Ax or Tx combos in his c‑betting range. There are negligible backdoor draws and Flash is deep and capable of barreling with value, so this is a marginal spot to invest more chips. Pot control and stack preservation are the priorities — I’ll fold and wait for a better, more profitable spot to contest.

Heads-up on the button with Q9o — a playable, positionally strong hand. Limping/calling 50 surrenders initiative to a very active BB (Flash) who defends and plays postflop aggressively; min-raising to 200 is too small and easily exploited. I’ll open to 300 total (3x BB): standard heads-up sizing that applies pressure, gives fold equity, builds a pot I can play in position, and preserves most of my 6,050 stack for postflop play. If Flash 3-bets large I can fold; I can continue or call smaller 3-bets depending on sizing and flop texture.

I am in the Big Blind with T5o. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300, leaving her with a stack of 5500. While T5o is a very weak hand, I only need to call 200 more into a pot of 400. However, out of position against Minni, who has been relatively aggressive, T5o will be difficult to play post-flop. Given that Minni's stack is quite short (about 58 big blinds total), I don't want to bleed chips calling with marginal trash. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with Ace-Seven offsuit. My opponent, Minni, has a relatively short stack of 6100 (61 Big Blinds). Ace-high is a very strong hand in heads-up play, and I want to put pressure on the big blind. A standard raise to 300 is consistent with my previous play and will likely either win the blinds or allow me to play a pot with a range advantage.

Heads-up in the big blind with 4c4d facing a button open to 300. Pot is 400 and it’s 200 to me (total 300 committed). With ~61BB effective and the opponent much deeper, this hand is a clear set-mining candidate against a wide SB opening range. Calling is the cheapest, most +EV line: I keep the pot manageable, preserve options, and gain large implied odds if I hit a set. A small 3-bet here (min to 500) is workable sometimes to take initiative, but I’d be out of position postflop and could face bigger aggression or a 4-bet from a deep-stacked, aggressive opponent — not worth inflating the pot with just a low pocket pair. Fold is too tight given the price. Plan: call to see the flop; fold to heavy, polarized pressure if I miss and villain barrels big; extract max value if I flop a set (check/raise or slow-play depending on texture).

Heads-up on a dry, paired flop (5c 2h 2d). I hold 4c4d — second pair. Villain (SB opener) has a wide range that contains many overcards and some small pairs; the hands that currently beat me are mostly 5x and pocket pairs >44 (and the rare pocket 2s/quads). Flash checked the flop, showing some weakness or a desire to pot-control. I want to extract value from worse hands and deny equity to any backdoor outs while keeping the pot manageable OOP. A small c-bet (~1/3 pot) accomplishes that: it folds a lot of pure overcards/floaters, gets called by worse pairs and Ax/Kx that I beat, and avoids bloating the pot against stronger made hands. If I get a raise, I’ll give credit to a polarized, strong range (5x, 66+, or a set/quads) and fold; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (call small bets, fold to large, and realize showdown if the turn is a blank). Betting larger here is unnecessary and checking surrenders fold equity and value. Action: small value/protection bet of 200 chips (~1/3 pot).

On a very dry board of 5-2-2, Minni has led out for a small bet of 200 into a 600 pot. My Ace-high (Ac 7d) retains significant value on this texture, as it beats all her air and missed draws. While she could have a 5 or a small pocket pair, her donk-bet range also includes many weaker holdings. I am getting 4:1 odds to call, and my hand has enough showdown value and potential to improve to continue. I will call to keep her range wide and see how the turn develops.

I hold 4c4d — a medium pocket pair — on 5c 2h 2d Kd turn (paired board with a King). Flash checked the turn after calling my small flop bet; that line is consistent with either a pot-control Kx/Ax, a defensive call with a small pair (5x/33–66), or a trap with 22. The King is a dangerous card for my hand (it brings many combos that now beat me), and I’m out of position against a very deep-stacked opponent. Betting here bloats the pot with a vulnerable hand and risks a check-raise or committing lots of chips when I’m often behind; checking preserves my stack and lets me realize equity. Plan: check and re-evaluate on the river — call small river bets for pot odds or fold to large aggression; if I improve (trip 4s) I can extract value then.

On the turn, the board has paired deuces and an overcard King has appeared. Minni, who led the flop with a small donk bet, has checked. This often indicates a marginal hand or a surrender. My Ace-Seven still has decent showdown value against her range, which could include missed draws or lower air. By checking back, I control the pot size and can see a free river. I am not likely to get called by worse if I bet, and I'd rather not face a check-raise from a short stack.

I c-bet the flop for value/protection with 4♣4♦ and checked the King turn to avoid bloating the pot against hands that now beat me. Flash called my flop bet and checked the turn, which is consistent with pot control/Ax/Kx/medium pairs (5x, 33–66) or showdown-ready weak hands. The river 8♣ is a blank; I still only have second pair (44). Betting here has very thin value — worse hands (A-high, some unpaired hands) may call a small bet, but many better hands (Kx, 5x, 66+, 22) will also call or raise, and I don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal hand out of position against a deep-stacked opponent. I’ll check to take it to showdown and avoid getting crushed by a value raise; if Flash then fires a small river bet I’ll call for pot odds, but I’ll fold to large river aggression.

The board is 5-2-2-K-8 and the action has gone check-check on both the turn and the river after Minni led the flop. My Ace-high (Ac 7d) has significant showdown value in a heads-up pot against an opponent who has checked twice. Minni's flop donk-bet followed by two checks suggests a weak hand or a missed draw. There is very little value in betting, as I am unlikely to get called by a worse hand, and checking back allows me to realize my equity and win the pot if my Ace-high is best.

Heads-up on the button with A♠8♥ is a clear open-raise — strong Ace, good kicker, and I have positional advantage. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x the BB / consistent with my typical opens) rather than just completing to 100 or min-raising to 200 to seize initiative, apply pressure to weaker holdings, and get value from worse Ax and broadway hands. Stack depth (~66 BB) lets me play postflop in position comfortably; plan is to c-bet favorable textures, control the pot on dangerous boards, and re-evaluate vs big 3-bets or heavy action.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 5h. Minni, who has a relatively short stack of 6,400 chips, has raised to 300 from the Button/Small Blind. The pot odds are favorable (200 to call into a 400 pot, requiring 33% equity). 95s is a hand that can play reasonably well post-flop, and given our massive chip lead over Minni, calling to see a flop and potentially outplaying her or hitting a strong draw is a sound strategy.

I have middle pair (fives) on a dry King-high board (Kh-5s-2c). Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to the raiser and evaluate their action. Middle pair is strong enough to call a standard continuation bet, but not strong enough to lead out into the pre-flop raiser.

I c-bet this dry K-5-2 rainbow board to take advantage of position, fold out lots of unpaired hands and worse broadways, and extract value from any A-high or weaker Ax/Queen-type holdings. I have A♠8♥ which blocks some AK combos and has good showdown value vs the BB’s wide defending range, so a medium-sized bet gives me both fold equity and protection for my hand. I’ll size to ~2/3 pot (400) — big enough to charge floats and small pairs but small enough to limit loss if I face a check-raise from sets or Kx. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (continue on blanks, pot-control or fold to strong aggression if a K or heavy action appears).

I have middle pair (fives) on a dry board of Kh-5s-2c. Minni bet 400 into a 600 pot. Middle pair is generally too strong to fold to a single continuation bet on such a board, especially when getting nearly 2.5:1 on a call. Raising would likely only get called by better hands (like a King or a set) and fold out her bluffs, so calling is the most profitable line to see a turn and keep her wider range in the pot.

I have middle pair (fives) on a board of Kh-5s-2c-6c. On the turn, the 6c is relatively blank, although it adds some flush and straight possibilities. Since I am out of position and my hand is a medium-strength showdown hand, checking is the best play to control the pot size and potentially induce bluffs or see a river card cheaply. I am not strong enough to bet for value against Minni's likely range.

I have A♠8♥ on 2♣-5♠-K♥-6♣. I c-bet the dry K-5-2 flop and villain called, so his range is likely heavy on Kx, some small pair (5s/2s), and a bunch of showdown-calling hands (Qx/Jx/Ax) — he checked the turn, which looks like pot control with Kx or a medium pair. I only have Ace-high (no pair) and I block some AK combos, but I am still behind most Kx hands. Betting the turn would be a pure bluff with limited fold equity (he called the flop and will often call turn bets), and it risks bloating the pot out of position while facing a lot of calls or a check-raise. With ~60BB effective and a pot of 1400, the prudent line is pot control: check and take a free river evaluation. Plan: if he checks river I can show down; if he bets small I will call for showdown frequency, and fold to large river aggression unless I pair up or pick up a clear improvement.

On the river, the board is 2c-5s-Kh-6c-Tc. I have a pair of fives, which is a marginal showdown hand. Minni checked behind on the turn after I checked to her, which suggests she might be holding a weak King, a medium pair, or is giving up on a bluff. There's little value in betting here, as I'm unlikely to be called by worse hands, and I don't want to turn my hand into a bluff against her remaining stack. Checking is the most prudent action to reach showdown and realize my equity.

I check. I only have Ace-high (A♠8♥) on 2♣-5♠-K♥-6♣-T♣ after c-betting the flop and getting called. Flash’s line (call flop, check turn) looks like Kx, a small pair, or a wide showdown-calling range — many of those hands will call a river probe. The river T♣ also completes club draws and pairs some hands, so my bluff would have very limited fold equity while risking chips out of position. Pot is 1,400 and I have ~6,000 behind; thinly bluffing here is negative EV given his tendencies. Checking preserves stack, lets me show down the Ace-high vs pure air or induce a bluff from him, and avoids getting raised off a non-made hand. If he now bets small I’ll call for showdown frequency; I’ll fold to large river aggression.

I am on the button with 98o and a significant chip lead over Minni. This is a standard opening range hand in a heads-up or short-handed scenario. I will raise to 300 to maintain my aggressive strategy and exert pressure on Minni's smaller stack.

Flash opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the big blind with KcTh — a strong, defendable heads‑up hand but out of position. The pot is 400 and I need to add 200 (total committed 300) to call. With ~59bb effective I don’t want to bloat the pot or get into a 4‑bet war with a marginal 3‑bet hand; calling preserves stack and realizes equity against what is likely a wide opening range from the button. Plan: see the flop and proceed for value on K/T or good draw flops, fold to heavy commitment on dry, unfavorable boards. Call 300.

I’m first to act out of position with T♥K♣ on 2♣ T♦ 6♥ — top pair with a very strong kicker on a relatively dry board. Button’s opening range is wide heads‑up, so this hand is worth leading for thin value and to deny equity to overcards. I don’t want to bloat the pot with ~59bb effective, so a medium-sized bet around 1/2 pot will extract calls from worse tens, Kx and some Ax/Kx hands while folding pure air. If Flash raises large I’ll give heavy consideration to folding since the board is dry and his raise will polarize to better tens/overpairs/sets. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn based on the card and sizing.

On this flop of 10-6-2, I have a gutshot straight draw with my 9-8. Minni has led out for half the pot (300 into 600). The pot odds are 3-to-1, which is reasonable to see a turn card, especially in a heads-up situation where her lead might represent a wide range including mid-pairs, a six, or even a pure bluff. I will call the 300 and re-evaluate on the turn based on whether I improve or if she continues her aggression.

I have K♣ T♥ on 2♣ T♦ 6♥ K♠ — two pair (Kings and Tens) heads‑up. The pot is 1,200 and Flash called my flop bet, so his continuing range is wide (Tx, Kx, small pairs, occasionally bluffs). The turn King puts me well ahead of almost everything he called the flop with (single Ks or Ts, middle pairs); the board is otherwise pretty dry with no meaningful draws to worry about. I should extract value now rather than give a free river card. A 2/3‑pot value bet (~800) accomplishes that: it prices Kx, Tx and most pocket pairs into calling, is small enough not to fold out plenty of worse hands, and doesn’t overcommit my ~54bb stack if I run into a set. If Flash raises big I’ll treat it as a polarized line (likely set/monster) and re‑evaluate — call a modest raise, respect an extreme shove depending on sizing and read. Action: bet 800 to get value and deny equity.

Minni has shown continuous aggression by leading into me on both the flop and the turn. On this board of 2-T-6-K, my 9-high holds very little showdown value, and my only real path to winning is hitting a 7 for a straight (4 outs). The pot odds of 3.5-to-1 are not sufficient to justify chasing a gutshot, especially given Minni's commitment to the pot and her short stack, which limits my implied odds. Since I have a massive chip lead in the overall game, I will fold and wait for a more favorable spot.

Heads-up on the button with 2dTd (T2s) and 65BB effective: this is a marginal hand but perfectly fine to open in position. I want to seize initiative, apply pressure to the big blind (Flash) and realize my postflop positional advantage, so I’ll open-raise rather than limp or fold. I’ll size to 300 (standard table open size here) to get reasonable fold equity while keeping the pot manageable with a speculative hand. Versus a 3-bet I’ll exercise caution and likely fold; if called I’ll play postflop in position and look to continuation-bet/fold when appropriate.

In a heads-up or short-handed situation, 4s 5c is a marginal but acceptable hand to defend the Big Blind against a standard 3x raise, especially with pot odds of 2:1. Minni's stack is relatively short (63BB), meaning if I hit a strong draw or two pair, there is good potential for a significant payout. I will call the 200 to see the flop and re-evaluate based on the board texture.
