On a flop of 4c-7d-3c, I have second pair (fours) and a gutshot straight draw (needs a 6). While this is a decent piece of the board, I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) here is generally inefficient as it allows the opponent to play perfectly. Checking is the standard play to see how Minni reacts to this low, coordinated texture. If Minni bets, I have a very comfortable call given my pair and straight draw equity.

I opened to 300 preflop and was called. The flop 4c-7d-3c is very dry and overall favors my IP raising range. I hold Td2d — only ten-high with a backdoor diamond possibility (one diamond on board), so I have little showdown value but I do have position and initiative. Flash checked, so a standard continuation bet should fold out many of his unpaired overcards and small float hands while keeping the pot controllable if called. I’ll make a half-pot c‑bet (300). Plan: if he raises big I’ll fold and preserve my stack; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and only continue with improvement (pair/diamond) or a very good turn card.

I have middle pair (fours) and a gutshot straight draw on a board of 7-4-3. Minni has bet 300 into a pot of 600, giving me 3:1 pot odds. My hand has significant equity against a wide button-opening range, and folding would be a mistake. I will call to see the turn and re-evaluate, as raising might bloat the pot against a short-stacked opponent when I only have a marginal holding.

I have middle pair (fours) and a gutshot straight draw on a board of 4c-7d-3c-9c. The nine of clubs completes potential flushes. Being first to act post-flop, checking is the standard play with a marginal hand to control the pot size and observe the opponent's reaction to the flush-completing card. This approach prevents me from bloating the pot when I am likely either way ahead or way behind.

I c‑bet the dry 4c‑7d‑3c flop and was called; the turn 9c completes a three‑club board. I hold Td2d with no club and no pair — only ten‑high — so my showdown equity is very poor versus a caller-heavy range that includes many clubs. Betting here would bloat the pot against a lot of made flushes and put me in tough spots; raising is a bluff with virtually no fold equity given the completed draw. I’ll exercise pot control and check. Plan: fold to any sizable lead/raise (likely value flush); if Flash checks back we go to the river and can consider a bluff only if a very favorable card (diamond or T) appears.

On a final board of 4c-7d-3c-9c-8h, I have a pair of fours. While Minni checked back on the turn, which often indicates a hand with marginal showdown value or a check-back with a draw, my pair of fours is near the bottom of my range. Checking is the most prudent action. If Minni bets, I will have to evaluate the size, but checking allows me to potentially see a cheap showdown if she also checks, or fold without losing more chips if she has a superior hand like a seven, eight, or nine.

I c‑bet the dry 4♣‑7♦‑3♣ flop with T♦2♦ and was called. The turn 9♣ completed the three‑club board and villain checked; the river 8♥ is a blank for my hand. I have no clubs and no pair — only ten‑high — so my showdown equity is essentially zero against a calling range that contains clubs, pairs, and the occasional straight. Betting here would be a thin bluff with poor fold equity (villain already called the flop) and would bloat the pot unnecessarily when I have no real outs. With 6k behind and a 1.2k pot, pot control is the right line: check and concede to any river bet. Action: check.

I have been dealt pocket nines, which is a very strong starting hand in a heads-up situation. My opponent, Minni, has a relatively short stack of 59 big blinds. I will open with a standard raise to 300 to build the pot and assert initiative.

Call. Facing a 300 open from the button (SB) with 5♠4♠ in the big blind I need to put 200 more to make my total contribution 300. That call risks 200 to win a 600 pot, so I need ~33% equity. 5-4s is a strong defend in heads-up: suited, connected, with good straight/flush potential and useful blockers to some of SB’s broadway/A-high combos. Flash has shown wide/aggressive opening tendencies and is very deep; 3-betting as a bluff risks bloating the pot out of position or getting 4-bet/isolated unnecessarily. With ~59BB effective it’s better to defend and see a flop where I can realize implied odds and play multi-street. I’ll call to 300 and evaluate continuation on the flop.

I have bottom pair (4s) with a 5 kicker on 4d-9d-8c. Heads-up vs Flash (who opens very wide and c-bets a lot) this hand has some showdown value but is vulnerable: the board is somewhat connected and there are two diamonds out which enable opponents’ backdoor diamond turn/river play. I’m out of position and the pot is only 600. Leading here would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and turn it into a multi-street battle against a deeper, aggressive opponent. Checking keeps the pot under control, lets Flash c-bet wide so I can call small bets to realize equity (and fold to large pressure), and allows me to re-evaluate on the turn (pot-control or extract value if the action and runout favor me). Default line: check — call small c-bets, fold to large raises, consider a small turn stab if checked to me on safe cards.

I have flopped top set with my pocket nines on a 4d-9d-8c board. This is an incredibly strong position. The board is somewhat coordinated with a flush draw and some straight draws (like 6-7 or 5-6), so I want to bet for value and to charge any draws Minni might have. Given the pot is 600 and Minni has about 5700 remaining, I will place a standard continuation bet of 400 to build the pot and set up for a potential stack-off on later streets.

Call. I have bottom pair (4♠) with a 5 kicker on 4♦-9♦-8♣. Flash is a very wide, aggressive opener and has c-bet small (400 into a 1,000 pot), which gives me roughly 400/(1,000+400) ≈ 28.6% break‑even equity — well within reach given how wide his range is. Out of position I prefer pot control and to realize showdown value rather than inflate the pot with a marginal hand by raising. Plan: call the 400, see the turn, fold to large turn/river aggression I can’t beat, and only expand action if I improve or get a cheap card and he shows weakness.

Check. I have bottom pair (4♠) with a weak 5-kicker on 4♦-9♦-8♣-Q♣. I only have ~5 outs to meaningfully improve (two 4s, three 5s), so my river equity is poor and this is a classic pot‑control spot heads‑up. Flash opened wide and c‑bet small on the flop then checked the turn — he can have a lot of air, some overcards, medium pairs or draws. Betting here polarizes my range and risks a raise that I should usually fold to, while checking keeps the pot manageable, lets me call small river bets as a bluff‑catcher, and allows me to bluff or extract thin value on the river depending on the card and Flash’s action.

On the turn, I still hold top set with my pocket nines. The board is now 4d-9d-8c-Qc. The Queen is a good card for me as it might have improved Minni to a top pair, which she is unlikely to fold, or it provides more straight/flush draw equity that she might be willing to pay for. With a pot of 1400 and Minni having 5300 remaining, I will bet 1000 to continue building the pot and extract value from her drawing range or any pairs she might be holding. This sizing also sets up a comfortable river shove if she calls.

Call 1000. Pot is 2400 and Flash bet 1000; calling 1000 to win ~4400 requires ~22.7% equity. I have bottom pair (4♠) with a weak kicker but against a very wide, aggressive button his turn-barrel range contains lots of bluffs, floats and overcards that I currently beat or can call down. Raising out of position with this marginal hand would bloat the pot and play badly against his frequency and stack depth. Plan: call to keep the pot under control, see the river and fold to meaningful river over‑bets if I don’t improve; call small river bets to catch bluffs or thin value.

Check. The river paired the board (9♦/9♥) and I only have bottom pair (4♠) with a weak 5‑kicker. Flash is a very wide, aggressive player who bet the flop and barreled the turn then checked the river — that can be weakness or a trap, but my hand is marginal and has decent showdown value vs his wide checking range (missed broadways, floats and failed diamond draws). Betting as a bluff risks getting called by many worse hands (A/K/J highs) and getting raised by the hands that beat me (Qx or any nine), which would commit too much of my short stack. Pot control + showdown is the best line here: check and see a showdown, call a small river bet but fold to large raises.

On the river, the board is 4d-9d-8c-Qc-9h, and I have made quads (four of a kind, nines). This is the absolute nuts given the board texture. The pot is 3,400, and my opponent, Minni, has 4,300 chips remaining in her stack. Since I am first to act (or in this case, she checked to me), and she has consistently called my bets on the flop and turn, I want to maximize value. A check-behind would be a disaster here. I will bet enough to put her all-in, as any hand she has called with so far (like top pair, two pair, or a missed draw she might feel compelled to bluff-catch with) is likely to call. Therefore, I will bet her remaining stack of 4,300 to capture all of her chips.

Pot before river was 3,400. Flash fires 4,300 into it; if I call 4,300 the final pot becomes 12,000. Break‑even equity = 4,300 / 12,000 ≈ 35.8%.

My hand: 5♠4♠ — bottom pair (pair of 4s) with a weak kicker. On the river there are no more cards to come; I can only win if Flash is bluffing or holding a worse one‑pair hand. What beats me: any 9, any Q (Qc on the turn), any 8, or any overpair/pocket pair — all very plausible given Flash’s line (small flop c‑bet, turn barrel, now a very large river overbet). Flash is aggressive and capable of bluffing, but this particular sizing (an overbet >1x pot after two prior barrels) strongly polarizes toward value (trips/Qx/two‑pair/overpairs) rather than a high enough frequency of bluffs to give me the ~36% equity I need.

Beyond the math, calling here commits my entire 4,300 stack in a cash game with 37 hands left — preserving chips to exploit his aggression in later spots is better than spewing an all‑in call with a marginal showdown hand. Given pot odds, the board texture, his line and sizing, folding is the correct +EV play.

Open‑raise to take the initiative. Heads‑up on the button I should be opening very wide — 6♥2♥ is marginal but suited, has blocker value and good postflop playability in position. With ~42.5bb effective a small standard open to 2xBB (200 total) applies pressure, gains fold equity and keeps the pot manageable. Plan: fold to a large 3‑bet, but if called use position to c‑bet favorable flops, probe with pressure, and realize equity on backdoor hearts or pairs. Raising maximizes long‑term expected value versus just completing for 50.

I am in the Big Blind with Qc 5c. Minni, who is on the button, has raised to 200. I need to call 100 more to see a flop. The pot is offering me 3:1 odds, and Qc 5c is a suited hand with some playability. Minni's stack is relatively short at 41 BBs, so I want to see a flop and realize my equity without bloating the pot pre-flop.

I am out of position in the Big Blind. The flop comes King-high (Kd 6d 2s), which generally favors the pre-flop raiser's range. I have Queen-high with no direct draws, although I have some backdoor potential with the clubs and a runner-runner straight. Standard play here is to check to the aggressor and see how they respond.

I have 6♥2♥ and flopped two pair on K♦ 6♦ 2♠. Heads‑up this is a very strong hand and Flash has shown a lot of calling/check‑call tendencies, so I want to extract value and deny equity to turn cards. Flash checked to me, so a value/protection bet of ~3/4 pot will charge Kx, single pairs, medium pairs and the rare pocket‑diamond combo with a 4‑card diamond draw while keeping worse hands and calls in. I’ll bet 300 into the 400 pot; if called I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn, and if I face a large check‑raise I’ll assume sets/K6/K2 and proceed cautiously.

I am in the Big Blind with Qc 5c. The flop is Kd 6d 2s, and Minni has bet 300 into a pot of 400. My hand is currently just Queen-high with very little potential to improve to the best hand on this board. Given that this board texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range and I have no pair or significant draw, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have QJ of diamonds on the button. This is a very strong hand in heads-up play, especially against a short-stacked opponent like Minni (44BB). I will raise to 300 to build the pot and apply pressure.

Heads-up vs button open to 300. Pot is 400 and I must call 200 (pot odds ≈33%). With J7o in the big blind I should defend rather than fold — heads-up the opener’s range is wide and J7o has decent showdown value and decent playability (can make top pair or improvise with backdoor draws). A 3-bet with this hand is too thin (I don’t have the blockers or strength to polarize profitably), and folding would be too exploitable vs a wide button. I’ll call to see a flop, keep the pot controllable out of position, and plan to fold to heavy turn/river aggression if I miss; continue/raise when I hit top pair or strong draws.

I defended preflop with J♥7♣ versus a wide button open and missed this dry K♣ Q♠ 8♦ rainbow on the flop — no pair, no real draws and no meaningful blockers. Leading here would bloat the pot out of position into an opponent who c-bets and raises frequently; I don’t have the equity or fold-equity to make that profitable. I’ll check to control the pot (current pot 600), induce bluffs, and realize equity cheaply. Plan: if Flash makes a small c-bet (≈≤1/3 pot, ~200) I will call to see a turn; I will fold to medium/large cbets (>~200–300) unless I improve to a pair or get a very favorable runout. If Flash checks back I’ll take a free turn and re-evaluate. This line preserves my stack and avoids difficult OOP decisions with air.

I have middle pair (Queens) with a Jack kicker on a King-high board. Minni has checked to me after calling my pre-flop raise. While a King is in her range, middle pair is quite strong in a heads-up pot. I will place a standard continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) for value and to protect my hand against overcards like an Ace or straight draws like J-T or T-9.

Flop (K♣ Q♠ 8♦): I checked OOP with J♥7♣ and Flash bets 300 into the 600 pot (pot = 900). Calling 300 to see a turn gives me ~4:1 on the money (I need ≈25% equity). Heads‑up the button’s c‑bet range is wide and contains a lot of missed A‑high/broadway hands and small pairs, so J7o has reasonable showdown value and some backdoor outs. Raising OOP with no pair or meaningful blockers is too thin and will bloat the pot; folding here would be overly nitty given the pot odds and villain’s wide tendencies. I’ll call to realize equity cheaply, keep the pot controllable, and re‑evaluate on the turn: fold to heavy continued aggression if I don’t improve, but continue or apply pressure if I pair or pick up a strong draw.

I improved on the turn — Jc pairs my Jh, giving me middle pair on K♣ Q♠ 8♦ J♣. Flash checked the turn after c-betting the flop, which smells like weakness/pot‑control from a wide c‑betting range. Heads‑up my pair of jacks has decent value vs his checked range (will get called by A‑high, smaller pairs, T‑x, some broadway hands) and I also want to charge/deter straight/2‑club combos that can outdraw me on the river. I’ll bet for value and protection but keep the pot manageable: bet ~1/2 pot (600 into 1200). If he check‑raises big (polarizing), I won’t automatically commit — I’ll fold to large pressure with this kicker against likely K/Q/two‑pair/straight lines and only continue vs a small raise or a read of a bluff. If called, I’ll re‑evaluate the river based on the card and his action.

On the turn, I have improved to two pair (Queens and Jacks). Minni has led out for 600 into the 1200 pot (a donk bet). While the board is now very connected and any Ten (T9, AT, KT, QT, JT) makes a straight, my two pair is still a very strong hand in heads-up play. Folding is out of the question given the strength of my hand and the pot odds. Raising might only isolate me against hands that beat me (like straights or sets). Calling allows me to keep her range wide, control the size of the pot, and re-evaluate on the river, especially since her remaining stack is relatively small.

Board K♣ Q♠ 8♦ J♣ K♠; my hand J♥7♣. My best five is K K J J Q — two pair, kings and jacks. That strength is respectable heads‑up, but the river pairing of kings makes many villain combos (any Qx -> KQ two pair; any Kx -> trips/quads; pocket QQ/JJ/88 -> full house) beat me. Flash’s line (open, c‑bet, calling my turn bet) is consistent with lots of Qx and some Ks/pairs that will now both check‑control and beat my hand. Betting here would mainly fold the air and get called by the hands that actually beat me, so it’s thin value with significant downside if I get raised. With stacks and the pot size I’d rather keep the pot small and see showdown. I’ll check and call reasonable river bets, folding to large, polarized shoves that are almost certainly full houses/quads.

On the river, the board has paired the King (K-Q-J-8-K). My best hand is two pair, Kings and Queens (K-K-Q-Q-J). While this is a strong hand in heads-up play, the board is very dangerous. Any Ten (A-T, K-T, Q-T, J-T, T-9) makes a straight, and any King makes trips. Minni's donk bet on the turn suggested she either had a piece of the board or a draw; her check on the river after the board paired the King could be a trap or a sign of caution with a straight. Since I have high showdown value but am unlikely to get called by worse hands (like a Jack or a lower Queen), checking back is the best play to realize my equity and avoid walking into a check-raise from a King or a straight.

Heads-up on the button with K♠Q♦ (KQ offsuit) and 32.5BB effective, this is a clear open-raise spot. I want to take the initiative, isolate the big blind and use my postflop position. A standard 3x-ish open is appropriate here — big enough to get folding equity and to deny cheap flops to weak hands, but small enough to keep pot control with 32BB. I’ll raise to 300 total. If Flash 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate based on size and frequency (likely fold to very large 3-bets, call smaller ones and play position postflop); if he calls I plan to continuation-bet favorable textures and extract value when I hit top pair or good draws.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Six offsuit. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind/Button. Looking at the stack sizes, Minni is very short with only 3,000 chips remaining (30 big blinds). While T6o is a weak hand, I only need to call 200 more into a pot of 400. However, against a short stack, their opening range is often tighter or they are looking to commit. Given my significant chip lead (36,600 vs 3,000), I don't need to defend with marginal hands that play poorly out of position. T6o is easily dominated and doesn't hit many boards well. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip advantage.

I am on the button with Ace-Trey offsuit. With a massive chip lead over Minni, who only has about 33 big blinds remaining, I want to keep applying pressure. A3o is a standard opening hand from the Small Blind in a heads-up or short-handed situation. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard open that forces Minni to either defend with a wide range or fold her blinds.

I’m in the big blind with K♠6♣ facing a 300 open from the SB (Flash). Pot is 400 and I need 200 more to call — that means committing 300 total (including my posted 100). Calling 200 into 400 gives break-even equity of ~200/(400+200)=33%. K6 is marginal but has reasonable equity against a wide SB stealing range, and big-blind defense heads-up is standard. With my stack only 3,300 (33 BB) and Flash deeply stacked and often aggressive, I don’t want to inflate the pot with a thin 3-bet or shove with a dominated kicker. I’ll call to see a flop, keep the pot manageable, and play cautiously out of position: fold to large continuation bets when missed, and continue only with top-pair/strong draws or good flop texture.

Flop is Q♦ Q♠ 6♥ and I hold K♠6♣ — I have second pair (pair of sixes) with a decent kicker but I'm out of position against an aggressive SB opener. Flash opened preflop from the button and shows a wide, aggressive range, so he will continuation-bet a lot; betting myself here would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and put me in difficult spots on later streets. I choose pot control: check and see what he does. Plan: call small-to-medium c-bets (roughly ≤ ~1/2 pot) to realize equity and keep weaker hands in, but fold to large, polarizing c-bets or multi-barrels unless I improve to two pair/trips. If he checks back I'll evaluate a small delayed bet on the turn or continue to control the pot depending on the card and his tendencies. Preserving my ~31BB stack vs his deep stack is important — avoid committing with just second pair OOP.

I hold Ace-high on a very dry, paired board (Qd-Qs-6h). As the pre-flop aggressor, this board texture favors my range significantly. Minni checked to me, which is expected. I will place a standard continuation bet of half the pot (300) to represent a Queen or a pocket pair, and to fold out her air hands. Given her relatively short stack, a small bet keeps the pressure on without overcommitting if she happens to have a piece of the board.

Call 300. I have K♠6♣ — second pair on Q♦ Q♠ 6♥. Flash is the aggressive button opener and his flop sizing (300 into the current pot) is small, consistent with a wide C-bet range that includes many air and one-pair hands I beat. Pot odds to call are roughly 25% (300 to win ~1200), and second pair has enough equity versus his wide c-bet range to justify a flat. I want pot control OOP with only second pair — raising would bloat the pot and commit chips with a marginal hand, folding is too weak because many of his bluffs/A-highs I beat. Plan after the call: fold to a large, polarized turn shove/raise unless I improve to two pair/trips or get clear weakness; if he checks back I can consider a small delayed bet on a safe turn.

Board is Q♦ Q♠ 6♥ 4♠ and I hold K♠6♣ — second pair with a decent kicker but out of position. Flash is a wide, aggressive opener who c-bet the flop small and then checked the turn, which looks like weakness or a pot-control line with Qx; his range contains many hands I beat (Kx, A-high, small pocket pairs) and some bluffs. I don’t want to bloat the pot with a large bet or a raise with only second pair, but I should extract value and deny free cards. A small probing bet (≈1/3 pot) is the right exploit: it gets value from worse hands, folds out some equity, and keeps the pot manageable. If he raises I’ll fold — second pair OOP is rarely good enough facing turn aggression. Bet 400 to take it down or get a cheap river read.

On a paired board of Qd-Qs-6h-4s, I hold Ace-high. Minni has led for a small amount (400 into 1200) after calling my flop continuation bet. This lead often represents a weak hand trying to block a larger bet or a small pair like a 6. Given the excellent pot odds (4-to-1) and the fact that Ace-high still has significant showdown value against her bluffs or lower high cards, calling is the most profitable play. Raising would likely only get called by hands that beat me (like a Queen or a 6), and folding is too tight given the price.

I have K♠6♣ — second pair on Q♦ Q♠ 6♥ 4♠ 5♥. Pot is 2,000 and my stack is 2,400, so I should keep the pot under control OOP with a marginal hand. Flash is a very wide/aggressive opener who called my turn probe; his calling range contains many Qx and some sets/overpairs that beat me and will rarely fold to a small river bet, while a bet would only extract a little value from worse K/A-high hands and some bluffs. Betting risks either getting called by better hands or face a raise that I should fold to; checking preserves showdown and maximizes my chance to scoop the pot when he shows weakness. Plan: check and go to showdown; if he leads into me I can call small value bets but fold to large, polarized raises.

On a board of Qd-Qs-6h-4s-5h, I hold Ace-high. Minni led the turn for 400 but has now checked the river. My Ace-high has significant showdown value in this spot, especially since the river didn't complete any obvious draws. Betting would likely only result in a call from hands that beat me (like a 6 or a small pocket pair) and a fold from hands I already beat. Checking back allows me to reach showdown and win against her bluffs or lower high cards without risking more chips against her short stack.

Heads-up on the button with 6♥4♦ — a weak but playable hand in position. In heads-up we should open very wide; limp-calling is passive and lets the big blind take initiative. I’ll raise to the standard 2bb open (raise to 200 total) to seize initiative, either take the blinds down or play postflop in position. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet, call small defense if given good odds, and continuation-bet on most favorable flops to pressure a wide defending range.

I am in the Big Blind with 64 suited. Minni, who is playing with a relatively short stack of 4,200 (42 BB), has raised to 200 from the Small Blind. I only need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 3:1 pot odds. 64 suited is a playable speculative hand in this spot, especially given the price. I will call and see the flop.

On a board of 3s-2c-3c, I have a flush draw (clubs) and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5). Minni, in the Small Blind position, has checked to me. While I have good equity, betting into a paired board where the opponent could easily have a 3 or be trapping with a pair is risky given her relatively short stack. Checking back allows me to realize my equity and see a free turn card without the risk of being check-raised off my draw.

I was the preflop aggressor in heads-up position and the flop (3♠ 2♣ 3♣) is very dry and low — it hits a lot of the big blind's calling range but also misses a lot. My hand (6♥4♦) has no pair and minimal showdown value, so my best line is to apply pressure and try to take the pot now. A half‑pot c-bet (~200 into 400) gives good fold equity vs overcards and unpaired hands, keeps the pot manageable if called, and still allows me to fold to strong resistance (check‑raise or a big raise) since I have little equity vs sets or two‑pair. Plan: bet to steal; if Flash folds we take it; if he calls, proceed cautiously on the turn (check to control or barreled only with a plan); if he raises big I will fold.

In this heads-up situation, I am in the Big Blind with 6c 4c. I checked to the aggressor on a flop of 3s-2c-3c, and Minni bet 200 into a 400 pot. I have a flush draw and a gutshot straight draw, giving me approximately 12 outs (9 clubs plus the three remaining non-club 5s). With pot odds of 3:1, calling is the most efficient play to see a turn card and potentially realize my equity. Raising is an option but is risky on a paired board against a short-stacked opponent who may be protective of any pair or a trips-holding.

On the turn Ace of Hearts, I am first to act. This card likely favors Minni's pre-flop raising range. I have a flush draw and a gutshot straight draw (12 outs total). Checking is the standard play here to see how she responds to the Ace. If she checks back, I get a free card to hit my draw. If she bets, I will evaluate the pot odds, but with 12 outs, I am likely to call most standard bet sizes.

I opened wide preflop and c‑bet the dry 3♠‑2♣‑3♣ flop with no pair to take it down; Flash called. The A♥ on the turn is a very bad card for my floating range — it significantly improves many hands that would call the flop (A‑x, slow‑played two‑pair/sets, and hands that now have good showdown value). I have essentially no equity and little showdown value, and a bet here will have very low fold equity versus his calling range, especially given his much deeper stack. I’ll check to control the pot and avoid committing chips with air. Plan: fold to meaningful aggression on the turn (don’t chase marginal spots), and if he checks back I’ll reassess the river and look for a cheap bluff only on a very favorable card or check down.

On a board of 3s-2c-3c-Ah-8h, I have missed my flush and straight draws, leaving me with 6-high. I am first to act on the river. Minni checked back on the turn when the Ace appeared, which suggests she either has a weak showdown value hand (like a small pocket pair or a deuce) or was also on a draw. Since I have zero showdown value, my only way to win is to bluff. However, given that the turn went check-check, it's possible Minni will call a river bet with any pair. With a significant stack lead and 6-high, I will check and hope for a cheap showdown, though I expect to lose this hand most of the time to her K-high or small pairs.

I opened wide preflop and c‑bet the dry 3♠‑2♣‑3♣ flop to take the pot or fold out unpaired hands. Flash’s call on the flop leaves a range with many A‑x, small pairs and the occasional set. The turn A♥ is a disaster for my bluffing equity — it gives lots of hands clear showdown value and materially reduces fold equity. I checked the turn to control the size of the pot since I have essentially no real equity. The river 8♥ does not meaningfully improve 6♥4♦; it helps only a tiny portion of his range and is not a “perfect” bluff card. Flash checked the river (passive line), which usually means he’s happy to call a small bet with Ax or a small pair. With the pot at 800, my fold equity is low and a river bluff is likely to be called; there’s no value to extract. Preserve chips and check down.

I am on the button with Ace-King offsuit, a premium hand in any poker format, particularly heads-up. My opponent, Minni, has a relatively short stack of 4,300 chips (43 big blinds). My goal is to build the pot and extract value from her stack. A standard raise to 300 is the most effective play here to maintain pressure and set up for post-flop play or to induce a shove from a wider range of her hands.

Heads-up vs a button open to 300. I hold 5♦6♣ — marginal but playable in position battles heads-up. The raise is standard and my pot odds to call (200 more into the ~400 pot) are favorable and this hand has postflop playability (straight/2‑pair possibilities). 3-betting OOP with 5-6o is thin — I’d often face a shove or a call and be out of position in a bloated pot; folding is too tight given the dead blind already posted. I’ll defend and see a flop, keeping the pot manageable and planning to proceed cautiously OOP.

Heads‑up on a T♣9♠4♥ rainbow flop I have 5♦6♣ — only six clean pair-outs (two 5s and two 6s of remaining suits = 6 outs) and only backdoor straight possibilities. My equity to improve by the river is ~24% and only ~12.8% to hit the turn. I called preflop to see a flop and planned to keep the pot manageable out of position. Betting here as the caller would be a thin semi‑bluff and requires a high fold rate to be +EV; this opponent tends to call a lot of flops, so I don't expect enough fold equity. With ~41BB I don't want to inflate the pot with marginal equity — I’ll check, control the pot, and call a reasonable c‑bet or fold to heavy aggression, and pursue value when I pair or pick up more equity.

On a board of 10-9-4 rainbow, my Ace-King offsuit remains a strong hand with two overcards. Minni has checked, which is common for the big blind after calling a pre-flop raise. I will make a standard continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to exert pressure, fold out her air, and maintain the initiative. This allows me to take down the pot now or potentially realize my equity on later streets if called.

Heads‑up on a T♣9♠4♥ rainbow flop I have 5♦6♣ — no pair, 6 clean outs (three 5s + three 6s) and tiny runner‑runner straight chance. Flash bet 300 into the 600 preflop pot (pot now 900), so calling 300 gets me to a 1200 pot and requires ~25% equity to break even. My raw chance to hit a pair by the river is ~24% (plus a small amount of backdoor straight equity), so this is borderline but very close. Flash is a button opener who tends to call a lot of flops, so a check‑raise or larger semi‑bluff here is unlikely to fold enough hands and would just inflate a pot I’m out of position in. With ~41BB I can comfortably call a small c‑bet to realize my equity and keep the pot controllable. Plan: call to see the turn; if I pair or pick up meaningful equity I can lead/raise for value, but if I miss and face substantial turn pressure I’ll fold.

Check. I have 5♦6♣ on 4♥‑9♠‑T♣‑3♠: I currently have no pair but a lot of live outs to improve. On the turn I pick up 2s and 7s as straight cards in addition to my three remaining 5s and three remaining 6s — 14 clean outs to something that will often be good (~14/46 ≈ 30.4% equity to hit by the river). The pot is 1,200 and my stack is 3,800, and Flash is a button opener who calls a lot of flops, so my fold equity OOP is low. Betting/raising here would bloat the pot out of position with a draw-heavy hand and would rarely get better hands to fold. Checking keeps the pot under control and lets me realize my equity or induce a small protection bet I can call. If Flash bets the turn, I’ll call small-to-medium bets (rough break-even bet ≈ 525 chips given ~30% equity) and fold to large turn sizing; if he checks back I go to the river cheaply and can proceed based on whether I hit. For these reasons I check now and plan to call reasonable turn bets but avoid bloating the pot OOP.

The turn card (3s) is essentially a blank that doesn't significantly change the board texture. Minni has checked for the second time after calling my flop continuation bet. While my Ace-King has good equity and potential to improve to the best hand on the river, betting here might not fold out a Ten or a Nine, which she likely holds if she called the flop. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, realize my equity for free, and can potentially win at showdown against her missed draws or weaker high-card hands like QJ or KQ. It also allows me to evaluate her action on the river more effectively.

River: board 4♥ 9♠ T♣ 3♠ K♣, my hand 5♦6♣ — I missed everything and have no pair or showdown value. Pot is 1,200 and my stack is 3,800; Flash checked the turn (could be weak or a slowplay), but from the table history he’s a very sticky caller and will call small bluffs too often for a river bluff to be +EV. Betting here would bloat the pot out of position and is likely to be called; therefore the lowest-variance +EV line is to check and see if he shows weakness. If he leads out into me I’ll fold — I have no showdown value and cannot improve.

On the river, the King of Clubs improves my hand to top pair with the best possible kicker (Ace). The action so far suggests Minni likely has a marginal made hand like a Ten or a Nine, or perhaps a missed straight draw like QJ. By checking the turn, I've underrepresented the strength of my hand. Now that I've hit my King, I need to extract value. Minni has checked the river, showing weakness. A bet of 600 (half the pot) is a standard value bet designed to get called by a Ten or a stubborn Nine, while not being so large as to scare off her mid-pair hands. Given her remaining stack, this size also leaves her room to call without being pot-committed.

I checked the river with 5♦6♣ and Flash leads out for 600. The pot was ~1,200 before his bet and is ~1,800 now; calling 600 means I need ≈25% equity (600/(1800+600)=25%) to break even. I have no pair and no river outs — I only win if he’s totally bluffing. Flash is a very sticky caller who often calls small bets, and his line (c‑bet flop, check turn, small river stab) looks like weak/medium value rather than a high-frequency bluff. Raising as a bluff (min to 1,200) would be risky and unlikely to get enough folds; calling is pure hope against a calling opponent. Fold to preserve stack and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with Ac6h — this is a standard open-raise spot. I want the initiative and fold equity rather than passively completing to 100. A6o is strong in HU and should be played aggressively from the button. I’ll raise to 3x the BB (300) — a size that pressures the big blind, denies cheap flops, and still keeps the pot manageable with my ~37.5bb stack. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll reassess (likely fold), but versus a call I can c-bet favorable flops and extract value when I hit top pair or good draws.

I am holding Jack-Ten suited, which is a very strong hand in a heads-up situation. My opponent, Minni, has a relatively short stack of 3,500 (35 big blinds). While a 3-bet is a viable option to put pressure on the short stack, calling allows me to see a flop with a hand that has great playability and equity. Since I am in the big blind, I only need to call 200 more to see the flop in a pot of 400.

On a board of 8d-2s-3h, I have two overcards and a backdoor flush draw with my Jack-Ten of diamonds. Minni, as the pre-flop aggressor on the button, will often continue-bet here. Checking to her is standard. I have 35,900 chips, and she has a relatively short stack of 3,500. Given I am out of position, I will check and see her reaction to this dry flop.

I opened preflop and took the initiative; the flop 8♦‑2♠‑3♥ is very dry and misses a large portion of Flash’s calling range. A♣6♥ has the Ace blocker and some showdown value, so c‑betting is the +EV play to win the pot outright and get value from worse A‑x or floaters. I’ll size up to ~2/3 pot (600 pot → bet 400) — big enough to fold out many missed hands and charge any backdoor draws, but small enough to keep options if called. Plan: if he folds I take it down; if he calls I re‑evaluate on the turn and value bet A or blank turns appropriately; if he check‑raises large I’ll fold this weak Ace‑high.

I am holding Jack-Ten of diamonds on an 8-2-3 rainbow flop. I have two overcards and backdoor flush and straight draws. Minni has bet 400 into a 600 pot. With her short stack of approximately 31 big blinds remaining, she could be continuation betting a wide range of hands on this dry board. I have the correct odds to call with my overcards and backdoor equity, and I want to see if the turn improves my hand or provides more clarity on her range.

I am in the big blind with Jack-Ten of diamonds. The turn card is the Seven of hearts, which does not improve my hand. The board is now 8d-2s-3h-7h. I have two overcards and a backdoor flush draw (needing two more diamonds, though only one is on the board). Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, Minni, the standard play is to check to her and see how she proceeds. My plan is to check and evaluate if she bets, considering her relatively short stack of 3,100 chips.

I opened pre and c‑bet the dry 8♦‑2♠‑3♥ flop; Flash called, showing a wide, often weak or medium-strength range (floats, small pairs, some Ax). The 7♥ on the turn is another relatively blank card that only improves a small portion of his range (pocket 7s, some 7x). I hold A♣6♥ — the Ace blocker makes me less likely to be dominated and gives decent showdown value. I want to apply pressure to fold out missed overcards and smaller pairs while extracting thin value from worse A‑x and 6‑x hands, but I don't want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand. I’ll fire a half‑pot turn bet (~700 into 1400). If he check‑raises big I’ll fold; if called I’ll re‑evaluate river for either a showdown or a manageable river bet.

I am holding Jack-Ten of diamonds on a board of 8d-2s-3h-7h. I have missed the flop and turn entirely, holding only two overcards. Minni has shown consistent aggression by raising pre-flop and betting both the flop and the turn. Given her relatively short stack (now only 2400 remaining), her second barrel on the turn suggests a strong hand or a committed semi-bluff. I am currently behind any pair, and even if I hit a Jack or Ten on the river, I cannot be certain it would be the best hand. With no draw to rely on and poor pot odds for a call with just overcards, the most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my large stack.

I am on the button with QT offsuit. This is a strong starting hand in a heads-up situation. Minni, the big blind, has a relatively short stack of 44 big blinds. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and put pressure on her stack while holding a hand that plays well post-flop.

Facing a 300 button open with K♦J♦ (heads-up, big blind). Pot is 400 and I only have to call 200 (to put 300 total) — a cheap price to see a flop with a hand that dominates much of the button's opening range. Flash has shown frequent opens and post‑flop aggression, so a tiny 3‑bet (to 500) would be a weak sizing and a big 3‑bet would commit too much of my 4,400 stack with a hand that can be dominated by A/Kx. I’ll call to keep the pot manageable, realize equity, and re‑evaluate on the flop: aggressively build when I hit (top pair/draws) and control/fold when I miss and face heavy pressure.

Heads‑up out of position with K♥J♦ on 6♦‑8♣‑6♣: I have no pair, no club draw, just two overcards (6 outs, ~24% to pair by river). Flash opens wide and is frequently aggressive, so he'll c‑bet a lot — but this flop is paired and somewhat drawy (two clubs), so I don’t want to bluff‑lead OOP with only overcards and risk bloating the pot. I’ll check to control the pot size, induce a c‑bet, and realize my equity. Plan: call a standard small/medium c‑bet (≈≤1/2 pot ≈300) to see a turn and fold to a large raise that represents a set or strong made hand; if I pair a K or J on the turn I’ll start betting for value. Checking preserves my 4,200 stack and keeps options open rather than overcommitting OOP.

On this paired board (6d-8c-6c), I have two overcards and a backdoor flush draw with the Ten of Clubs. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and the board is relatively dry (paired boards are hard for the defender to hit), a continuation bet is standard here to put pressure on Minni's air and marginal hands. A half-pot bet is sized to fold out hands like J-high or low unpaired cards while maintaining pot control against her short stack.

Heads‑up out of position with K♥J♦ on 6♦‑8♣‑6♣. I checked the flop to control the pot and induce a c‑bet — Flash bets a small 300 into a 900 pot (≈1/3 pot) which is a sizing consistent with a wide/loose c‑bet. I have two overcards (6 outs, ≈24% to pair by the river). Calling 300 into 900 gives me break‑even equity ≈25%, so this is a marginal but acceptable price given his wide, aggressive range and the small sizing. I’ll call to realize equity and keep weaker hands and bluffs in his range; I’ll fold to heavy turn aggression if I still miss, and if I pair a K or J on the turn I’ll start extracting value.

Out of position with K♥J♦ on 6♦‑8♣‑6♣‑A♠. I checked the flop and called a small c‑bet to see the turn; the Ace on the turn now heavily improves Flash’s PFR/c‑bet range (lots of A‑x) while I still haven’t hit. I only have 6 outs to pair by the river (6/46 ≈ 13% equity), so calling any turn bet is +EV only versus extremely small sizings — and Flash is very capable of calling or raising light. Betting as a bluff here bloats the pot out of position and will often be called or raised by hands that beat me. Best line is to check, control the pot, and fold to meaningful turn aggression; if he checks back I can try to realize equity and only invest further if I pair K or J on the river (or get a very favorable sizing to bluff‑catch).

On the turn Ace of Spades, I still have only Queen-high. While the Ace is a good card for the pre-flop aggressor to represent, Minni's call on the paired flop (6-8-6) suggests she has a piece of the board, like an 8 or a pocket pair, or a draw. Given her short stack (3900 remaining into a 1200 pot), she may be less likely to fold a marginal hand to a single bet. Checking back allows me to see a free river and potentially realize my equity if I hit a Queen or Ten, or decide to bluff on a later street if she checks again.

Board has trip sixes (6d 8c 6c As 6s). I hold K♥J♦ — no pair, no draw, only showdown value against a bluff. Flash checked the turn after an earlier flop raise and now checks the river as well; given how coordinated the board is, betting as a bluff OOP here is thin and will rarely get folds from A‑x or small pairs that beat me. Best line is to protect my stack: check and see a showdown, folding to any river bet. If Flash checks back I’ll show and preserve my chips; if he fires a very tiny bet (<~10% pot) I might consider a call, but otherwise I fold to river aggression.

On a board of 6d-8c-6c-As-6s, the best five-card hand is determined by the three sixes on the board, the Ace, and the highest kicker available between the board (the 8) and the players' hole cards. With Qd in my hand, my best hand is 6-6-6-A-Q. This beats any hand Minni holds unless she has a King (for 6-6-6-A-K) or the case Six (for Quads). Minni has checked turn and river, suggesting she likely doesn't have a monster. While a small value bet could potentially be called by a weaker kicker (like a Jack or an 8), checking back is the safest way to realize my equity and win the pot against her range without risking a check-raise or losing to a slow-played King.

Heads-up on the button with 2♣9♠ and ~50bb effective. This is a weak offsuit hand — not worth an open shove or big isolation raise. Facing a deep, aggressive BB, min-raising to 200 risks a 3-bet or bloating the pot with a poor hand. Calling the 50 to complete and seeing a flop keeps the pot controllable and preserves stack depth while giving me position post-flop to realize equity or fold if the flop is unfavorable. I will call the 50 more (total committed 100) and proceed from position.

I am in the big blind with a very strong hand, Ace-Queen suited. My opponent, Minni, has a relatively small stack of 5,000 chips remaining compared to my 34,800. Minni just limped in from the small blind. I should raise here for value and to take control of the pot. A standard raise to 3x or 4x the big blind is appropriate.

I completed the blind with 2♣9♠ intending to see a flop, but Flash’s raise to 400 changes the math. I must call 300 into a 500 pot (300 to win 800) — I’d need ~37.5% equity to justify a call. 2‑9 offsuit has very poor equity and post‑flop playability even though I’d have position. A thin 3‑bet bluff to the 700 min‑raise is too risky against a deep, frequently‑raising opponent and would bloat the pot with a marginal holding. Preserve my ~50bb stack for better spots and fold here.

I am in the Small Blind with 7-3 offsuit, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. In heads-up play, while many hands are playable from the button, 7-3 offsuit offers very little post-flop equity and does not warrant even a small investment against an opponent who has shown willingness to defend and play aggressively. I will fold to preserve my significant stack lead.

With 4♠8♠ I'm dealt a marginal but suited hand in the SB/Button with 50bb effective. The cost to see a flop is just 50 more (pot ~150 -> 200), giving ~4:1 immediate pot odds and the advantage of acting last postflop. Flash (BB) has shown a tendency to defend and be aggressive, so a min-raise to 200 would have limited fold equity and would simply bloat the pot out of position with a weak hand. I prefer to call (complete to 100 total) — keep the pot small, retain position, and evaluate the flop; fold to a large 3-bet, and proceed postflop with a plan (check/call on safe boards, lead or raise selectively when we connect). This preserves stack and allows exploiting postflop opportunities.

I have a strong pocket pair (9s 9c) in the big blind. Minni has limped from the small blind, which usually indicates a weaker range or a desire to see a cheap flop. Given her relatively short stack (under 50 BB), I want to raise for value and to take control of the pot. Raising to 400 is a standard play here.

Heads-up on the button with 4♠8♠ (50bb effective). Flash made a standard-sized raise to 400 from the BB. This is a playable, suited speculative hand in position — I want to keep the pot manageable and use my positional advantage postflop. Folding is too tight here vs a likely wide raising range; a 3-bet bluff is riskier and bloats the pot out of position when I have a marginal holding. Calling preserves fold equity on later streets, realizes equity on flush/straight draws, and lets me play postflop based on the texture. Plan: call to see the flop; proceed with pot control if I miss, and apply pressure or extract value when I connect. If Flash 3-bets large preflop, re-evaluate and likely fold.

I raised pre-flop with pocket nines and was called by Minni. The flop comes K-Q-7, which is quite unfavorable for my hand as it contains two overcards. Minni's range for calling a raise from the small blind can easily include Kx or Qx hands. However, she checked to me. I'll check back to control the pot and see a turn card cheaply. Betting into this board might just isolate me against hands that have me crushed.

Flop texture: Kc Qd 7d — two diamonds, high-card flop. I hold 8♠4♠ and have missed completely (no pair, no diamond draw, only a remote backdoor spade runner-runner). Flash checked, which can mean a wide range (Kx/Qx, diamond draws, underpairs, or floats). With virtually no showdown value and very little equity, betting here would bloat the pot and put me in tough spots versus a call or raise; I also don't hold meaningful blockers to a K/Q bluff. Pot control and realizing equity in position is best: check and see a turn for free, then re-evaluate. If Flash bets a small amount on turn I may call to probe depending on price, but I will fold to meaningful aggression since I currently have no real equity.

The board has paired on the turn with a second Queen (K-Q-7-Q). I hold pocket nines. Minni has checked for the second street in a row. My hand has decent showdown value against her range, which includes smaller pairs, ace-high, or missed draws, but I am behind any King or Queen. Checking back is the most prudent play to control the pot and see the river for free, as betting likely only gets called by hands that have me beat.

I have 8♠4♠ on a Kc Qd 7d Qh board — no pair, no diamond draw, only a remote backdoor spade. Flash checked both flop and turn; his range here includes Qx (top/trips), Kx, missed diamond draws, underpairs, and some floats. I have virtually no showdown equity vs his value hands and I don’t block meaningful parts of his value or bluffing range, so a bluff bet is thin and will often get called or raised by better hands. Best play is pot control and to realize equity in position: check the turn. If he bets turn or river I’ll fold to meaningful pressure and only consider calling very small, obvious bluff bets. If he checks the river I’ll check back and see the showdown (or the runner-runner spade), keeping risk minimal.

On the river, I have improved to a full house (Nines full of Queens) with the 9h. The board is Q-Q-K-7-9. My hand, 9-9-9-Q-Q, is exceptionally strong, beating all 3-of-a-kind hands (like Qx), all two-pair hands (like K7), and lower full houses (like 7-7-7-Q-Q). It is only beaten by pocket Kings (KK) or a specific Queen-King (QK) or pocket Queens (QQ), all of which are unlikely given Minni's passive play (checking three streets). Since the pot is 800 and Minni has shown no aggression, I will place a value bet of 500, hoping to get called by a King or a Nine.

Board Kc Qd 7d Qh 9h — I hold 8s4s with no pair, no diamond draw and essentially no meaningful blockers. Flash checked flop and turn and now fires a small river bet (500 into ~800). That line is consistent with slow-played/checked-to turn Qx (now trips) and many Kx/medium pocket pairs or straightforward thin value hands; missed diamond draws/bluffs are possible but not frequent enough to justify a call. Call math: I must call 500 to win ~1,300 (≈2.6:1 or ~28% equity required) — I have nowhere near that vs his likely value-weighted range. This is a clear -EV call, so fold and preserve stack for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with Qc 3d. Although the hand is weak, I have a massive chip lead over Minni, who has been playing relatively cautiously and folding to pre-flop raises in recent hands. I will apply pressure with a standard raise to 300 to try and win the pot immediately or play a pot in position.

Flash opened to 300 from the button (SB). I’m in the big blind with 8♣6♦. This is a marginal offsuit hand but heads‑up I should defend a wide range. The pot is 400 and I need to call 200 more (300 total), giving ~3:1 immediate pot odds to see a flop. I don’t want to 3‑bet this hand — it’s easily dominated and I’d be taking a lot of initiative OOP with little equity. Plan: call to realize equity and evaluate the flop; fold to significant post‑flop aggression if I miss, and only continue aggressively if I hit a decent pair or strong draw.

I defended wide preflop heads‑up, but this flop (A♠ Q♠ 4♥) completely misses 8♣6♦ — no pair, no spade draw and very little equity versus a button opener’s c‑betting range (lots of A/Q/x). I’ll check to control the pot and avoid investing with air. Plan is to fold to any meaningful bet — only consider calling an extremely tiny probe or bluff‑catching a very small sizing — and if Flash checks back I can take a free turn to re‑evaluate.

I have hit middle pair on an Ace-high board. While I have a Queen, my kicker (3) is very weak, meaning I could easily be out-kicked by other Qx hands in Minni's range. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and exercise pot control with a hand that has showdown value but isn't strong enough to value bet multiple streets. This also allows me to see how Minni reacts on the turn while keeping the pot manageable.

I defended wide preflop heads‑up with 8♣6♦. The flop A♠ Q♠ 4♥ missed me and I checked; Flash checked back. The turn J♣ gives me zero pair and no credible draw (no spade or straight outs). Pot is 600, my stack 4350 vs Flash 35050 — no reason to bloat the pot with air. I will check again to see what Flash does and preserve chips; plan is to fold to any meaningful turn bet and only continue if I pick up a pair/draw or get a very tiny probe that offers correct pot odds.

On the turn, the board is 4h Qs As Jc. I have middle pair with a weak kicker (Qc 3d). Minni has checked to me for the second time. The Jack of Clubs completes some straight draws (KT) and adds more broadway cards to the board. My hand has showdown value but is not strong enough to value bet here, especially given my very weak kicker. Checking back ensures pot control and allows me to reach showdown or see if Minni tries to bluff the river.

I defended a very wide heads‑up range preflop, but the entire board (A♠ Q♠ 4♥ J♣ 5♦) missed my 8♣6♦ — no pair, no spade draw and no credible river outs. Both players checked flop and turn, so Flash has either a showdown‑worthy Ace/Queen/Jx or air that didn’t want to bloat the pot. Betting as a bluff could pick up some hands, but against a large‑stacked opponent who checked back twice, many of his calling hands (Ax, Qx, small pairs) will call a small river probe and crush my bluff. I don’t want to create unnecessary variance or risk a big portion of my 4350 stack for a marginal fold equity opportunity in a 600‑chip pot. Stick to the earlier plan: check to showdown and fold if he fires a value‑sized bet.

On the river, the board is 4h-Qs-As-Jc-5d. I hold middle pair with a Queen, but my kicker (3) is extremely weak. Minni has checked on every street (flop, turn, and river), indicating a lack of strength. While I might have the best hand against her missed draws or small pairs, a bet is unlikely to be called by anything I beat, and I could easily be out-kicked by a Queen like Q6 or Q7. Checking back allows me to realize my showdown value and win the pot without risking a loss to a better marginal hand or a trap.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with J♣2♦ — a weak hand but I have positional advantage and can open a wide stealing range. The pot is 150 and it only costs me +150 to raise to the min (200 total), which is a small, standard heads-up sizing that preserves fold equity and keeps the pot manageable. Plan: raise to take the initiative; fold to a sizeable 3-bet since this hand plays poorly vs aggression; if called I’ll use position to c-bet appropriate flops and give up when the board heavily favors the big blind. Given the opponent is very deep and tends to defend, the small raise maximizes long-term EV while limiting downside with this junk hand.

I am in the big blind with Ten-Six offsuit. Minni, who is on the button (small blind) with a relatively short stack of 41.5 big blinds, has made a minimum raise to 200. The pot is currently 300 and it only costs me 100 more to call. Given the 3:1 pot odds and the fact that we are heads-up, this is a standard defend in the big blind. I will call and see the flop.

I am in the big blind with Ten-high on a King-high flop. The board texture favors the pre-flop aggressor (Minni). My hand has very little equity here, missing the board entirely. The standard play in this situation is to check and see how the opponent acts. I will not be investing more chips into this pot unless I improve significantly or see a reason to bluff, but for now, checking is the most appropriate action.

I raised preflop and the flop checked to me — K♠-5♥-4♠ is a dry board that misses my J♣2♦ but is a great spot to c-bet heads-up. A small continuation bet preserves fold equity against many of Flash’s calling hands (overcards, air) while keeping the pot controllable vs a deep, sticky opponent. I’ll bet ~1/3 pot (150 into 400) to take the initiative; plan is to fold to a strong check-raise or large raise, and if called use position to evaluate the turn and barrel only when it meaningfully improves my range or gives me realistic fold equity.

I am in the big blind with Ten-Six offsuit on a King-high board. I missed the flop completely and have no significant draws or backdoors. Minni has made a continuation bet of 150 into a pot of 400. While the price is relatively small, my hand has very little equity and limited potential to improve. Continuing here would likely require a bluff on a later street, which is not ideal given the board texture favors the pre-flop aggressor. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play in this spot.

I am on the button with 6c4h. This is a very weak starting hand, well below the range of hands I should typically be opening or completing with in a heads-up or 3-way scenario, even with a large stack advantage over Minni (who has about 4450 chips). Given that I am in the Small Blind and the pot is small, the most prudent move is to simply fold this hand and wait for a better spot to utilize my chips, as 64 offsuit has very little playability and I would be out of position post-flop if I were to just call the 50.

Heads-up on the button with K♠K♦ — a premium hand. Pot is 150 and it's only 50 to call; I want to build the pot and deny cheap equity to weaker hands. Flash in the BB looks deep and frequently opens, so a standard heads-up open to ~3x the BB (300 total) extracts value from worse hands (Ax, broadways, pairs) while keeping my stack flexibility if he 3-bets. Raising to 300 (rather than a bare min-raise) reduces free cards and prices in callers without committing too much of my 4550 stack.

I am in the Big Blind with pocket deuces. Minni, who is playing on a relatively short stack of 4,300, has raised to 300 from the Button/Small Blind. While pocket deuces are the weakest pair, they have significant value in a head-up situation. Calling the 200 to see a flop is the most standard play here; it allows me to set-mine or potentially win the pot if the board comes out low, while keeping the pot controlled given I will be out of position.

On a board of 8s Kh Ad, my pocket deuces are in very poor shape. This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range, as it contains both an Ace and a King. Since I am out of position and have very little equity to improve (only two outs for a set), the best strategy is to check and see how Minni responds. If Minni bets, I will likely fold, as it is difficult to represent a stronger hand or find a reason to call with the lowest possible pair on a board with two overcards.

I flatted preflop with KK and the K on the flop gives me trips — a very strong, mostly-dominated value hand heads-up. Flash checked the flop, which can mean weakness or a slow-played A/K/medium pair. My objective is to extract value from Kx, lower pocket pairs and other hands that will call, while not charging so much that I fold them out or build a pot that lets an Ace (which currently beats me) get too cheap equity on later streets. A half‑pot bet (~300 into 600) accomplishes that: it gets called by worse hands, denies free cards, and keeps the pot manageable if Flash check‑raises (I can then re‑raise or commit based on sizing). Betting now builds the pot with the best hand in most of Flash’s calling range.

I am holding pocket deuces on a board of 8s-Kh-Ad. This is a very poor board for my hand as it contains two overcards (Ace and King) that likely hit Minni's pre-flop raising range. Minni has bet 300 into a 600 pot. With only a 4% chance of improving to a set on the turn and almost no showdown value against any part of Minni's continuing range, I should fold and wait for a better opportunity. Preservation of my chip lead is more important than calling with the lowest possible pair on such a coordinated high-card board.

I'm on the Button (Small Blind) with Ace-Six offsuit. With my opponent, Minni, having a relatively short stack of 48 big blinds, I want to apply pressure. A6o is a solid hand to open for a standard raise in this position.

Heads-up in the big blind with AdAs — premium hand. Flash opened to 300; pot is 400 and I need 200 to call. Standard, high EV line is to 3-bet for value and fold equity. I’ll make it a 4x open to 1200 total: big enough to charge broadways/medium pairs and deny equity to speculative hands, but leaves ~3700 behind to extract more postflop. If Flash flats we’ll play the AA OOP for value; if he 4-bets shove we’ll call. This sizing is above the min-raise (500) and balances value vs. playability with 48BB effective.

I opened on the button with Ace-Six offsuit, which is a standard play. Minni has responded with a large 3-bet to 1,200. Given that Minni's stack is quite shallow (approximately 49 big blinds total), this 3-bet represents a significant portion of their chips and indicates a strong range or a commitment to the pot. A6o performs poorly against a typical 3-betting range and lacks the post-flop playability to justify calling 900 more in this situation. Folding is the most disciplined and long-term profitable move here.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with Qs Jd — a solid, highly playable hand in position. I want initiative and fold equity versus Flash’s wide BB defending range, so I’ll open to a standard 3xBB. Raising to 300 builds the pot when I’m likely ahead or flipping vs many calling hands, and lets me play post-flop in position. Stack is ~51BB, so I can comfortably continue if he 3-bets to a normal size (~900) and will play postflop; I’ll be prepared to fold to very large 3-bets/4-bets that commit too many chips without a made hand. Plan: c-bet favorable flops, control pot when checked to, and avoid bloating the pot without improvement.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Six offsuit. Minni, who is on the Short Stack with 49BB, has raised to 300 (a 3x open). Given the 3:1 pot odds and the fact that Queen-high has decent equity against a wide button/SB opening range, I will call the additional 200 to see a flop. My significant chip lead allows me to play slightly more pots against Minni's short stack.

I am out of position on a board that I completely missed with Queen-high. Standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor and see their action. I have no draws and very little equity on this texture.

Heads-up in position with Qs Jd on a 3s Js 8h flop: I have top pair with a very strong queen kicker plus a backdoor spade; this is a clear spot to take the initiative as the preflop raiser. Flash called preflop wide, so his range includes many worse Jx, 8x, pocket pairs and draws that will pay a half-pot. Pot is 600 — I’ll make a standard ~1/2-pot c-bet (300) to extract value, deny equity to overcards/draws, and keep the pot manageable vs a potential check-raise. Plan: if called, re-evaluate on the turn and continue for value on safe cards or pot-control if dangerous bricks; treat a large check-raise as strong and fold unless the sizing is small enough to merit a call and further evaluation.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-high on a board of Jack-Eight-Three. I have no pair and no real draw. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, has bet 300 into a 600 pot. This is a standard continuation bet on a board that generally favors a pre-flop raiser's range. However, with my specific hand (Q6o), I have very little equity and no backdoor draws to continue. Calling would be a losing play long-term in this spot.

I am on the Button (SB) in a heads-up situation with Q2o. While Q2o is a weak hand, standard heads-up strategy involves opening a wide range from the Button to take advantage of position. Minni has a relatively short stack of 54BB. I will raise to 300 to apply pressure and potentially win the blinds immediately. If I encounter significant resistance (a 3-bet), I can comfortably fold this marginal hand.

Flash opened to 300 on the button. I’m in the big blind with Q♠2♥ and a 5,400 stack. Heads-up I should defend a wide range — Q2o has reasonable equity vs a standard HU open and the pot odds are favorable (calling 200 more to contest the ~400 pot). I don’t want to 3-bet as a bluff with this weak, offsuit hand out of position and against a much deeper stack; that just bloats the pot and makes postflop decisions harder. Plan: call to see the flop (I’ll be ready to fold to heavy aggression on dry A/K-type flops without improvement, and continue/raise for value if I pair the Q or get favorable draws).

Check. On this 8♦-7♠-9♣ rainbow flop my Q♠2♥ has missed completely (only a backdoor spade). Heads‑up I defended preflop correctly, but out of position with only Q‑high I don’t want to inflate the pot by barreling as a bluff. Plan: check and concede to significant aggression. If Flash checks back I get a free turn to try to hit a Queen or a real draw; if he C‑bets small (~1/3 pot ≈200) I’ll consider a call to realize equity, but I’ll fold to medium/large bets (≳1/2 pot ≈300) unless I improve. If I pair the Q or pick up a real draw on the turn I’ll switch to a more aggressive line.

I am on the Button with Q2o and the flop is 8d-7s-9c. This is a very coordinated board that hits a lot of the Big Blind's calling range, including various straights, straight draws, and pairs. My hand has no value and no significant draws. Minni checked, which is standard for the Big Blind on this texture. Since I have no showdown value and the board texture is unfavorable for a pure air continuation bet, I will check behind and hope to improve on a later street or see a cheap showdown.

Check. I picked up a rag bottom pair on the turn (2♥) but I’m out of position with a weak kicker and Flash checked the flop, so his range is wide and can include lots of overcards and medium pairs. Leading here would bloat the pot OOP with a marginal holding and expose me to check-raises or multi-street pressure from stronger hands. I’ll control the pot and see a river: plan is to call small turn bets (≈ ≤1/3 pot ≈200) to realize equity, fold to medium/large turn bets (≳1/2 pot ≈300) since I’m vulnerable, and if we go checked to the river re-evaluate — bet for thin value only if I improve or check to showdown otherwise.

I have improved to bottom pair on the turn. The board is extremely coordinated (8-7-9), which typically favors the Big Blind's calling range. Minni has checked twice. While my pair of Deuces has some showdown value, it is very thin. Betting here doesn't achieve much as better hands are unlikely to fold and worse hands have very little to call with. Checking behind allows me to control the size of the pot and see a river for free, potentially reaching a cheap showdown or seeing if the board becomes less dangerous.

River: I have two pair (8s & 2s) on 8♦-7♠-9♣-2♦-8♥. Flash opened preflop but checked both flop and turn, which points to a wide, often passive/missed range rather than a slow‑played full house/trips. The river pairing the board introduces hands that beat me (any 8, pocket 2s, or pocket pairs that turn into a full house like 9‑9 or 7‑7), but those lines are less likely given his passivity. Betting risks folding out worse hands and bloating the pot vs the small chance he has the few combos that beat me. With a small pot (600) and a vulnerable two‑pair on a paired board, the highest EV line is to check and take showdown for free rather than build the pot.

On the river, the board is 8-7-9-2-8. I have a pair of Deuces. Minni has checked every street (Flop, Turn, and River). On this coordinated board, it is possible she has a marginal hand like a 7 or 9, or she might have missed a straight draw. Since I have a small amount of showdown value with my pair of Deuces, betting doesn't make much sense as I'm unlikely to get called by worse hands, and checking allows me to realize my equity and win the pot if she was holding a missed draw or Ace-high. I will check behind to go to showdown.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with K♥3♦ and about 54 BB effective. In heads-up play the button should open-raise a very wide range to take advantage of position and initiative. K3o is marginal but perfectly raisable here — raising denies the big blind a cheap flop, narrows his range, and gives me postflop leverage. I’ll make a standard 2x BB raise (min allowed) to 200 total: it keeps pot manageable, preserves stack depth for postflop play, and still has good fold equity. If Flash 3-bets I can fold or re-evaluate; limping/calling surrenders initiative and is suboptimal heads-up.

I am in the Big Blind with T8s, a strong semi-connected suited hand. The Button (Minni) has raised to 200, making it 100 more for me to call. Given the pot odds (100 to win 300) and the playability of T8s in a heads-up pot, this is an easy call to see a flop. My opponent is quite short-stacked (5300 chips), so I need to be mindful of stack-to-pot ratios if I hit a piece of the board.

On the flop of 2c-Qs-7c, I have Ten-high with 8d-Td. I've missed the board completely. Minni, as the pre-flop raiser, will likely have a range advantage on this texture, though it's relatively dry. My hand has very little backdoor equity (only a gutshot straight draw needing a 9 and a 6/J). I will check to see what the original aggressor does. If Minni bets, I will likely fold unless the bet size is extremely small, as I have no real draw or pair.

I opened preflop and have position/initiative. The flop Q♠–2♣–7♣ is relatively dry and I missed with K♥3♦, but heads‑up I should c‑bet a large portion of my range to steal the pot and deny equity. A small sizing (~1/3 pot) preserves stack depth while giving good fold equity and keeps weaker parts of my range in play. I’ll bet 150 into the 400 pot. If Flash check‑raises heavily I’ll fold; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and only continue with improvement or a clear plan.

On this flop of 2c-Qs-7c, I have Ten-high with no draws or pairs. Minni, as the pre-flop aggressor, has bet 150 into a pot of 400. While the odds are decent, my hand has virtually no equity and no real way to improve on most turn cards. Continuing here would be a mistake with such a weak holding against a range that includes many Queens and better high-card hands.

I am in the Small Blind with 2c 3s, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Given its poor playability and lack of high-card value, there is no reason to commit more chips to the pot, even if it is only a small amount to call. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) I should be opening a wide range. Js7s is a playable, suited hand with good postflop playability and a Jack blocker that reduces some of the opponent's strong J/Ax combos. I raise to take the initiative, deny the BB cheap equity, and build a pot where I have position. I pick a standard 3x-style open to 300 (table standard sizing) rather than the min-raise to 200 to maintain fold equity and avoid giving them excellent odds to see a flop for free. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet, or continue postflop with a c-bet on favorable textures and play aggressively in position.

I am in the Big Blind with 8c 6h. Minni, on the Button/Small Blind, has raised to 300. My hand is a weak suited-gapper, but the price to call is only 200 more into a pot that will be 600. However, Minni's stack is quite short (5450 chips), and my hand does not play particularly well against her typical opening range from the button when she is short-stacked. I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot, as defending with 8-high out of position is generally a losing play long-term in this configuration.

I am on the button with King-Four offsuit. In this structure, I have a significant chip lead over Minni. While K4o is a marginal hand, it is generally worth an opening raise from the small blind to apply pressure on the big blind, especially given that I have position throughout the hand. I will raise to 300 to either take the pot immediately or see a flop with the betting lead.

I have 2♥3♣ (very weak offsuit). Heads-up from the big blind versus a button open, I should defend widely but avoid bloating the pot OOP with marginal hands. The pot is 400 and I must call 200 more — that gives ~3:1 pot odds (need ≈25% equity). Versus a standard wide BTN opening range 2-3o has roughly that equity plus some backdoor play, and effective stacks (~57 BB) make a flat call low risk. A min 3-bet to 500 is too small to fold him out often and would put me OOP in tricky spots; folding would be too nitty heads-up. Plan: call to 300 total, see the flop, fold to heavy aggression on dry high-card textures, and continue/raise when I pair, pick up a good draw, or detect weakness.

Heads-up on a dry K-9-5 board I have virtually no showdown or semi-bluff equity with 2♥3♣. Flash’s button raising range is weighted toward Kx/overcards and he’ll c-bet this texture a lot, so defending preflop was fine but I shouldn’t bloat the pot OOP now. Pot is 600 and facing no bet, best line is to check and fold to meaningful aggression — if Flash checks back I see a free turn; if he bets I’ll fold most sizes (only consider calling miniscule probes occasionally). Preserve stack and wait for a better spot to attack.

I have flopped top pair on a dry board (5h 9d Kc). Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and have position, a continuation bet is standard here to extract value from weaker hands like middle pairs, draws, or Ace-high. I will bet 400 into the 600 pot to maintain pressure and build the pot with my pair of Kings.

Facing a 400 bet into a 1000 pot (I must call 400 to win 1400 → need ≈28.6% equity). On Kc‑9d‑5h with 2♥3♣ I have no pair and no meaningful draws — only eight bare outs to pair by river (≈31% raw vs a random hand), but Flash’s heads‑up c‑betting range is heavy in Kx/9x/paired and strong overcards, so my realized equity is significantly lower. I’m out of position with ~55BB effective; the bet is a standard c‑bet size (not a tiny probe), so calling to see a turn would be playing a marginal hand in a bloated pot. I stick to my earlier plan to defend preflop but fold to meaningful aggression on dry boards. Fold.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with Qh7s and ~55bb effective. In heads-up play my opening range should be wide — Q7o is playable and I want the initiative and positional advantage postflop. Raising also gives fold equity and prevents giving Flash a free look. I'll raise to the min-open of 200 (2x BB). Plan: fold to a large 3-bet (Q7o is poorly positioned vs a strong 3-betting range); if he calls I'll continuation-bet favorable flops, value-push when I pair the queen, and play cautiously on A-high or heavily coordinated boards.

I am holding 7d 6d in the Big Blind. Minni, on the button, has raised to 200. I only need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 4:1 pot odds. Suited connectors like 76s play very well and have great equity realization. Given Minni's relatively short stack compared to the blinds (around 53 BBs), I want to see a flop and potentially outplay her post-flop or hit a strong draw/pair. Calling is the standard and most profitable play here.

On a low board of 2h-5c-3d, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 4 to complete a 2-3-4-5-6 straight) and two overcards. While this board texture often favors the Big Blind's range, I will start by checking to the pre-flop aggressor to see how she navigates the flop.

I raised preflop to take the initiative with a wide button range. The flop (2h-5c-3d) is very dry and heavily favors the raiser — few legitimate draws and many of BB’s call hands (Broadways, A‑x, K‑x, missed connectors) will fold. Q7 is two overcards to the board so I have decent equity if called and I continue to represent a strong range. I’ll c‑bet for fold equity and to deny a free turn card: bet ~60% of the pot (250 into 400). If Flash calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn; I’ll give up on large check‑raises which will usually indicate sets/two‑pair/strong made hands.

I have a gutshot straight draw (any 4 completes the straight) and two overcards (7 and 6). On this low board (2h-5c-3d), Minni's continuation bet is expected. Calling 250 to win a pot that will be 900 provides pot odds of roughly 2.6:1 (needing ~28% equity). My gutshot provides some equity, and my overcards may occasionally be live. Given the board texture favors a Big Blind defending range, I will call to see the turn and potentially realize my equity or outplay the opponent on later streets.

On the turn, the board pairs with a second three (2h-5c-3d-3h). I still have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 4) and two overcards. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, I will follow the standard line of checking to the bettor. This allows me to see if she continues her aggression or checks back, helping me define her range and decide if I can profitably chase my draw or potentially bluff later streets if she shows weakness.

I opened wide on the button with Q7 and c‑bet the dry 2h‑5c‑3d flop to take the initiative. Flash called, so his continuing range is weighted toward hands with at least some showdown value (small pocket pairs, A/5/2 combos, occasionally a 3). The turn (3h) pairs the board and actually helps the caller more than me (it gives trips to any 3, and it makes two‑pair combinations with 2/3 or 3/5 possible). Flash checked the turn, which can be either weakness or a trap, but with only Q7 (two overcards, no pair) I don’t want to bloat the pot and get called or raised by the hands that beat me. Pot is 900 and my stack is ~5100 — a sizable bet commits meaningful chips. I’ll check back the turn to control the pot, realize equity (hope he checks river), and keep my options to bluff or fold on the river depending on the card and his action.
