Checking avoids unnecessary fold equity loss against a calling/raising range that likely has me crushed.

I missed my gutshot straight draw on the river, and the Jack of Hearts is not a particularly helpful card for my perceived range. I have seven-high, which has no showdown value. However, Minni checked the turn after leading the flop, which often indicates a marginal made hand (like Ace-high or a small pair) or a complete surrender with a draw. Since I am out of position and Minni's stack is relatively short (around 50 BBs), a bluff here is risky as she may feel committed to calling with any pair. I will check and hope to see a cheap showdown or fold if she bets, as I cannot win this hand without improving.

I opened wide, c‑bet the dry 2h‑5c‑3d flop and was called. The turn (3h) paired the board and I checked to control the pot; Flash checked back, which reduces the probability of him holding trips or a value full house line. The river Jh gives some hands a pair (Jx) and brings a three‑heart board, but Flash again checked. With only Q7 I am behind most of his flop‑call range (small pairs, A‑x, some Jx), so checking to showdown is a losing line. A small polarized river stab (~1/3 pot = 300) pressures missed A/K/X hands and some small pairs to fold; the break‑even fold frequency for this size is only 25% (300/(900+300)), so this bluff is +EV. I also hold Qh which blocks some strong heart/J combinations. Plan: make the small river bet to fold out enough of his checking range; if called I will muck; if he raises I’ll fold. Bet 300.

I started the hand by defending my big blind with a suited connector, 76d. On a low board (2-5-3), I had a gutshot straight draw and two overcards. I called a continuation bet on the flop to see if I could improve. The turn paired the board (3) and went check-check, suggesting Minni might have a marginal made hand or was giving up on a bluff. The river (J) didn't help me, and I still have 7-high. Minni's small river bet of 300 into 900 (1/3 pot) represents thin value, likely a small pair or an Ace-high she thinks is good. Since I have zero showdown value and a bluff against a short-stacked opponent who is likely to call with any pair is high-risk, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have Ace-King offsuit, which is a premium starting hand. Given that Minni is the only opponent remaining and is relatively short-stacked compared to me, I want to build a pot immediately. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard open that puts pressure on her and allows me to play for value with a significant range advantage.

Heads-up vs a button open to 300. Pot is 400 and I need to call 200 (pot odds ~2:1). Kc4h is a marginal hand but in the big blind vs a wide button opening range it’s worth defending. A 3-bet to 500 would be a weak bluff out of position and likely to get called or re-raised by this opponent’s wide/aggressive tendencies. Folding is too tight given the pot odds. I’ll call to see a flop and proceed cautiously — check to control the pot and fold to heavy aggression unless I pair my king or pick up strong draws/backdoors.

Flop is 4c 2s 8s and I hold Kc4h — I’ve flopped middle pair (4s) with a decent K kicker but the board has two spades and many overcards in the opener’s wide range. Pot is 600 and I’m out of position versus a deep, aggressive opponent. I prefer pot control here: checking keeps worse hands and draws paying to see a turn, lets me call a standard c-bet and re-evaluate, and avoids bloating the pot with a marginal made hand. Plan: check now; call most small-to-medium continuation bets to see the turn, fold to very large/ polarizing bets unless I improve, and extract value later if checked to.

I have Ace-King high on a low board of 4-2-8 with two spades. As the pre-flop aggressor, I have a range advantage. A standard continuation bet of 300 (half pot) is appropriate here to maintain initiative, deny equity to random cards, and get value from smaller draws or weaker high-card hands that Minni might have defended with in the Big Blind.

Call 300. I have middle pair (4s) with a good K kicker on a two-spade flop versus a wide, aggressive button. Flash’s 300 c-bet is a small-to-medium size and gives me ~25% pot odds to see a turn — calling preserves pot control and lets me realize equity against bluffs and weaker pairs. Raising OOP with this marginal made hand is unnecessarily bloating the pot and folding is too nitty given the odds and villain’s frequency of continuation bets. Plan: call now, re-evaluate on the turn — if the turn is a blank and villain checks I’ll look to extract value; if villain barrels big or polarizes I’ll fold unless I improve to top/two pair or pick up a clear read to continue.

Heads-up on a checked turn. I have K♣4♥ — middle pair (pair of 4s) with a decent kicker but no spade. The turn (T♠) completed a three‑spade board (2♠,8♠,T♠), so a lot of villain’s flop‑calling/cbetting range now contains completed flushes and hands that beat my pair (any spade, top pair T, sets). Flash checked to me after c‑betting the flop, which can be either a missed draw/air or a pot‑control/induce line with a strong hand. With a marginal made hand and only ~5.4k behind into a 1.2k pot, I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP or face a check‑raise from a flush — that’s a clear exploitative line to avoid. Checking keeps the pot small, realizes my equity, and lets me make a disciplined river decision based on river texture and sizing. Plan: check and evaluate the river — call small/medium river bets from obvious bluffs or worse one‑pair hands, fold to large polarizing river bets, and consider a small thin value bet only if the river is clean and villain shows weakness.

I have Ace-King offsuit with the King of Spades, giving me the Nut Flush Draw on a board featuring three spades (8s 2s Ts). I also have two overcards (Ace and King) which could provide the best hand if I hit them on the river. Minni has checked to me on the turn. While I could continue betting as a semi-bluff, checking behind allows me to realize my high equity for free and potentially see a spade, Ace, or King on the river without risking a check-raise from a short-stacked opponent who might already have a flush or a made hand. Checking also keeps the pot size controlled while I still have decent showdown value.

I have K♣4♥ on 4♣ 2♠ 8♠ T♠ K♥ — that’s two pair (Kings & Fours). Villain opened wide, c‑bet the flop (small) and then checked turn and checked the river, which polarizes their line toward missed draws, single pairs and some air rather than a high frequency of monsters. The hands that beat me are mainly sets (22,88,TT) and the less common two‑spade combos that made a flush; those are relatively rare in their wide range. With strong showdown value vs a wide checking range I should bet for value instead of simply checking and allowing bluffs to realize equity or giving a free showdown. I’ll make a modest value bet (~½ pot = 600) to extract calls from worse one‑pair hands, Kx, and many missed draws while keeping the pot controlled OOP. If villain raises large on the river I will fold to represent I’m likely up against a flush/set; I’ll call small/medium river raises only if sizing and reads justify it.

On the river, the King of Hearts (Kh) gives me top pair with an Ace kicker (Ah Ks). The board completed with three spades (2s 8s Ts), making a flush a distinct possibility for my opponent, Minni. After I checked the turn to control the pot and realize my equity, Minni has led out for 600 into a 1200 pot. Given the 4:1 pot odds, my top pair is too strong to fold, especially since my turn check might have induced a lead from a wider range of hands, including weaker Kings or bluffs. However, raising is unwise as it likely only gets called by better hands (flushes or two-pair). Therefore, calling is the most profitable decision to catch potential bluffs while limiting losses against a made flush.

Heads-up on the button with A♦T♦ — a strong, very playable hand in position. I want to seize initiative, build the pot and isolate the big blind, so I’ll open-raise rather than just call the extra 50. A raise to 300 (3BB) is a standard size here: it gives fold equity against Flash’s wide defending range, keeps postflop playability with 71 BB effective, and is consistent with table tendencies. Plan: if called, play aggressively in position (c-bet favorable boards, extract value vs worse A/x and T/x); if met with a sizeable 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate (call smaller 3-bets, fold to very large 4-bets).

I am in the Big Blind holding 8h 5h. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. I need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 600, giving me 2:1 odds. 85 suited is a reasonable hand to defend in a heads-up situation, especially since I have a massive chip lead and want to maintain pressure while seeing flops with speculative hands.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h 5h on a Kc 3d 2d flop. I checked to Minni, the pre-flop raiser. The board is quite dry but contains two diamonds. My hand has no direct connection to this board other than a backdoor heart draw. However, since the current action is checked to me (or rather, I am first to act and checking is standard here to see how the aggressor reacts), I will check to evaluate Minni's continuation bet.

Flop: Kc 3d 2d; I hold AdTd — Ace-high plus a nut diamond draw (4-to-a-flush) and a strong semi-bluff in position. As the preflop aggressor I should take the initiative: this board textures favors betting (fold equity vs Kx, Qx and many unpaired hands) and I also have ~35% to make my flush by the river plus additional outs to pair the Ace/T. A half-pot c-bet keeps the pot manageable while extracting value and denying correct odds to many draws, so I’ll bet ~50% pot. Plan: if called, I’ll barrel improving cards (diamonds/A) for value and check/control on blanks; if I face a sizable check-raise I’ll give credit to a very strong holding and re-evaluate (fold to extreme aggression, call smaller sizing to realize equity).

I am in the Big Blind with 8h 5h. The flop is Kc 3d 2d, providing me with no pair and no real draw. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, has followed up with a 300 continuation bet into a 600 pot. While I am getting 3:1 pot odds, my hand has very little equity against a range that includes Kings, pocket pairs, or diamond draws. Continuing with 8-high on this board is not profitable, so I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with King of Spades, Ten of Spades (Ks Ts), which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. My opponent, Minni, has a stack of 7400 chips (74 BB). I have a significant chip lead. Standard strategy from the small blind is to raise with a wide range of hands, and K-Ts is well within the top tier of that range. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and take the initiative in the hand.

Heads-up to a 300 open from the button (SB). I have K♦6♣ in the big blind with a 7,400 stack (≈74bb). The opener is likely wide from the button; K6o has some showdown value and decent equity against a wide range, and the price is reasonable: calling 200 more (300 total committed) puts 600 in the pot, so I need ~33% equity to break even. I don't want to 3-bet out of position with this marginal hand — calling to see a flop and proceeding cautiously is the best EV line. Plan: call now, fold to excessive aggression on dry boards, but play back/raise if I improve (pair/top pair or better) or pick up strong draws.

Check. The flop A♠ 9♠ J♣ is very unfavorable for K♦6♣: I have no pair, no spade, and essentially no meaningful draw. Flash opened wide from the button preflop, so his range contains lots of A‑x, Jx, 9x, and spade combos that are ahead or have strong equity. My preflop plan was to call and then fold to aggression on dry boards — this is exactly that spot: pot control is best. By checking I avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand and keep the option to fold to a continuation bet. If Flash checks back I can realize equity and potentially apply pressure later; if he bets a standard c‑bet (especially > ~1/3 pot) I will fold because I have poor equity and little showdown value. I may consider calling only a very tiny probe to see a turn, but overall checking and folding to meaningful aggression is the +EV line here.

I have King-Ten of spades on an Ace-high board with a flush draw (spades) and a gutter-ball straight draw (Queen). Minni checked. This board hits both of our ranges, but being the pre-flop aggressor and having significant equity with the flush draw and overcard/straight possibilities, I will place a continuation bet. A half-pot bet of 300 is standard here to apply pressure and build the pot if I hit my draws.

Flop A♠9♠J♣ with K♦6♣ — I have no pair and no spade, so very little showdown value, but Flash opened wide preflop and is c-betting a small 300 into 900 (~1/3 pot). Calling 300 gives me ~25% break-even equity (300/(900+300)=25%), and the price to see a turn is attractive. I won’t bluff‑raise out of position with this ragged hand; instead I’ll call to realize equity and keep the pot small. Plan after the call: fold to meaningful turn aggression if I don’t improve; if I pair the King or pick up a strong draw I’ll start applying pressure.

Board A♠ 9♠ J♣ 5♣ — I hold K♦6♣ with no pair, no spade, and only 6 clean river outs (3 Ks + 3 sixes ≈13%). I called the small 300 flop c‑bet to realize equity cheaply, but I didn’t improve on the turn. Betting OOP with this rag is a losing line: I have poor fold equity versus a wide but often-Ax/Jx/9x range and risk a check‑raise or getting called by better one‑pair hands. Pot control is the correct play — check and fold to meaningful aggression; if Flash checks back I can show down K‑high or take a stab on a safe river card or when I actually pair up.

On the turn, the 5 of clubs is a relatively blank card. I currently hold King-high with a strong flush draw and a gutshot straight draw (12 total outs to improve to a very strong hand). Minni has checked to me after calling my flop continuation bet. Since the Ace on the board favors my pre-flop raising range, I will continue to tell the story that I have a strong hand (like an Ace) by betting. A bet of 800 into the 1200 pot (roughly 66%) applies pressure to her marginal hands like Jx or 9x and builds the pot for the instances where I hit my draws on the river.

I checked the turn with K♦6♣ and Flash responded by betting 800 into the 2000 pot. Calling 800 gives me ~28.6% break‑even equity (800/(2000+800)), while I only have ~6 clean outs (~13% to pair on the river). Some of those outs are also likely blocked by Flash's range (A‑K, Kx), and the opponent's line (wide open pre, small flop c‑bet then a turn barrel) is consistent with many A‑x/J‑x/9‑x value combos plus some bluffs. Raising OOP as a bluff here is too thin and risky; calling is a clear -EV. Preserve the stack for better spots and fold.

Heads-up on the button with 7♦6♦ — a suited connector with strong postflop playability. In heads-up play I should be opening very wide; raising to the minimum allowed (to 200 total) gives me initiative, fold equity, and keeps the pot manageable with ~68.5 BB effective. If Flash calls I keep position and plenty of implied odds to realizemy equity (backdoor diamond + straight possibilities); if he 3-bets heavily I can fold or reevaluate depending on size. Raise to 200 is the standard, low-variance aggressive line here.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Nine offsuit. Minni, on the Button/Small Blind, has made a minimum raise to 200. I need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 3:1 odds. K9o is well above the average hand in a heads-up situation and is easily worth a call to see a flop, especially since Minni is playing with a relatively short stack compared to mine.

I'm in the Big Blind with King-high on an Ace-high flop. The pre-flop raiser (Minni) is likely to have an Ace or will represent one with a continuation bet. My hand has very little equity on this dry board. I will check to the aggressor and likely fold to any significant bet.

I raised preflop to take initiative; on the flop (8♦ A♣ 2♥) I have 7♦6♦ — no pair, only backdoor diamond and runner-runner straight potential. Heads‑up as the preflop aggressor I should continuation‑bet a good portion of my range to extract fold equity from Kx/Qx and other non‑Ace hands that the BB might check with. I’ll make a half‑pot c‑bet (200) — large enough to apply pressure but small enough to preserve stack if called. Plan: fold to a significant check‑raise (I have very little made equity), call small single raises only in specific sizing reads, and if called proceed cautiously on the turn — continue barreling only if I pick up meaningful equity (a diamond or a very favorable low card).

I am in the Big Blind with King-high. The flop comes Ace-high and very dry (8d-Ac-2h). Minni, who was the pre-flop aggressor, has followed up with a continuation bet of 200 into a 400 pot. This board texture heavily favors her perceived range. My hand has very little equity and no potential draws to improve. Given the board state and the action, folding is the most disciplined and correct move.

In this heads-up situation from the Button, 5-6 offsuit is a speculative hand but worth playing to maintain pressure, especially since I have a significant stack lead over Minni. Raising to 300 has been a standard and effective opening for me in previous hands, allowing me to take control of the pot early or win it pre-flop.

I have KcKd in the big blind facing a standard button open to 300 heads-up. KK is a premium hand (only behind AA) and is a clear 3-bet preflop for value and to seize initiative. I don't want to just call and play out-of-position with a near-best hand; a 3-bet isolates the opener, gets value from worse aces and medium pairs, and generates fold equity against wide button opening ranges. I choose a raise to 1,200 total (~4x the open) — large enough to extract value and force difficult decisions while preserving most of my ~70bb stack (I’ve posted 100, so this is +1,100 from me). If Flash 4-bets very large I’ll re-evaluate then, but right now 3-betting to 1,200 is the best balance of value and protection.

I am on the Button/SB with 5c 6h. I opened for 300 and Minni 3-bet to 1200. While I have a massive stack lead, 5-6 offsuit is a very weak hand to continue with against a 4x 3-bet from a relatively short-stacked opponent. Minni has shown strength with these 3-bets recently (Hand #129), and calling here with such low equity out of position or even in position with a low SPR would be a mistake. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with T♠6♠ — suited, playable hand with good post-flop potential. In heads-up I should open a wide range rather than limp; calling 50 (completing to 100) is too passive. I’ll raise to take the initiative, gain fold equity, and keep SPR reasonable with deep stacks (~73bb). A standard 3xBB open (300 total) matches table norms and balances pressure vs the big blind. Plan: if Flash calls I’ll use positional advantage — c-bet dry flops, check/control on highly coordinated or Ace-high boards; fold or fold-heavy to large 3-bets while calling smaller 3-bets only with appropriate backdoor equity.

Nine-four offsuit is a very weak hand to defend from the big blind against a 3x open, even with the pot odds offered. Given my significant stack lead and the poor playability of this hand out of position, folding is the most prudent move.

I have Jack-Ten offsuit on the button. This is a strong starting hand in a heads-up situation. I will raise to 300, which is my standard opening size, to take control of the hand and put pressure on the big blind.

Heads-up vs a button open to 300, I’m in the big blind with K♦J♥. This is a strong defending hand heads-up but not so strong that I want to inflate the pot out-of-position with a 3-bet. I’ve already posted 100 and need 200 more to see a flop; calling keeps the pot manageable (good pot odds) and lets me realize equity and play post-flop with top-pair/backdoor possibilities. I’ll call the 300 total and proceed based on the flop texture — raise or pot-control as appropriate.

Flop: A♥ J♦ T♣ — I have J♥ K♦: second pair with a very healthy kicker, but I’m out of position and there’s an Ace on board that beats me. Button opened a very wide heads‑up range preflop, so many of his hands are worse than mine (T, smaller Jx, some Kx, QJ, QTs, pocket pairs) and he will continue with lots of bluffs and single‑pair hands. I also block some of the KQ Broadway combos (I hold the K), so the nut‑straight combos are thinner than they look.

Plan: I want value and to deny free cards to hands that can improve (mainly KQ, QK). Checking OOP hands the initiative to him and lets him float/bluff; betting extracts value from tens and worse Jx and protects my hand. A half‑pot bet (~50%) is the right balance here — it gets called by worse and makes big bluffs and many Ax hands pay to raise. If he folds I take the pot. If he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn (reevaluate if an Ace/Queen/King or scary pairing/runner appears). If he raises large on the flop, I’ll give credit (likely an Ace or rare made straight) and fold; if he makes a small/medium raise (probable bluff or thin value), I’ll consider calling to see a turn and re‑evaluate.

I have flopped bottom two pair (Jacks and Tens) on a very coordinated board (A-J-T). Minni has led into me with a donk bet of 300. This could represent a weak Ace, a straight draw like KQ, Q9, or K9, or even a semi-bluff. With such a draw-heavy board, I need to raise for value and protection. Raising to 900 charges her draws and extracts value from her one-pair hands while I still likely hold the best hand.

Heads‑up on A♥ J♦ T♣ with K♦ J♥ — I have second pair with a very strong kicker but an Ace on board that beats me. I bet a half‑pot (300) to get value and protect; Flash’s raise to 900 is polarizing (value: A‑x, sets, JT, KQ straight; bluffs/semi‑bluffs possible given his aggressive history). Calling 600 more (total 900) is the lowest variance, +good pot odds (~5:1) to see a turn and realize equity against a wide raising range. Raising OOP to 1500 is too committal with a vulnerable second pair (I’d be bloating the pot against many hands that currently beat me). Plan: call to keep the pot under control, re‑evaluate on the turn — fold to heavy turn aggression that strongly represents an Ace/straight/set, but continue to extract value or probe if the turn is a favorable or blank card and he checks/calls.

Turn: A♥ J♦ T♣ Q♥. With K♦ J♥ in my hand I’ve just made the Broadway straight (A‑K‑Q‑J‑T). This is essentially the nuts on this board — the only realistic way to beat me is that villain also holds a King (resulting in a chop) or an extremely unlikely straight‑flush/full house line. Flash’s flop raise polarized his range (Ax, sets, Kx, strong broadway combos and bluffs), so many of his continuing hands are exactly the ones I want to extract from now. 

Plan: bet for value and to deny cheap river cards to any two‑card heart combos or hands that can turn into full houses. I’ll size to ~2/3 pot to charge A‑x, K‑x, Qx, Tx and worse Jx hands — large enough to get value but not so large I fold out lots of calls. If he raises big I’ll strongly consider calling/flatting given my straight (only very rare river cards could turn me into a trailing hand), and if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river (be cautious if the river pairs and he barrels again, since full houses become possible).

On the turn, the Queen of Hearts is a highly problematic card. It completes several straights, most notably any King (Broadway straight) or any Nine (King-high straight). While I have bottom two pair (Jacks and Tens), this hand has significantly diminished in value relative to the board's texture. Minni's decision to 'donk lead' on the turn after I raised her on the flop is a show of immense strength, especially given the coordination of the board. Given her stack size, this 1,600 bet is essentially committing her to the pot. I am likely behind better two pairs like AQ, AJ, or a straight. Since I have only four outs to improve to a full house (and even then, QJ or QT could have me beat if she has a set of Queens), the correct professional play is to fold and preserve my significant chip lead.

Heads-up on the button with K♣3♠ — not a premium hand but very playable in position. Against a single big blind I should open a wide range to steal and dictate the flop; a standard 2xBB raise (200 total) is the right sizing: it applies pressure, avoids giving a cheap flop to the BB, and keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks (8650 effective). If Flash 3-bets large I can fold K3o; if he calls I’ll use positional advantage to c-bet or probe the flop and realize equity. Raise to 200 to take the initiative.

I have a pocket pair (77), which is a very strong hand in a heads-up situation. Minni opened with a minimum raise from the button/small blind. I will 3-bet to 700 to take initiative and build the pot, as 77 has significant equity against a wide opening range.

Flash 3-bet to 700 after my 200 open. Pot is 900 and I must call 500 to continue — that requires about 35.7% equity (500 / (900+500)). I hold Kc3s, which is frequently dominated by a standard BB 3‑bet range that contains many Ax and Kx hands and strong broadways. Calling here is a marginal, likely negative-EV play and 4‑betting is worse: K3 has poor blockers and is a bad 4‑bet bluff candidate against a deep-stacked, aggressive player. Preserve my 8.5k stack and wait for a better spot late in the session. Fold.

I am on the button (small blind) with King-Deuce of spades, which is a strong starting hand in a heads-up or short-handed situation. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and apply pressure on the big blind.

Heads-up against the button with T♠ J♠ — a very strong heads-up hand (suited broadway). Flash opens to 300 frequently and shows a fairly wide/aggressive opening range from the button. I want to take the initiative and get value/fold equity rather than playing a marginal pot out of position. A small min-re-raise (to 500) surrenders initiative and invites calls; a moderate 3-bet to ~1,000 total (≈3.3x the open) pressures his wide filings, folds out weaker hands, and builds the pot when I have a clear equity edge vs his calling range. If he folds I scoop the pot; if he calls I still have good playability postflop and can continue to apply pressure or pot-control depending on the board. If he 4-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (TJs is sometimes foldable vs a large polarized 4-bet but can proceed vs light 4-bets). Action: 3-bet to 1,000 total.

In a heads-up situation with only a few hands remaining, King-Deuce suited is a very playable hand. I raised initially, and Minni 3-bet to 1000. Given the pot odds (700 to call for a 2000 total pot) and my position on the button, I have enough equity and post-flop playability to see a flop. 4-betting here would be risky against a 75bb stack, so a call is the most prudent way to proceed while maintaining control.

I have T♠J♠ — top pair, decent kicker on J♦6♣5♣. I 3-bet preflop so I still have initiative and my range should be relatively strong vs Flash's call-range, but the board has two clubs so there are club draws and some two-card backdoors in his calling range. I want to charge draws and extract value from worse jacks / QJ / KJ and medium pairs rather than giving free cards, so a standard ~50% pot c-bet (~1000 into 2000) is appropriate: big enough to charge/price out some equity while keeping worse hands and draws in. Plan: if called proceed cautiously on turn and continue to apply pressure on non-club/blank turns; if he raises a large, polarized sizing I will re-evaluate (likely fold to an extreme polarizing shove, call smaller/medium raises to realize equity).

On a board of Jack-Six-Five, my King-high has very little equity against a range that 3-bet pre-flop and is now leading for half-pot. Minni's stack is small enough that she is likely committed to this hand if she has any piece of the board or an overpair. Given that there are only 5 hands remaining and I have a significant stack, I will fold and avoid a high-variance confrontation with a marginal hand.

Heads-up on the button I can open a very wide range. J3o is marginal but perfectly openable in heads-up — I want to seize the initiative and apply pressure to the big blind rather than just seeing a flop for a small price. A min-raise to 200 buys fold equity and keeps the pot manageable; if Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold (this hand plays poorly as a 3-bet call or vs strong aggression), and if called I can play postflop in position, c-betting favorable textures and checking back when clearly behind. Raise to 200 to steal or play the pot in position.

I am in the Big Blind holding Kd 2d. Minni has raised to 200 from the Small Blind, leaving me with 100 to call in a pot of 300. This provides me with 3:1 pot odds. My hand is suited and contains a King, which plays reasonably well heads-up, especially against Minni's likely wide opening range. With only a few hands left in the session and a significant stack advantage (30,400 vs 9,300), calling to see a flop is a standard and profitable play.

In the Big Blind, I called a Small Blind open with King-Deuce suited. The flop of Qc 5d 6s is relatively dry. I have one overcard (King) and a backdoor diamond flush draw, but I do not have a made hand. Following standard poker strategy in a heads-up pot where I am out of position, I will check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they respond. Given my massive stack lead and the few hands remaining in the session, there is no reason to lead out with a bluff or play aggressively with only King-high at this stage.

Heads-up on the button I opened a very wide range, so continuation-betting a dry Q-5-6 board is standard. My hand (J♣3♠) has almost no showdown value and no realistic backdoor flush/straight draw, so its value is in fold equity and initiative. Flash checked, which often indicates weakness or a float/calling range — both of which I can pressure. I’ll make a standard-sized c-bet (~60% pot) to fold out missed overcards and small pairs while getting called by worse hands occasionally. Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate the turn (check back most low, unhelpful turns unless I pair or face very weak action); if he check-raises large I will concede and fold because J3 has poor equity against a strong line.

On a flop of Qc-5d-6s, I have King-high with a backdoor flush draw. Minni's bet of 250 into the 400 pot is a standard continuation bet from the small blind. Given the pot odds (roughly 2.6:1) and the fact that I'm heads-up, King-high has some equity against a wide range of bluffs or semi-bluffs. With a massive stack lead and only a few hands left in the session, calling here to see the turn is a low-risk play. If the turn improves my hand (a King or a Diamond) I can continue; otherwise, I can comfortably fold to a second barrel.

I am out of position with King-high and no real draw on a Queen-high board that now has a Ten. After calling a bet on the flop to see if I could improve or if Minni would slow down, I will check the turn. My hand has very little equity, and I have no reason to lead out and bloat the pot given my massive chip lead and the fact that there are only a few hands remaining in the session. I will check and likely fold to any significant bet from Minni.

I opened a very wide heads‑up range and c‑bet the dry Q♣‑5♦‑6♠ flop as a bluff; J♣3♠ has almost no showdown value and only a one‑card backdoor club. Flash called my flop stab, which usually represents some showdown value or a decent draw. The T♥ on the turn doesn’t help my hand — it gives my caller additional two‑card combinations (ten‑hands, some straight/paired combos) and removes the thin equity my turn barrel would have. With a healthy stack and an opponent who is willing to call flop, a second barrel here is thin and can be exploited by check‑raises or hands that now legitimately beat me. I’ll check to control the pot, avoid being priced out or raised off, and re‑evaluate on the river — calling small river bets as a bluff‑catcher but folding to large aggression since I have no pair and very limited equity.

On the river, the board is Qc-5d-6s-Th-8h. I have King-high. Minni checked the turn after betting the flop, which often indicates a hand that wants to see a cheap showdown or has given up on a bluff. While King-high is weak, it has some showdown value against complete air or missed lower straight draws. Given my significant stack lead and the few hands remaining in the session, there is no reason to turn my hand into a bluff here. Checking is the safest play and allows me to reach showdown for free.

I’ll check. I have J♣3♠ on Q♣–5♦–6♠–T♥–8♥ — no pair and almost zero showdown value. Flash called my flop c‑bet (shows some showdown value: pairs, Qx, T‑x or a sticky marginal hand) then checked the turn and river, which lowers my folding equity on the river. A bluff here would need a small bet (e.g. 300) to break even only if he folds ~25%+ of the time; larger sizes need even higher fold rates. From the table history Flash is a relatively sticky caller and has shown a tendency to call down with marginal hands, so I can’t count on enough folds to make a profitable river stab. Pot is only 900 and my stack is healthy (9,050) — preserving chips and avoiding a thin, likely losing bluff is the correct long‑term play. Checking keeps the pot small and avoids getting raised off a non‑hand; if he shows weakness I'll pick spots later.

I am holding 82o on the button (SB) in a heads-up situation against Minni. This is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Minni has shown significant aggression recently, frequently 3-betting my pre-flop raises or defending the big blind. Given the hand strength and the opponent's recent profile, there is no reason to invest any chips in this pot. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity in the final few hands.

Heads-up on the button with A♣2♥ is a standard open — the hand is strong enough to raise for value and to steal the blind. Flash in the BB plays wide, so I want initiative and to deny cheap odds to small pairs and connectors. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x the BB): big enough to apply pressure and build a pot with position, but small enough to preserve stack vs a 3-bet. Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I’ll C-bet on many dry/ace-high flops and play cautiously on highly coordinated boards. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll fold this weak-ace holding against a polarized line.

I am in the Big Blind with 6s 4s, which is a playable suited hand. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. I need to call 200 more to see a flop. Given the pot odds (3:1) and the fact that we are nearing the end of the game with me holding a significant chip lead, calling to see a flop is a standard and low-risk play.

On this J-T-3 rainbow board, I have six-high with no direct draw. Minni is the pre-flop aggressor. Since I have checked, I will wait to see how she acts. Currently, I have no equity to justify a bet or a check-raise, but checking is the standard move to see a free turn or respond to a bet.

Heads‑up I was the preflop raiser with A♣2♥ so I still have initiative. The flop Js Tc 3c gives me only ace‑high, but my Ac is a useful blocker to the nut club combos and I can fold out a lot of Missed hands and small pairs with pressure. This is a standard continuation‑bet spot — I’ll use a half‑pot sizing to get folds and protect my range while keeping the pot manageable against a deep stack. If Flash folds I take it; if he calls I will proceed cautiously on the turn (check unless I pair or hit a meaningful club); if he raises big I’ll fold A2o to a polarized raise.

On this Js-Tc-3c flop, I have six-high with no direct draw and only very thin backdoor potential. Minni, as the pre-flop aggressor, has continued with a half-pot bet. Given the board texture strongly favors a typical opening range (containing hands like KJ, QJ, AJ, or even sets and straight draws like KQ), my 6s4s has very little equity. Since I am in the final three hands of the match with a substantial chip lead, there is no strategic reason to make a speculative call or attempt a low-probability bluff. I will fold and wait for better spots in the remaining two hands.

I am on the button with Ace-Seven offsuit. In a heads-up or short-handed situation, Ace-high is a strong hand that warrants a raise to take control of the pot and apply pressure on the Big Blind. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate given the stack depths.

Heads-up vs the button (Flash) who opened to 300. I have A♣K♣ — the top non-pair hand and a clear 3-bet candidate. Calling 200 lets a very wide SB range see a cheap flop and preserves their positional advantage; folding is absurd. I want to extract value from worse broadways and pocket pairs and get fold equity vs weaker A/Kx and speculative hands. I’ll 3-bet to a size that pressures marginal holdings but leaves plenty of stack behind to play postflop (I keep ~8k effective). Raising to 1200 (≈4x the open) is a standard, exploitative size here — big enough to deny equity to many hands, small enough to still realize value when called. If Flash folds I scoop the pot; if called I’ll C-bet appropriate textures and proceed carefully OOP. If he 4-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (often folding to a very large 4-bet, calling or shoving vs smaller/targeted 4-bets).

I am in the Small Blind/Button with Ace-Seven offsuit. Minni has 3-bet me to 1200. Minni's stack is quite short (8200 total), making the effective stack about 82 big blinds. Given Minni's recent aggressive pre-flop patterns (as seen in Hand #141, #145), she can be quite wide. However, A7o is a marginal hand to continue with against a 4x 3-bet, especially when facing a player who has shown strength in these spots. If I call, I'll be playing a bloated pot out of position or with a hand that is often dominated. If I 4-bet, I'm essentially committing to a stack-off with a marginal hand. Folding is the most disciplined play here to preserve my significant lead in the final hands of the match.

Heads-up on the button (I posted the 50 SB) I should be opening a very wide range. J♣5♥ is marginal but perfectly playable from the button because I have position and can apply pressure. Raising gains fold equity, avoids playing a cold-call pot out of position, and lets me take the initiative postflop. I’ll make a standard 3xBB open to 300 (total committed) — big enough to discourage some light defenses but small enough to keep things manageable. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold this hand; if called I’ll use position to C-bet or control the pot based on the flop texture.

I have King-Four suited (Kd 4d) in the Big Blind. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind/Button. Given that this is the final hand of the game and I have a significant stack lead, calling here to see a flop is a standard play. K4s has reasonable playability and I am getting 2:1 odds to call. I will call and see the flop.

On this flop of Qd Jd 3h, I have the nut flush draw with the King of diamonds. My opponent, Minni, has checked to me. While betting as a semi-bluff is a valid strategy, checking back allows me to realize my equity for free and see a turn card. Since this is the final hand of the game and I have a substantial stack lead, I prefer the lower-variance play of checking and seeing how the turn develops.

Heads-up on the button with J♣5♥ — I have top pair but a very weak kicker on J♦Q♦3♥. Flash checked after calling my preflop open, so his range contains lots of broadways, pocket pairs and diamond draws. I want to extract value from worse jacks and charge/price out diamond draws and overcards, so I’ll make a sizing that penalizes draws but still gets called by worse. Bet ~2/3 pot (400 into 600) — if he folds I take it down, if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if he check-raises big I’ll give up this marginal kicker.

On this flop of Qd Jd 3h, I have a King-high flush draw (Kd 4d). My opponent, Minni, has bet 400 into a pot of 600. This gives me pot odds of 3.5:1 to call, which is a good price to try and hit my flush, as I have approximately a 35% chance to do so by the river. Given that this is the final hand of the game and I have a significant stack lead (30,000 to 9,000), calling is a safe and high-equity play that allows me to realize my draw without risking my entire stack unnecessarily.

On the turn, the 4h gives me a bottom pair of fours to go along with my King-high flush draw. Being first to act on this street, I will check. This allows me to see how Minni reacts. If Minni checks behind, I get a free look at the river to complete my flush. If Minni bets, I have more than enough equity with the flush draw and a pair to call. Given that this is the final hand and I have a significant stack lead, minimizing risk while maintaining equity is the most prudent strategy.

I opened wide from the button and flopped top pair (J♣5♥ on J♦ Q♦ 3♥). My c-bet (400) was called, so Flash’s calling range contains worse jacks, Qx, some small pairs, and diamond combinations/draws. The turn (4♥) is a relatively blank card — it doesn’t create many new made hands that dominate me, but it does leave diamond/runner-runner and two-heart-in-hand combos that can make a strong river. With a marginal top pair and position, I want to extract value from worse Jx and charge/removal for draws while keeping the bet small enough to fold to a big check-raise. Pot is 1400, so a ~50% pot turn bet (700) is the right balance: it gets value from worse, folds out pure floats/missed draws sometimes, and preserves maneuverability for the river. If he folds I take it down; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate river action; if he check-raises large I’ll let go of this weak kicker.

On the turn, the Four of Hearts gives me a bottom pair to go with my King-high flush draw. Minni has bet 700 into a pot of 1400, offering me 3:1 pot odds. With a flush draw (8 outs) and a pair of fours (potentially 2 outs for trips or 3 outs for a King), I have roughly a 25% chance to improve to a very strong hand, and my current pair might even be ahead of some bluffs or straight draws. Given that this is the final hand and I have a massive chip lead, calling is the most mathematically sound and low-risk play to realize my equity and potentially win a larger pot if the river is favorable.

On the river, I have improved to two pair (Kings and Fours) with the King of Clubs landing. The board is Jack-Queen-Trey-Four-King. While my two pair is strong, there are several two-pair combinations (KQ, KJ, QJ) and sets (QQ, JJ, 33) that beat me, and Minni has shown aggression on both the flop and turn. Since this is the final hand of the game and I have a massive stack lead, my primary goal is to conclude the hand safely. Checking first allows me to see Minni's reaction. If she checks behind, I likely win the pot with my two pair. If she bets, I can evaluate the sizing, but I will most likely call to reach the showdown, as I have a strong hand and plenty of chips to spare.

I check. River K♣ completes some hands that beat me (Kx, KQ combos) and Flash’s line — calling my 400 flop c-bet and 700 turn bet — looks like a fairly stubborn showdown range (Qx, Jx, medium pairs, missed diamond/heart draws). My J♣5♥ is top pair but a very weak kicker; a value bet here will be called by many hands that actually beat me (Qx/Kx) and will often fold out the only hands I can get value from (worse Jx). Betting also exposes me to a check-raise where I have to fold. With the pot at 2,800 and plenty of dead money already in, pot control and taking this to showdown is the highest EV line: if Flash checks back I go to showdown; if he check-raises as a bluff I can fold knowing my kicker is poor and I’m unlikely ahead.
